topics.title: Fluid Statics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT403 - FLUID FLOW AND PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fluid Statics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Fluid Statics

**Fluid Statics (Hydrostatics)** is the branch of fluid mechanics that studies fluids at rest — that is, fluids in equilibrium where the velocity is zero everywhere and only pressure and gravitational (body) forces act on the fluid element. Since there is no motion, shear stresses vanish, and the analysis reduces to balancing pressure forces against body forces (typically gravity).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *Fluid statics deals with the study of fluids at rest. It includes the study of pressure, its measurement, manometry, buoyancy, and the forces exerted by static fluids on submerged and immersed surfaces.*

## 1.2 Key Physical Quantities

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Dimensional Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure | $P$ | $\text{Pa} = \text{N/m}^2$ | $\text{[M L}^{-1}\text{T}^{-2}\text{]}$ |
| Density | $\rho$ | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | $\text{[M L}^{-3}\text{]}$ |
| Specific Weight | $w = \rho g$ | $\text{N/m}^3$ | $\text{[M L}^{-2}\text{T}^{-2}\text{]}$ |
| Specific Gravity | $SG$ | dimensionless | — |
| Viscosity (static context) | $\mu$ | $\text{Pa·s}$ | $\text{[M L}^{-1}\text{T}^{-1}\text{]}$ |
| Depth | $h$ | $\text{m}$ | $\text{[L]}$ |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Stack of Books"**
> Imagine a stack of 10 identical books lying on a table. The bottom book bears the weight of all 9 above it, the next bears 8, and the topmost book bears nothing extra. Similarly, in a static fluid column, each horizontal layer of fluid supports the weight of all the fluid above it. The deeper you go, the greater the pressure — **pressure increases linearly with depth**.

Real-world examples engineers encounter daily:
- A **dam wall** being thicker at the base (because hydrostatic pressure rises with depth).
- A **submarine** crushing deeper as the ocean's weight of water above it grows.
- A **barometer** measuring atmospheric pressure from a column of mercury.
- A **hydraulic jack** in a workshop lifting cars using Pascal's principle.

## 1.4 Core Physical Constants (Bolded per KTU Standard)

- Standard gravity: $\mathbf{g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2}$
- Density of water at **4 °C**: $\mathbf{\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3}$
- Density of mercury at **20 °C**: $\mathbf{\rho_{Hg} \approx 13550 \text{ kg/m}^3}$
- Standard atmospheric pressure: $\mathbf{P_{atm} = 101325 \text{ Pa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar}}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Hydrostatic Equation (Pressure Variation with Depth)

Consider a small fluid element of cross-sectional area $dA$ and vertical thickness $dh$ at depth $h$ below a free liquid surface. Forces acting on the element in the vertical direction:

1. **Pressure force from below** (pushing up): $P \, dA$
2. **Pressure force from above** (pushing down): $(P + dP) \, dA$
3. **Weight of the element** (pulling down): $w \, dA \, dh = \rho g \, dA \, dh$

For static equilibrium (no acceleration in the vertical direction):

$$
P \, dA - (P + dP) \, dA - \rho g \, dA \, dh = 0
$$

Simplifying, we obtain the **fundamental hydrostatic differential equation**:

$$
\frac{dP}{dh} = \rho g
$$

Integrating between two points 1 and 2 (with heights $h_1$ and $h_2$ from a common datum):

$$
P_2 - P_1 = \rho g (h_1 - h_2) = \rho g \Delta h
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result:** In a static fluid, pressure depends **only on the vertical depth difference**, not on the shape of the container or the path between the two points. This is the **Pascal's Law / Stevin's Law** result.

## 2.2 Absolute vs Gauge vs Vacuum Pressure

- **Absolute Pressure:** Measured from a perfect vacuum (zero reference). $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$
- **Gauge Pressure:** Measured above local atmospheric pressure. $P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$
- **Vacuum Pressure:** When $P_{abs} < P_{atm}$, the deficit is called vacuum pressure. $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$

## 2.3 Manometry — Pressure Measurement Using Fluid Columns

A **manometer** uses the height difference of a static liquid column to infer pressure.

### 2.3.1 Simple U-Tube Manometer
$$
P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \, g \, h
$$

where $\rho_m$ = manometric fluid density, $\rho_f$ = fluid above the manometric fluid, $h$ = manometer deflection.

### 2.3.2 Inclined Manometer
$$
P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \, g \, L \, \sin\theta
$$
where $L$ = length along the inclined tube, $\theta$ = angle of inclination with horizontal. Used for measuring small pressure differences (higher sensitivity).

### 2.3.3 Inverted U-Tube (Differential) Manometer
For measuring pressure drop across a flow device using a lighter fluid (often air) in the manometer:

$$
P_A - P_B = h \left( \rho_f - \rho_m \right) g
$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | Open tank problems |
| 2 | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + \rho g h$ | Absolute pressure under open liquid surface | Reservoir, open vessel |
| 3 | $P_A - P_B = \rho g (z_B - z_A)$ | Pressure difference between two points in a static fluid (with elevation $z$) | Closed vessels |
| 4 | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ | Simple U-tube manometer reading | Pressure in pipes |
| 5 | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g L \sin\theta$ | Inclined manometer | Low pressure differences |
| 6 | $F_B = \rho_{fluid} \, g \, V_{displaced}$ | Buoyant force (Archimedes' Principle) | Floatation, settling |
| 7 | $F_R = \rho g h_c A$ | Resultant hydrostatic force on a flat submerged plate | Force on dams/gates |
| 8 | $y_{cp} - y_c = -\dfrac{I_{xc} \sin^2\theta}{\rho g h_c A}$ | Center of pressure offset (or use the parallel-axis form below) | Gate design |
| 9 | $y_{cp} = \dfrac{I_{xc}}{y_c A} + y_c$ | Center of pressure for a vertical plane surface (clean form, see derivation in Section 3) | Wall, gate |
| 10 | $W = \rho_{object} \, V \, g$ | Weight of submerged object | Floatation stability |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Hint:** Memorize formulas 1, 3, 4, 7, and 9 — these account for **~80%** of the marks in any Fluid Statics problem.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Dam engineering:** Computing total hydrostatic thrust and overturning moment on a dam face.
- **Chemical process vessels:** Determining wall thickness required to withstand static liquid head.
- **Storage tanks:** Designing flat bottoms and sloped roofs accounting for fluid pressure.
- **Hydraulic presses & lifts:** Leveraging Pascal's law to multiply force.
- **Barometers & altimeters:** Atmospheric pressure measurement.
- **Ship and submarine design:** Buoyancy calculations for stability and depth control.
- **Settling tanks (this course):** Using density difference for sedimentation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Hydrostatic Equation

**Step 1 — Free Body of a Fluid Element.**
Consider a small cylindrical fluid element of cross-sectional area $dA$ and height $dh$ immersed in a static liquid of density $\rho$. The element is at depth $h$ below the free surface.

**Step 2 — Identify the Forces.**
- Pressure force on the bottom face (upward): $F_{bot} = P \, dA$
- Pressure force on the top face (downward): $F_{top} = (P + dP) \, dA$
- Weight of the element (downward): $W = \rho g \, dA \, dh$

**Step 3 — Apply Static Equilibrium.**
Since the fluid is at rest, the net force is zero:

$$
P \, dA - (P + dP) \, dA - \rho g \, dA \, dh = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.**
The $P \, dA$ terms cancel:

$$
-dP \, dA - \rho g \, dA \, dh = 0
$$

Dividing throughout by $dA$:

$$
dP = -\rho g \, dh
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate.**
For an incompressible fluid ($\rho$ = constant), integrate from the free surface (where $P = P_0$ and $h = 0$) to a depth $h$ (where pressure is $P$):

$$
\int_{P_0}^{P} dP = -\rho g \int_{0}^{h} dh
$$

$$
P - P_0 = \rho g h
$$

$$
\boxed{P = P_0 + \rho g h}
$$

**Step 6 — Physical Interpretation.**
The pressure at any depth $h$ equals the surface pressure $P_0$ plus the weight per unit area of the liquid column above it. This is the foundation of all manometry.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Center of Pressure for a Vertical Flat Submerged Surface

**Step 1 — Set Up the Geometry.**
Consider a vertical flat plate of area $A$ submerged in a static fluid with its top edge at the free surface. The plate is divided into infinitesimal horizontal strips of width $b(y)$ and thickness $dy$ at depth $y$.

**Step 2 — Pressure on an Infinitesimal Strip.**
The pressure at depth $y$ is:

$$
P(y) = \rho g y
$$

The force on the strip is:

$$
dF = P(y) \, dA = \rho g y \, b(y) \, dy
$$

**Step 3 — Total Force (Integrate over the plate).**
The total hydrostatic force on the plate is:

$$
F_R = \int_A P \, dA = \rho g \int_A y \, dA
$$

Recognizing that the integral $\int_A y \, dA = y_c \, A$ (the first moment of area about the free surface, equal to the centroidal depth times area), we obtain:

$$
\boxed{F_R = \rho g \, y_c \, A}
$$

where $y_c$ is the vertical depth of the centroid of the submerged area.

**Step 4 — Center of Pressure (Depth where the resultant force effectively acts).**
The moment of the resultant force about the free surface must equal the sum of the moments of the distributed pressure forces:

$$
F_R \cdot y_{cp} = \int_A y \, (P \, dA) = \rho g \int_A y^2 \, dA
$$

Define the **second moment of area** (about the free surface axis) as $I_x = \int_A y^2 \, dA$.

**Step 5 — Apply the Parallel-Axis Theorem.**
The second moment of area about the centroidal axis is $I_{xc} = I_x - y_c^2 A$, so $I_x = I_{xc} + y_c^2 A$.

Substituting:

$$
F_R \cdot y_{cp} = \rho g \left( I_{xc} + y_c^2 A \right)
$$

Since $F_R = \rho g \, y_c \, A$:

$$
\rho g \, y_c A \cdot y_{cp} = \rho g \left( I_{xc} + y_c^2 A \right)
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $y_{cp}$.**

$$
y_{cp} = \frac{I_{xc} + y_c^2 A}{y_c A} = y_c + \frac{I_{xc}}{y_c A}
$$

$$
\boxed{y_{cp} = y_c + \frac{I_{xc}}{y_c A}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The center of pressure is always **below** the centroid of the area ($y_{cp} > y_c$) because the pressure is non-uniform — higher pressure at the bottom pulls the resultant force point downward.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Buoyancy (Archimedes' Principle)

**Step 1 — Consider a Submerged Body of Volume $V$.**
The pressure at the bottom face (depth $h + L$) exceeds the pressure at the top face (depth $h$) by $\rho g L$, where $L$ is the vertical height of the body.

**Step 2 — Net Vertical Force.**
The vertical components of the pressure forces acting on the body integrate to a net upward force equal to the weight of the displaced fluid. By the hydrostatic equation, integrating $\rho g$ over the volume of the body:

$$
F_B = \int_V \rho_{fluid} g \, dV = \rho_{fluid} \, g \, V_{displaced}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_B = \rho_{fluid} \, g \, V_{displaced}}
$$

**Step 3 — Floatation Condition.**
An object floats when buoyant force equals weight:

$$
\rho_{fluid} \, g \, V_{submerged} = \rho_{object} \, g \, V_{object}
$$

Fraction submerged:

$$
\frac{V_{submerged}}{V_{object}} = \frac{\rho_{object}}{\rho_{fluid}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Problem Archetypes

| Problem Archetype | Governing Equations | Key Trick |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure at depth in open tank | $P = P_{atm} + \rho g h$ | Use gauge pressure if tank open to atmosphere |
| U-tube manometer reading | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ | Walk down from A, up to interface, down to B |
| Force on vertical gate | $F_R = \rho g y_c A$ | Locate centroid depth $y_c$ from free surface |
| Center of pressure on gate | $y_{cp} = y_c + I_{xc}/(y_c A)$ | Look up $I_{xc}$ for common shapes |
| Force on inclined submerged surface | $F_R = \rho g h_c A$ | Use vertical centroid depth $h_c$, not slant length |
| Buoyancy in two-layer fluid | $F_B = (\rho_1 g V_1 + \rho_2 g V_2)$ | Split volume by the interface |
| Floatation stability | $F_B = W$ at equilibrium line | Check metacentric height for tilt stability |

### 3.4.1 Useful $I_{xc}$ Values for Standard Shapes (Vertical Orientation)

| Shape | Centroid Depth $y_c$ | $I_{xc}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Rectangle (width $b$, height $L$, top at surface) | $L/2$ | $b L^3 / 12$ |
| Triangle (vertex at surface, base $b$ at depth $L$) | $3L/4$ | $b L^3 / 36$ |
| Circle (radius $R$, top at surface) | $2R$ | $\pi R^4 / 4$ |
| Trapezoid | weighted average | parallel-axis combination |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture of Hydrostatic Pressure Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Fluid Properties] --> B[Identify rho, g, h]
    B --> C{Is Vessel Open or Closed?}
    C -- Open --> D[Apply P equals P_atm plus rho g h]
    C -- Closed --> E[Use Manometer Equation]
    D --> F[Compute Pressure at Depth]
    E --> G[Walk Down from A to Interface]
    G --> H[Walk Up from Interface to B]
    H --> I[Solve P_A minus P_B equals rho g Delta h]
    F --> J[Convert to Absolute or Gauge as Required]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Final Pressure Value in Pa or bar]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Force and Center of Pressure on a Vertical Gate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Submerged Vertical Plate] --> B[Free Liquid Surface at y equals 0]
    B --> C[Depth y_c: Location of Centroid]
    C --> D[Pressure increases linearly with depth]
    D --> E[Resultant Force F_R acts at y_cp]
    E --> F["y_cp equals y_c plus I_xc over y_c A"]
    F --> G[F_R equals rho g y_c A]
    G --> H[Pressure distribution is triangular]
    H --> I[F_R acts through centroid of pressure prism]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: U-Tube Manometer Working Principle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Point A in Pipe] --> B[Process Fluid of density rho_f]
    B --> C[Down leg of U-tube]
    C --> D[Interface with Manometric Fluid rho_m]
    D --> E[Height Difference h between two legs]
    E --> F[Up leg to Point B]
    F --> G[Equation: P_A minus P_B equals rho_m minus rho_f times g times h]
    G --> H[Solve for unknown pressure]
```

## 4.4 Flowchart: Floatation Stability Check

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Submerged or Floating Body] --> B[Compute Weight W equals rho_obj g V]
    B --> C[Compute Buoyancy F_B equals rho_fluid g V_sub]
    C --> D{F_B greater than W?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Body Floats partially submerged]
    D -- No --> F[Body Sinks]
    E --> G[Equilibrium: F_B equals W]
    G --> H[Find Submerged Depth]
    H --> I[Compute Metacentric Height GM]
    I --> J{GM positive?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Stable Equilibrium]
    J -- No --> L[Unstable Equilibrium]
```

## 4.5 Concept Map: Relationship Between Core Hydrostatic Quantities

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Density rho] --> B[Specific Weight w equals rho g]
    B --> C[Hydrostatic Pressure P equals rho g h]
    A --> D[Buoyancy F_B equals rho g V]
    C --> E[Manometer Reading h]
    E --> F[Pressure Difference Delta P]
    C --> G[Force on Submerged Surface F_R]
    D --> G
    G --> H[Center of Pressure y_cp]
    H --> I[Moment about Hinge]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]**
**Define:**
**(a)** Specific gravity of a liquid.
**(b)** Hydrostatic pressure.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(a)** Specific gravity (SG) is the ratio of the density of a substance to the density of a standard reference substance, usually water at 4 °C. It is a dimensionless quantity:

$$
SG = \frac{\rho_{substance}}{\rho_{water}} = \frac{\rho_{substance}}{1000 \text{ kg/m}^3}
$$

**(b)** Hydrostatic pressure is the pressure exerted by a fluid at rest due to the weight of the fluid above a given point. Mathematically, at depth $h$ below the free surface:

$$
P = P_0 + \rho g h
$$

where $P_0$ is the surface pressure (often atmospheric).

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]**
**State and explain Pascal's Law. Give one industrial application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Pascal's Law states that *a pressure change applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished to every portion of the fluid and to the walls of the container.*

**Application (1 mark):** The **hydraulic lift / hydraulic jack** used in automobile service stations and heavy machinery lifting systems. A small force on a small-area piston generates a large force on a large-area piston because the pressure is the same on both pistons.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / 2024 Model — Set A]**

**(a)** A vertical rectangular gate of width 2 m and height 3 m is installed in a water reservoir. The top of the gate is located 1 m below the free water surface. Calculate:
(i) The resultant hydrostatic force on the gate.
(ii) The depth of the center of pressure from the free surface.
(iii) The location of the center of pressure measured from the top edge of the gate.
**Use $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. (7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working of a U-tube manometer with a neat sketch. Derive the expression for the pressure difference between two points connected by a U-tube manometer containing a heavier manometric fluid. **Use the relation for the manometer reading correctly, ensuring no confusion of fluids.** (7 marks)

---

### **Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model — Set B]**

**(a)** A wooden block of dimensions 0.4 m × 0.4 m × 0.6 m and density 650 kg/m³ floats in water. Determine:
(i) The depth of immersion of the block.
(ii) The position of the center of buoyancy.
(iii) Whether the block will float in stable equilibrium. **Compute GM using $I = b^4/12$ for the square base.**
**Take $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. (7 marks)**

**(b)** A closed tank contains oil (SG = 0.85) up to a depth of 4 m, with air space above at a gauge pressure of 50 kPa. Determine:
(i) The absolute pressure at the bottom of the tank.
(ii) The equivalent water column height of the oil column.
**Take $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$. (7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Step-by-Step Model Solutions

### **Solution to Question A(a):**

**Given:**
- Width $b = 2$ m, Height $L = 3$ m, Top of gate at depth 1 m.
- $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Step 1 — Centroid Depth.**
The centroid of a rectangle is at its geometric center. So depth of centroid from free surface:

$$
y_c = 1 + \frac{3}{2} = 1 + 1.5 = 2.5 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Area of Gate.**

$$
A = b \times L = 2 \times 3 = 6 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Resultant Hydrostatic Force (7 marks, part i):**

$$
F_R = \rho g y_c A = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 2.5 \times 6
$$

$$
F_R = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 15 = 147150 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_R = 147.15 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step 4 — Depth of Center of Pressure (part ii).**
For a vertical rectangle, $I_{xc} = b L^3 / 12$:

$$
I_{xc} = \frac{2 \times 3^3}{12} = \frac{54}{12} = 4.5 \text{ m}^4
$$

$$
y_{cp} = y_c + \frac{I_{xc}}{y_c A} = 2.5 + \frac{4.5}{2.5 \times 6} = 2.5 + \frac{4.5}{15} = 2.5 + 0.3
$$

$$
\boxed{y_{cp} = 2.8 \text{ m (from free surface)}}
$$

**Step 5 — Center of Pressure from Top Edge of Gate (part iii).**

$$
\boxed{y_{cp, \text{from top}} = 2.8 - 1 = 1.8 \text{ m}}
$$

---

### **Solution to Question A(b):**

A U-tube manometer consists of a U-shaped tube containing a manometric fluid (denser than the process fluid) connected to two points A and B. Let the manometric fluid have density $\rho_m$ and the process fluid above it have density $\rho_f$.

Applying the principle that pressure is the same at the same horizontal level within a connected static fluid, the lower interface of the manometric fluid in the left leg is at the same elevation as the corresponding interface in the right leg.

Let the height difference of the manometric fluid columns be $h$. Starting from A and moving through the manometer to B:

**Step 1 — Pressure at Left Interface (in left leg, going down from A).**

$$
P_{\text{left, interface}} = P_A + \rho_f g \, h_A
$$

**Step 2 — Pressure at Same Level in Right Leg.**

$$
P_{\text{right, same level}} = P_{\text{left, interface}} = P_{\text{right, interface}} + \rho_m g \, h
$$

**Step 3 — Pressure at B (going up from right interface).**

$$
P_B = P_{\text{right, interface}} - \rho_f g \, h_B
$$

**Step 4 — Equate and Rearrange.**

By equalizing the horizontal levels and simplifying, the net result is:

$$
\boxed{P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \, g \, h}
$$

**Sketch (text representation):**
```
    A                                   B
    |                                   |
    |  process fluid (rho_f)            |  process fluid (rho_f)
    |                                   |
    v                                   v
   ___                              ___
  |   |----+                +------|   |
  |   |    |                |      |   |
  |   |    |  manometric    |      |   |
  |   |    |  fluid (rho_m) |      |   |
  |   |    +----------------+      |   |
  |___|         height h           |___|
       <----------- U-tube -------->
```

---

### **Solution to Question B(a):**

**Given:** Block dimensions 0.4 × 0.4 × 0.6 m, $\rho_{block} = 650 \text{ kg/m}^3$, in water ($\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$).

**Step 1 — Volume of Block.**

$$
V_{block} = 0.4 \times 0.4 \times 0.6 = 0.096 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Weight of Block.**

$$
W = \rho_{block} g V_{block} = 650 \times 9.81 \times 0.096 = 612.14 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Depth of Immersion (part i).**
For floating equilibrium, $F_B = W$:

$$
\rho_w g V_{sub} = W
$$

$$
V_{sub} = \frac{W}{\rho_w g} = \frac{612.14}{1000 \times 9.81} = 0.0624 \text{ m}^3
$$

Since the block has a square base of 0.4 m × 0.4 m, the submerged depth is:

$$
h_{sub} = \frac{V_{sub}}{A_{base}} = \frac{0.0624}{0.4 \times 0.4} = \frac{0.0624}{0.16} = 0.39 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{h_{sub} = 0.39 \text{ m}}
$$

**Step 4 — Center of Buoyancy (part ii).**
The center of buoyancy is the centroid of the submerged volume. For a rectangular block immersed to depth $h_{sub}$:

$$
\boxed{y_B = \frac{h_{sub}}{2} = 0.195 \text{ m (above the base of the block)}}
$$

**Step 5 — Stability Check (part iii).**
The block floats with its 0.4 × 0.4 m square base immersed. We compute the metacentric height $GM$:

- $BM = I / V_{sub}$, where $I$ is the second moment of area of the waterline plane (the 0.4 × 0.4 m square) about its centroidal axis.

$$
I = \frac{b^4}{12} = \frac{0.4^4}{12} = \frac{0.0256}{12} = 2.133 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^4
$$

$$
BM = \frac{2.133 \times 10^{-3}}{0.0624} = 0.0342 \text{ m}
$$

- $BG = $ distance between center of buoyancy B and center of gravity G.
  Center of gravity G is at the geometric center of the block:

$$
y_G = \frac{0.6}{2} = 0.3 \text{ m (from the base)}
$$

$$
BG = y_G - y_B = 0.3 - 0.195 = 0.105 \text{ m}
$$

- Metacentric height:

$$
GM = BM - BG = 0.0342 - 0.105 = -0.0708 \text{ m}
$$

Since $GM < 0$, the block is in **unstable equilibrium** in the upright position. It will tilt and float on its side, where stability is recovered.

$$
\boxed{\text{Block will NOT float in stable equilibrium in the upright position.}}
$$

---

### **Solution to Question B(b):**

**Given:** Oil SG = 0.85, depth = 4 m, air gauge pressure = 50 kPa, $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa.

**Step 1 — Absolute Pressure at Top of Oil.**

$$
P_{top, abs} = P_{atm} + P_{air, gauge} = 101.325 + 50 = 151.325 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Hydrostatic Pressure Due to Oil Column.**

$$
P_{oil} = \rho_{oil} g h = (0.85 \times 1000) \times 9.81 \times 4 = 850 \times 9.81 \times 4 = 33354 \text{ Pa} = 33.354 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Absolute Pressure at Bottom of Tank (part i).**

$$
P_{bottom, abs} = P_{top, abs} + P_{oil} = 151.325 + 33.354
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{bottom, abs} = 184.679 \text{ kPa} \approx 184.68 \text{ kPa}}
$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent Water Column Height (part ii).**
Equivalent head of water produces the same pressure as the oil column:

$$
\rho_{water} g h_{water, eq} = \rho_{oil} g h_{oil}
$$

$$
h_{water, eq} = \frac{\rho_{oil}}{\rho_{water}} \times h_{oil} = SG_{oil} \times h_{oil} = 0.85 \times 4
$$

$$
\boxed{h_{water, eq} = 3.4 \text{ m}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up absolute and gauge pressure** — KTU commonly tests this distinction. Always state clearly which one your final answer represents.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert depths to SI (meters) before substitution** — A common 1–2 mark loss in numericals.
> 3. **Manometer sign errors** — When "walking" through the manometer, going **down** increases pressure, going **up** decreases pressure. Sign errors yield a wrong magnitude with a wrong sign.
> 4. **Using slant length instead of vertical depth** for inclined surfaces — The centroid depth is measured **vertically**, not along the surface.
> 5. **Forgetting the $I_{xc}/(y_c A)$ term** for center of pressure — The question specifically asks for center of pressure, not centroid. Don't shortcut.
> 6. **Using the wrong $I_{xc}$** — Look up the second moment of area for the specific geometry (rectangle, triangle, circle) and **about the centroidal axis**, not the free surface axis.
> 7. **Floatation stability** — Students often confuse $BG$ direction. If G is **above** B, $BG$ is positive (block tends to be unstable for tall, thin objects).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fluid Statics**

- ☐ **Hydrostatic equation:** $dP/dh = \rho g$ → integrated: $P = P_0 + \rho g h$
- ☐ **Pascal's Law:** Pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted undiminished.
- ☐ **Absolute vs Gauge:** $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ (and vacuum = $P_{atm} - P_{abs}$)
- ☐ **Manometer reading (simple U-tube):** $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$
- ☐ **Inclined manometer:** $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g L \sin\theta$ — gives better sensitivity for small $\Delta P$.
- ☐ **Resultant force on a flat submerged surface:** $F_R = \rho g h_c A$ where $h_c$ is the **vertical** centroid depth.
- ☐ **Center of pressure:** $y_{cp} = y_c + I_{xc} / (y_c A)$ — always **deeper** than centroid.
- ☐ **Standard $I_{xc}$:** Rectangle $bL^3/12$, Triangle $bL^3/36$, Circle $\pi R^4/4$.
- ☐ **Buoyancy (Archimedes):** $F_B = \rho_{fluid} g V_{displaced}$
- ☐ **Floatation condition:** Buoyancy = Weight → fraction submerged = $\rho_{object}/\rho_{fluid}$
- ☐ **Stability criterion:** Metacentric height $GM = BM - BG > 0$ for stable equilibrium.
- ☐ **Units check:** Always convert kPa, bar, mmHg, mH₂O consistently to Pa before substituting.
- ☐ **Standard values:** $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³, $\rho_{Hg} \approx 13550$ kg/m³, $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa.
- ☐ **Direction matters:** Pressure is a scalar but force is a vector — always specify direction (perpendicular to surface).
- ☐ **Curved surfaces:** Compute horizontal and vertical components separately using projected area and weight of fluid above/below.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forces on Fluids and Hydrostatic Equilibrium, Measurement of Pressure Using Different Types of Manometers, Forces on Submerged Bodies, Stability of Floating and Submerged Bodies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT403 - FLUID FLOW AND PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Forces on Fluids and Hydrostatic Equilibrium, Measurement of Pressure Using Different Types of Manometers, Forces on Submerged Bodies, Stability of Floating and Submerged Bodies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Forces Acting on a Fluid Element

In fluid statics and dynamics, every fluid element experiences two broad categories of forces that govern its motion (or lack thereof).

### A. Body Forces (Volume Forces)
These are forces that act on the **entire mass (or volume)** of the fluid element without physical contact. They arise from external fields acting through the fluid body.

- **Gravity Force**: The most common body force, $F_g = m \cdot g$, acting vertically downward.
- **Centrifugal Force**: Arises in rotating systems (e.g., centrifuges in dairy/bio separations), directed radially outward.
- **Electromagnetic Force**: Relevant in magnetorheological fluids, plasma flows, and electrochemical processes.
- **Buoyancy Force**: Effectively the net gravity minus the displaced fluid weight (a derived body force under gravity).

### B. Surface Forces (Contact Forces)
These forces act **on the surface** of the fluid element through direct contact with surrounding matter.

- **Pressure Force**: Normal to the surface, always compressive.
- **Viscous (Shear) Force**: Tangential to the surface, opposes relative motion; zero in static fluids.
- **Surface Tension Force**: Acts at fluid-fluid interfaces (liquid-gas or liquid-liquid), significant at small scales (capillary rise, droplets).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Distinction**: In a static fluid, **viscous forces vanish** (no shear rate) and **surface tension is negligible** except at interfaces. Hence, the **only surface force retained in hydrostatics is pressure**, balanced against body forces (gravity).

---

## 1.2 Hydrostatic Equilibrium — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Hydrostatic Equilibrium** is the condition of a fluid at rest (or moving as a rigid body with no deformation) in which the **sum of all body forces equals the sum of all pressure gradient forces** at every point within the fluid. The fluid possesses no shear stress, and pressure at any point is **isotropic** (same in all directions).

### The Hydrostatic Equation (Euler's Static Equation)
For a fluid at rest under gravity alone:

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial P}{\partial y} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial P}{\partial z} = -\rho g$$

This means pressure depends **only on vertical depth**, not on horizontal position.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Stacked Books" Model
Imagine a stack of heavy textbooks. The bottom book bears the weight of all books above it. Similarly, in a static fluid, a horizontal layer at depth $h$ bears the weight of **all the fluid columns stacked above it**. The deeper you go, the more fluid weight presses down, so pressure increases linearly with depth. Sideways movement of the stack does not change the pressure at a given depth — only the **vertical column height** matters.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value (SI) |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Density of water (4°C) | $\rho_{w}$ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Density of mercury (20°C) | $\rho_{Hg}$ | **13,600 kg/m³** |
| Specific weight of water | $\gamma_w$ | **9810 N/m³** |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101,325 Pa = 1.01325 bar** |
| Surface tension of water (20°C) | $\sigma$ | **0.0728 N/m** |

### Pascal's Law (Law of Transmission of Fluid Pressure)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pascal's Law**: Pressure applied to an **enclosed, incompressible static fluid** is transmitted **undiminished and equally in all directions** throughout the fluid and acts **perpendicular** to the surfaces of the container.

This principle is the foundation of the **hydraulic press**, **hydraulic lift**, and **hydraulic brakes** in chemical process plants.

---

## 1.3 Measurement of Pressure — Manometers

A **manometer** is a device used to measure fluid pressure by balancing it against a column of liquid (manometric fluid) of known density.

### Classification of Manometers
1. **Simple Manometer** — measures pressure at a single point.
2. **Differential Manometer** — measures the difference in pressure between two points.
3. **Inverted Manometer** — uses a lighter fluid (e.g., oil) above a heavier fluid (water).

### Types of Simple Manometers
- **Piezometer**: A vertical open tube; only suitable for liquids under positive pressure.
- **U-tube Manometer**: A U-shaped tube with manometric fluid (mercury, CCl₄); suitable for gases and liquids.
- **Single-column / Well-Type Manometer**: A reservoir ("well") on one side, graduated tube on the other; amplifies reading accuracy.
- **Inclined Manometer**: A well-type manometer with the graduated tube inclined to amplify small pressure differences (used in low-pressure gas flow measurement).

### Types of Differential Manometers
- **U-tube Differential Manometer**
- **Inverted U-tube Differential Manometer** (used when manometric fluid is lighter than the flowing fluid, e.g., for water pipelines).
- **Micromanometer** — for very small pressure differentials.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: In chemical process industries, differential manometers are extensively used across **orifice meters, venturi meters, and rotameters** to compute flow rates from measured pressure drops.

---

## 1.4 Forces on Submerged Bodies — Buoyancy

### Archimedes' Principle (Formal Statement)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When a body is **fully or partially immersed in a fluid at rest**, it experiences a **vertical upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the body**, and this force acts through the **centroid of the displaced volume** (called the center of buoyancy).

Mathematically:
$$F_B = \rho_{fluid} \cdot g \cdot V_{displaced}$$

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Underwater Balloon"
Picture a sealed balloon pushed under water. The water pushes up on the bottom of the balloon **more strongly** than it pushes down on the top, because the bottom is at greater depth (higher pressure). This **net upward push** is the buoyant force. If the balloon weighs less than the water it displaces, it pops up to the surface and floats. If it weighs more, it sinks. If equal, it hovers in equilibrium (neutral buoyancy — used in submarines!).

### Real-World Engineering Relevance
- Design of **ships, submarines, and offshore platforms**.
- **Flotation tanks** in mineral processing.
- **Fermentation vessels** with agitator impellers — buoyancy affects shaft design.
- **Specific gravity determination** of solids and liquids in quality-control labs.

---

## 1.5 Stability of Floating and Submerged Bodies

A body in a fluid is in **static equilibrium** when the net force and net moment on it are zero. However, equilibrium can be **stable, unstable, or neutral**.

### Definitions
- **Stable Equilibrium**: If slightly tilted, the body returns to its original position. A restoring couple exists.
- **Unstable Equilibrium**: If slightly tilted, the body capsizes and does not return. An overturning couple exists.
- **Neutral Equilibrium**: The body remains in the new tilted position (no restoring or overturning couple).

### Key Concept: The Metacenter
The **metacenter (M)** is the point where the **line of action of the new buoyant force** (after a small angular displacement) intersects the **original vertical axis** of the body.

- **Metacentric Height (GM)** = distance between the **center of gravity (G)** and the **metacenter (M)**.

### Stability Criterion
- If **GM > 0 (M above G)** → **Stable**
- If **GM = 0 (M at G)** → **Neutral**
- If **GM < 0 (M below G)** → **Unstable**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip**: For **submerged bodies**, the metacenter lies above the center of gravity for a stable configuration, but for **floating bodies** with the metacenter below G, even a tall narrow ship can capsize. This is why ships are designed with a **low center of gravity** (heavy machinery placed at the bottom).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Tippy Toy" and the "Brick"
- A flat-bottomed **pancake-shaped object** floating on water is hard to tip over (low G, large metacentric height) → **stable**.
- A tall, narrow **pencil** balanced on its tip has G above M → **unstable**; the slightest nudge topples it.
- A perfect **sphere** floating in a fluid with uniform density has G = M → **neutral**; it stays wherever you push it.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hydrostatic Pressure Equation — Detailed Derivation Logic

Consider a small fluid element of dimensions $dx \times dy \times dz$ submerged in a static fluid of density $\rho$.

- **Step 1 — Force Balance in x-direction**:
  Pressure on the left face ($P$) pushes right; pressure on the right face ($P + \frac{\partial P}{\partial x}dx$) pushes left. Net force = 0 in static fluid.
  $$\left(P - \left[P + \frac{\partial P}{\partial x}dx\right]\right) dy\, dz = 0 \implies \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} = 0$$

- **Step 2 — Force Balance in y-direction**:
  $$\frac{\partial P}{\partial y} = 0$$

- **Step 3 — Force Balance in z-direction** (vertical, against gravity):
  Pressure on bottom face pushes up; pressure on top face pushes down; gravity acts downward on the mass.
  $$\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} = -\rho g$$

- **Step 4 — Integration**:
  Integrating the z-equation from point 1 (at depth $z_1$) to point 2 (at depth $z_2$):
  $$P_2 - P_1 = -\rho g (z_2 - z_1) = \rho g (z_1 - z_2) = \rho g h$$
  where $h = z_1 - z_2$ is the **vertical depth** from point 1 to point 2.

### Why Does This Matter in Engineering?
This single equation underpins:
- **Tank wall design** (knowing the maximum pressure at the bottom of a chemical storage tank).
- **Dam engineering** (hydrostatic thrust on a dam face).
- **Piezometer installation height** in process piping.
- **Blood pressure measurement** in biomedical engineering (modified hydrostatic principle).

---

## 2.2 The Hydrostatic Paradox

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **hydrostatic pressure at the bottom of a vessel depends ONLY on the height of the fluid column above it, NOT on the shape or total volume of the vessel**. Three vessels with identical base area and fluid height produce identical bottom pressure, even if they hold vastly different volumes.

This counterintuitive result follows directly from $P = \rho g h$.

---

## 2.3 Types of Manometers — Operating Principles

### A. Piezometer
Used for measuring gauge pressure of a liquid in a pipe. The height of the liquid column $h$ in the open tube directly gives the pressure head.

$$P_A = \rho g h$$

**Limitation**: Cannot be used for gases (the gas column would be huge) or for negative pressure (vaporization occurs).

### B. U-Tube Manometer
A U-tube containing a **manometric fluid (manometer liquid)** of density $\rho_m$, denser than the working fluid of density $\rho_f$. The pressure at point A in the pipe is balanced by the manometer column.

$$P_A = \rho_m g h - \rho_f g h_1$$

where $h$ is the manometer reading and $h_1$ is the height of the working fluid above the manometer-fluid interface.

### C. Well-Type (Single-Column) Manometer
The well has a much larger cross-section than the graduated tube, so the liquid level in the well remains essentially constant. The pressure is read directly from the scale in the narrow limb.

$$P_A = \left(1 + \frac{a}{A}\right) \rho_m g h$$

where $a$ = area of narrow tube, $A$ = area of well, $h$ = difference in levels. Often $a/A \ll 1$, so $P_A \approx \rho_m g h$.

### D. Inclined Manometer
The graduated tube is inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal. This multiplies the vertical pressure head by $1/\sin\theta$, allowing precise measurement of very small pressures.

$$P_A = \rho_m g L \sin\theta$$

where $L$ is the length of fluid travel along the inclined tube.

### E. Inverted U-Tube Differential Manometer
The manometric fluid is **lighter** than the working fluid (e.g., oil for water lines, air for water). The differential pressure is:

$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_f - \rho_m) g h$$

### F. Two-Fluid U-Tube Manometer
Two immiscible manometric fluids of close but distinct densities amplify small pressure differences dramatically.

$$P_A - P_B = g h \left(\rho_{m1} - \rho_{m2}\right) \left(1 + \frac{a}{A}\right)$$

---

## 2.4 Forces on Submerged Bodies — Detailed Mechanics

### Center of Buoyancy
- The **center of buoyancy (B)** is the **centroid of the displaced fluid volume**.
- For a fully submerged body with uniform density, B coincides with the geometric centroid.
- For a partially submerged (floating) body, B depends on the **submerged geometry**, which changes as the body tilts.

### Apparent Weight (Weight in Fluid)
$$W_{apparent} = W_{actual} - F_B = (\rho_{body} - \rho_{fluid}) \cdot g \cdot V$$

### Three Cases
1. $\rho_{body} > \rho_{fluid}$ → Body **sinks** (apparent weight positive).
2. $\rho_{body} = \rho_{fluid}$ → Body is in **neutral equilibrium** anywhere in the fluid.
3. $\rho_{body} < \rho_{fluid}$ → Body **floats**, with a fraction $V_{sub}/V_{total} = \rho_{body}/\rho_{fluid}$ submerged.

---

## 2.5 Stability — Metacentric Height (GM) Detailed

For a floating body tilted by a small angle $\theta$ about a longitudinal axis:

$$GM = \frac{I}{V_{sub}} - BG$$

where:
- $I$ = second moment of area of the waterline (plan) section about the tilt axis,
- $V_{sub}$ = submerged volume,
- $BG$ = distance from center of buoyancy (B) to center of gravity (G), $BG = \bar{x}_G - \bar{x}_B$ (positive when G is above B).

For a **fully submerged body**:
$$GM = \frac{I}{V_{sub}} - BG$$
with $I$ taken about the relevant axis of tilt through the centroid.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **KTU Board Exam favorites**. Memorize the variables, units, and sign conventions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Sign Convention |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Hydrostatic Pressure Difference | $P_2 - P_1 = \rho g h$ | $\rho$ in kg/m³, $g$ in m/s², $h$ in m | $P_2 > P_1$ when $h > 0$ (point 2 below point 1) |
| 2 | Absolute Pressure | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ | All in Pa | Gauge can be negative (vacuum) |
| 3 | Gauge / Vacuum Relation | $P_{vacuum} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ | All in Pa | When $P_{abs} < P_{atm}$ |
| 4 | Piezometer | $P_A = \rho g h$ | $h$ = liquid column rise | Open to atmosphere |
| 5 | U-Tube Manometer | $P_A = \rho_m g h - \rho_f g h_1$ | $h_1$ = working fluid above manometer | Manometric fluid below |
| 6 | Differential U-Tube | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ | $h$ = differential reading | Same fluid on both sides |
| 7 | Inverted U-Tube | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_f - \rho_m) g h$ | $m$ fluid above, lighter | Lighter manometric fluid |
| 8 | Inclined Manometer | $P_A = \rho_m g L \sin\theta$ | $L$ = travel along slope, $\theta$ = inclination | $\theta$ from horizontal |
| 9 | Buoyant Force (Archimedes) | $F_B = \rho_{fluid} \cdot g \cdot V_{disp}$ | $V_{disp}$ in m³ | Acts upward through B |
| 10 | Apparent Weight | $W_{app} = V \cdot g (\rho_{body} - \rho_{fluid})$ | $V$ = body volume | Positive = sinks; negative = floats up |
| 11 | Fraction Submerged (Float) | $V_{sub}/V = \rho_{body}/\rho_{fluid}$ | Dimensionless ratio | Used for hull design |
| 12 | Metacentric Height (Float) | $GM = I/V_{sub} - BG$ | $I$ in m⁴, $V_{sub}$ in m³, $BG$ in m | $GM > 0$ stable |
| 13 | Restoring Couple | $C_R = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$ | $W$ = weight of body | $C_R$ is restoring when $GM > 0$ |
| 14 | Specific Gravity | $S = \rho_{substance}/\rho_{water}$ | Dimensionless | $S < 1$ floats on water |
| 15 | Capillary Rise (bonus) | $h = 2\sigma\cos\theta/(\rho g r)$ | $\sigma$ surface tension, $r$ tube radius | Used in fine-bore manometers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip**: Always explicitly state the **datum (reference level)** before applying hydrostatic equations. Many KTU students lose marks by jumping into formulas without defining where $z = 0$ is.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Storage tanks** (oil, chemical): Wall thickness increases with depth due to hydrostatic pressure.
- **Dam design**: Horizontal thrust at base = $\frac{1}{2}\rho g H^2 \cdot L$ per unit width.
- **Submarines**: Ballast tanks control $\rho_{body}$ to achieve sinking, hovering, or surfacing.
- **Bridges over rivers**: Pier foundations must resist buoyancy uplift in flood conditions.
- **Manometers in process plants**: Differential manometers measure flow across orifice plates in pipelines.
- **Pharmaceutical packaging**: Stability of liquid-filled vials is tested using buoyancy principles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Hydrostatic Pressure Equation (Full Euler Derivation)

We start with Newton's second law applied to a static fluid element. Since the fluid is at rest, the **net acceleration is zero**, and the **net force is zero**.

### Step 1 — Define the Fluid Element
Consider a rectangular fluid element of dimensions $dx \times dy \times dz$ at coordinates $(x, y, z)$, with center of mass coincident with that point.

### Step 2 — Surface (Pressure) Forces
The pressure on each face acts **perpendicular** and **inward** (compressive).

- On the face at $x$ (left): Force $= P \cdot dy\, dz$ acting in $+x$ direction.
- On the face at $x + dx$ (right): Force $= \left(P + \frac{\partial P}{\partial x}dx\right) dy\, dz$ acting in $-x$ direction.
- Similarly for $y$ and $z$ faces.

### Step 3 — Net Pressure Force in Each Direction

- **x-direction**:
  $$F_{Px} = P\, dy\, dz - \left(P + \frac{\partial P}{\partial x}dx\right) dy\, dz = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} dx\, dy\, dz$$

- **y-direction**:
  $$F_{Py} = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial y} dx\, dy\, dz$$

- **z-direction**:
  $$F_{Pz} = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} dx\, dy\, dz$$

### Step 4 — Body Force (Gravity)
Gravity acts only in the $-z$ direction with magnitude $\rho \cdot g \cdot dV$:

$$F_{gz} = -\rho g \, dx\, dy\, dz$$

### Step 5 — Equilibrium (Static) Condition
Since the fluid is at rest, $\sum F = 0$ in every direction:

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} = 0 \qquad (1)$$

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial y} = 0 \qquad (2)$$

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} = -\rho g \qquad (3)$$

### Step 6 — Integration Along z
Integrating equation (3) from a reference point 1 (at elevation $z_1$) to point 2 (at elevation $z_2$):

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} dP = -\rho g \int_{z_1}^{z_2} dz$$

$$P_2 - P_1 = -\rho g (z_2 - z_1)$$

### Step 7 — Express in Terms of Depth
If $h$ is the depth of point 1 **below** point 2 (so $z_1 < z_2$ and $h = z_2 - z_1$):

$$P_1 - P_2 = \rho g h \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{P_1 = P_2 + \rho g h}$$

If point 2 is open to atmosphere, $P_2 = P_{atm}$:

$$\boxed{P_{abs} = P_{atm} + \rho g h}$$

This is the **fundamental hydrostatic pressure equation**, valid for incompressible, static fluids.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the U-Tube Manometer Equation

Consider a pipe carrying fluid of density $\rho_f$ under pressure $P_A$. A U-tube manometer containing a heavier manometric fluid of density $\rho_m$ is connected at point A.

### Setup
- Point A is at the centerline of the pipe.
- Working fluid of density $\rho_f$ fills the connecting tube from A to the top of the manometric fluid column on the left side (height $h_1$ above the interface).
- Manometric fluid of density $\rho_m$ fills the rest of the U-tube up to a level that is $h$ above the corresponding level on the right side.
- The right side is open to atmosphere (or connects to a reference).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

- **Step 1**: Start at A with pressure $P_A$ and move down the left limb to the manometric fluid interface.
  $$P_{\text{interface (left)}} = P_A + \rho_f g h_1$$

- **Step 2**: Move across the manometric fluid (horizontal at the bottom) — no pressure change.
  $$P_{\text{interface (right)}} = P_{\text{interface (left)}} = P_A + \rho_f g h_1$$

- **Step 3**: Move up the right limb a distance $h$ in the manometric fluid, reaching the surface exposed to atmosphere.
  $$P_{atm} = P_A + \rho_f g h_1 - \rho_m g h$$

- **Step 4**: Rearrange for $P_A$:
  $$\boxed{P_A = P_{atm} + \rho_m g h - \rho_f g h_1}$$

If the right limb is connected to another point B (differential manometer), the same procedure gives:

$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Buoyant Force (Archimedes' Principle)

Consider a fully submerged body of arbitrary shape. We will prove the buoyant force equals the weight of displaced fluid.

### Step 1 — Pressure at Top and Bottom Surfaces
For a column-shaped element of cross-section $dA$ at the bottom of the body:

- Pressure at top of element (at depth $h_1$): $P_{top} = P_{atm} + \rho g h_1$
- Pressure at bottom of element (at depth $h_2$): $P_{bot} = P_{atm} + \rho g h_2$

### Step 2 — Net Upward Force on the Element
$$dF_B = (P_{bot} - P_{top}) dA = \rho g (h_2 - h_1) dA = \rho g \, dV$$

where $dV$ is the volume of the small column element.

### Step 3 — Integrate Over the Entire Body
The total buoyant force is the integral of $dF_B$ over the submerged surface, but since pressure on vertical sides cancels out, only the vertical components remain, and the integration reduces to:

$$F_B = \int \rho g \, dV = \rho g \cdot V_{displaced}$$

### Step 4 — Recognize as Fluid Weight
$$F_B = \rho g V_{disp} = m_{fluid\,displaced} \cdot g = W_{fluid\,displaced}$$

$$\boxed{F_B = \text{Weight of displaced fluid}}$$

This is **Archimedes' Principle**.

### Step 5 — Direction
The pressure field is hydrostatic, and the resultant of all pressure forces on the closed submerged surface passes through the **centroid of the displaced volume** (center of buoyancy, B), directed vertically upward.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Metacentric Height Formula

For a floating body in equilibrium, consider a small angular tilt $\theta$ about a longitudinal axis.

### Step 1 — Identify the Original and New Buoyancy Lines
- Original vertical axis passes through the center of gravity $G$ and the original center of buoyancy $B$.
- After tilting by angle $\theta$, the new center of buoyancy $B'$ is shifted because the **submerged geometry changes** (more volume on one side, less on the other).

### Step 2 — Shift of Center of Buoyancy
The new line of action of the buoyant force (vertical) is offset laterally from the original $B$ by a small distance $\bar{BB'} \approx \theta \cdot \overline{GB_n}$ where $\overline{GB_n}$ is some geometric quantity.

For small angles, the lateral shift is given by:

$$\overline{BB'} = \frac{I \cdot \theta}{V_{sub}}$$

where $I$ is the **second moment of area** of the waterline plane about the axis of tilt.

### Step 3 — Locate the Metacenter
The metacenter $M$ is the intersection of the **new line of action of buoyancy** (vertical through $B'$) with the **original vertical axis** (line $GB$).

From the geometry of the small triangle $GBM$:

$$\tan\theta = \frac{\overline{BM}}{\overline{BM}} \quad \text{(not needed; we go directly)}$$

The relationship is:
$$\overline{BM} = \frac{I}{V_{sub}}$$

This follows because, for small angles, $BB' = BM \cdot \theta$, so $BM \cdot \theta = I \cdot \theta / V_{sub}$, hence $BM = I/V_{sub}$.

### Step 4 — Metacentric Height
$$\overline{GM} = \overline{GB} + \overline{BM} \quad \text{or} \quad \overline{GM} = \overline{BM} - \overline{BG}$$

Since $\overline{GB} = -\overline{BG}$:

$$\boxed{GM = \frac{I}{V_{sub}} - BG}$$

### Step 5 — Stability Criterion
- If $GM > 0$ → $M$ is above $G$ → **Stable** (restoring couple).
- If $GM = 0$ → **Neutral**.
- If $GM < 0$ → **Unstable** (overturning couple).

### Step 6 — Restoring Couple (Bonus)
The restoring couple that rights the body is:

$$C_R = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$$

where $W$ is the weight of the body.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem**: A U-tube manometer connects two points A and B in a horizontal water pipeline. The manometric fluid is mercury ($\rho_m = 13600$ kg/m³). The mercury level in the limb near A is 0.30 m **below** the pipeline centerline, and the level in the limb near B is 0.50 m **below** the pipeline centerline. The difference in mercury levels is $h = 0.20$ m. Find the pressure difference $P_A - P_B$.

### Solution
Given:
- $\rho_m = 13600$ kg/m³
- $\rho_f = 1000$ kg/m³ (water)
- $h = 0.20$ m
- Mercury is **higher on the B side** (since B's limb is more depressed) → $P_A > P_B$ (intuitively, A pushes harder).

Apply the differential manometer formula:

$$P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot h$$

Substituting:

$$P_A - P_B = (13600 - 1000) \times 9.81 \times 0.20$$

$$P_A - P_B = 12600 \times 9.81 \times 0.20$$

$$P_A - P_B = 12600 \times 1.962$$

$$P_A - P_B = 24721.2 \, \text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{P_A - P_B \approx 24.72 \, \text{kPa}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake**: Forgetting to **subtract** $\rho_f$ from $\rho_m$ — students often write $P_A - P_B = \rho_m g h$, ignoring the correction for the working fluid column above the manometer interface. This error becomes more pronounced for high-pressure pipelines where $h_1$ is large.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

```mermaid

    B --> B2["Centrifugal Force"]:::leaf
    B --> B3["Electromagnetic Force"]:::leaf

    C --> C1["Pressure Force"]:::leaf
    C --> C2["Viscous Shear Force"]:::leaf
    C --> C3["Surface Tension Force"]:::leaf

    C1 --> D1["Acts normal to surface"]:::note
    C2 --> D2["Acts tangent to surface"]:::note
    C3 --> D3["Acts at fluid interfaces"]:::note

    B1 --> E1["Dominant in most engineering flows"]:::note
    C2 --> E2["Zero in static fluids"]:::note
```

## 4.4 Block Architecture — Buoyancy & Stability Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    A --> B["STEP 1: Compute displaced volume V_disp"]:::proc
    B --> C["STEP 2: Compute buoyant force F_B equals rho_f g V_disp"]:::proc
    C --> D{"Compare body density rho_b with rho_f"}:::decision

    D -->|"rho_b greater than rho_f"| E["Sinks: Apparent weight is positive"]:::output
    D -->|"rho_b equals rho_f"| F["Neutral: Hovers in fluid"]:::output
    D -->|"rho_b less than rho_f"| G["Floats: V_sub by V equals rho_b by rho_f"]:::output

    G --> H["STEP 3: For floating body compute I waterline plane"]:::proc
    H --> I["STEP 4: Compute metacentric height GM equals I by V_sub minus BG"]:::proc
```

## 4.5 Hydrostatic Pressure Variation — Conceptual Plot (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Fluid Surface at z equals 0: P equals P_atm"]:::top
    B["At depth h: P equals P_atm plus rho g h"]:::mid
    C["At depth 2h: P equals P_atm plus 2 rho g h"]:::bottom

    A -->|"Linear increase"| B
    B -->|"Linear increase"| C

    A_note["Reference Datum: z equals 0"]:::n --> A
    B_note["Pressure Head: h metres of fluid"]:::n --> B
    C_note["Pressure Head: 2h metres of fluid"]:::n --> C

    classDef top fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#854d0e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef mid fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef bottom fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The pressure-vs-depth relationship is **linear**, with slope $\rho g$ in Pascals per metre. This is the foundation of piezometer and manometer operation — they essentially measure a **vertical height** that is then converted to pressure using $\rho g$.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Hydrostatic Equilibrium and State the Hydrostatic Pressure Equation. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer**:

> **Hydrostatic Equilibrium** is the state of a fluid at rest in which there is no relative motion between fluid layers, implying the absence of shear stress. At every point in the fluid, the **pressure gradient force balances the body force** (gravity).

The fundamental equation of hydrostatics is:

$$P_2 = P_1 + \rho g h$$

where $P_1$ and $P_2$ are the pressures at two points within the static fluid, $\rho$ is the fluid density, $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity, and $h$ is the vertical depth of point 1 below point 2.

> **Key Implication**: Pressure increases linearly with depth and is independent of horizontal position. The free surface of a static liquid always lies in a horizontal plane (equipotential surface for gravity).

---

### Question 2: State and Explain Archimedes' Principle. What is Apparent Weight? `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**Model Answer**:

> **Archimedes' Principle**: When a body is fully or partially immersed in a fluid, it experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the **weight of the fluid displaced** by the body. This force acts vertically upward through the **center of buoyancy** (centroid of displaced volume).

**Mathematical Form**:
$$F_B = \rho_{fluid} \cdot g \cdot V_{displaced}$$

**Apparent Weight**: The true weight of a body in vacuum is $W = m \cdot g$. When immersed in a fluid, the body experiences a buoyant force that **reduces the effective weight** experienced:

$$W_{apparent} = W - F_B = V \cdot g \cdot (\rho_{body} - \rho_{fluid})$$

- If $W_{apparent} > 0$ → body sinks.
- If $W_{apparent} = 0$ → body is neutrally buoyant.
- If $W_{apparent} < 0$ → body rises (and floats at the surface).

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1 — Pressure Sign Convention**: Many students confuse **gauge vs absolute** pressure. If a question asks for "pressure of gas in the pipe" without specifying, by convention give **gauge pressure**. If it explicitly states "absolute," subtract atmospheric. Mixing these in a single problem costs 2–3 marks easily.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2 — Manometer Direction Error**: In a U-tube manometer, students often guess whether $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f)gh$ or $(\rho_f - \rho_m)gh$ without a clear sign convention. Always **start from A, walk to B, and write the pressure changes at each level change**. The sign of the final expression tells you the direction.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #3 — Metacenter vs Centroid**: In stability problems, students compute the metacentric height using the wrong $I$ (second moment of area of the waterline section, **not** the submerged cross-section) or the wrong $V$ (use **submerged volume**, not total body volume for floating bodies). This error produces wrong stability conclusions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**A. (a)** Derive the equation for the pressure difference measured by a **differential U-tube manometer** connecting two points A and B in a pipeline carrying a fluid of density $\rho_f$. The manometric fluid has density $\rho_m$. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**A. (b)** A vertical cylindrical tank of diameter 2 m and height 3 m is filled with oil of specific gravity 0.85. Calculate: (i) The hydrostatic pressure at the bottom of the tank in kPa. (ii) The total thrust force on the tank bottom. (iii) The thrust on the curved side wall. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution A(a):

**Assumptions**:
1. Manometric fluid is **immiscible** with the working fluid and is heavier.
2. The connecting tubes are filled with the working fluid up to the manometric fluid interface.
3. Fluid densities are constant (incompressible).
4. The system is in static equilibrium.

**Derivation**:

Let $A$ and $B$ be the two points in the pipeline. The manometric fluid in the U-tube has density $\rho_m$, and the pipeline fluid has density $\rho_f$.

- Let the level of manometric fluid in the limb connected to A be at height $h_1$ above some reference.
- Let the level in the limb connected to B be at height $h_2$ above the same reference, with $h_2 > h_1$ (so B side is higher, meaning $P_A > P_B$).
- The difference in manometric fluid levels is $h = h_2 - h_1$.
- The working fluid fills the connecting tube from each point up to its respective manometric fluid interface. Let the height of working fluid above A in the connecting tube be $h_{A1}$, and above B be $h_{B1}$.

**Step 1**: Start at A, descend to the manometric fluid interface on the A side:

$$P_{A,\text{interface}} = P_A + \rho_f g h_{A1}$$

**Step 2**: At the bottom of the U-tube, the pressure is the same across the manometric fluid (since the manometric fluid is continuous at the same level in the U-tube bend):

$$P_{A,\text{interface}} = P_{B,\text{interface}}$$

**Step 3**: From the B side interface, ascend by $h$ in manometric fluid and then $h_{B1}$ in working fluid to reach B:

$$P_B = P_{B,\text{interface}} - \rho_m g h - \rho_f g h_{B1}$$

**Step 4**: Combine:

$$P_A - P_B = \rho_m g h + \rho_f g h_{B1} - \rho_f g h_{A1}$$

If the pipeline is horizontal and at the same elevation (i.e., $h_{A1} = h_{B1}$), the working fluid terms cancel:

$$\boxed{P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h}$$

This is the **differential U-tube manometer equation**.

---

#### Model Solution A(b):

**Given**:
- Tank diameter $D = 2$ m, so radius $R = 1$ m
- Tank height $H = 3$ m
- Oil specific gravity $S = 0.85$, so $\rho_{oil} = 850$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa (assume gauge pressure is needed → use $\rho g h$ only)

**(i) Pressure at the bottom** (gauge):

$$P_{bottom} = \rho_{oil} \cdot g \cdot H = 850 \times 9.81 \times 3$$

$$P_{bottom} = 25015.5 \, \text{Pa} \approx 25.02 \, \text{kPa (gauge)}$$

**(ii) Total thrust on tank bottom**:

$$F_{bottom} = P_{bottom} \times A_{bottom} = \rho g H \times \pi R^2$$

$$F_{bottom} = 850 \times 9.81 \times 3 \times \pi \times (1)^2$$

$$F_{bottom} = 25015.5 \times \pi$$

$$F_{bottom} = 78596.7 \, \text{N} \approx 78.60 \, \text{kN}$$

**(iii) Thrust on curved side wall**:

The horizontal thrust on a curved surface equals the thrust on the **vertical projection** of that surface.

- Projected area of the curved wall = diameter × height = $D \times H = 2 \times 3 = 6 \, \text{m}^2$
- Pressure at the centroid of the projected area (at depth $H/2 = 1.5$ m):

$$P_{centroid} = \rho g \times \frac{H}{2} = 850 \times 9.81 \times 1.5 = 12508 \, \text{Pa}$$

- Horizontal thrust:

$$F_H = P_{centroid} \times A_{projected} = 12508 \times 6 = 75048 \, \text{N} \approx 75.05 \, \text{kN}$$

> **Answers**: (i) **25.02 kPa**, (ii) **78.60 kN**, (iii) **75.05 kN**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**B. (a)** Explain the concept of **metacenter and metacentric height** for a floating body. Derive the expression for the metacentric height. State and justify the stability criterion. **(7 marks)**

**B. (b)** A solid cube of side 0.4 m and density 720 kg/m³ floats in water. Find: (i) The depth of immersion. (ii) The position of the center of buoyancy. (iii) Whether the equilibrium is stable, and the metacentric height. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution B(a):

**Concept of Metacenter**:
When a floating body is tilted by a small angle $\theta$ about a longitudinal axis, the center of buoyancy $B$ shifts to a new position $B'$ because the submerged volume redistributes. The vertical line of action of the new buoyant force, when extended upward, intersects the **original vertical axis** (the line $GB$) at a point $M$ called the **metacenter**.

**Metacentric Height** $\overline{GM}$ is the distance between the **center of gravity (G)** and the **metacenter (M)**, measured along the original vertical axis.

**Derivation of $GM$**:

When the body tilts by a small angle $\theta$:
- The new center of buoyancy $B'$ is displaced laterally by a distance $\overline{BB'} = \dfrac{I \cdot \theta}{V_{sub}}$, where $I$ is the second moment of area of the **waterline plane** about the tilt axis, and $V_{sub}$ is the submerged volume.
- The metacenter is located on the original vertical line at a distance $\overline{BM} = \dfrac{I}{V_{sub}}$ from the new buoyancy line.
- The metacentric height is:

$$\overline{GM} = \overline{BM} - \overline{BG} = \dfrac{I}{V_{sub}} - BG$$

**Stability Criterion**:
The body is in **stable equilibrium** if the couple produced by the weight (down through G) and the buoyant force (up through B') is **restoring** — i.e., it tries to right the body. This happens only if the metacenter lies **above** the center of gravity.

- $\overline{GM} > 0$ → **Stable** (restoring couple acts)
- $\overline{GM} = 0$ → **Neutral**
- $\overline{GM} < 0$ → **Unstable** (overturning couple capsizes the body)

The restoring couple is $C_R = W \cdot \overline{GM} \cdot \sin\theta$.

---

#### Model Solution B(b):

**Given**:
- Side $a = 0.4$ m
- $\rho_{cube} = 720$ kg/m³
- $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³

**(i) Depth of immersion**:

For floating, the weight of the cube equals the buoyant force:

$$\rho_{cube} \cdot g \cdot V_{cube} = \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot V_{sub}$$

$$\rho_{cube} \cdot a^3 = \rho_{water} \cdot a^2 \cdot d$$

where $d$ = depth of immersion. Solving:

$$d = \frac{\rho_{cube} \cdot a}{\rho_{water}} = \frac{720 \times 0.4}{1000} = \frac{288}{1000} = 0.288 \, \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{d = 0.288 \, \text{m}}$$

**(ii) Position of center of buoyancy**:

The center of buoyancy is at the centroid of the submerged portion, which for a rectangular block is at depth $d/2$ from the waterline (or equivalently at height $a/2 - d/2$ from the bottom).

From the bottom of the cube:
$$h_B = \frac{d}{2} = \frac{0.288}{2} = 0.144 \, \text{m}$$

The center of gravity $G$ is at the geometric center of the cube, at height $a/2 = 0.2$ m from the bottom.

Therefore, $BG = h_G - h_B = 0.2 - 0.144 = 0.056$ m.

**(iii) Stability and metacentric height**:

For a square cross-section waterline of side $a = 0.4$ m, the second moment of area about the tilt axis (say, a horizontal axis through the center of the waterline) is:

$$I = \frac{a^4}{12} = \frac{(0.4)^4}{12} = \frac{0.0256}{12} = 0.002133 \, \text{m}^4$$

Submerged volume:
$$V_{sub} = a^2 \cdot d = (0.4)^2 \times 0.288 = 0.16 \times 0.288 = 0.04608 \, \text{m}^3$$

Metacentric height:
$$\overline{GM} = \frac{I}{V_{sub}} - BG = \frac{0.002133}{0.04608} - 0.056$$

$$\overline{GM} = 0.04630 - 0.056 = -0.00970 \, \text{m}$$

$$\overline{GM} \approx -9.7 \, \text{mm}$$

Since $GM < 0$, the equilibrium is **unstable**. The cube will tip over if slightly disturbed (the density is too low and the cube is too tall relative to its waterline area — a classic example of why wide, low hulls are stable).

> **Answers**: (i) **d = 0.288 m**, (ii) **h_B = 0.144 m from bottom; BG = 0.056 m**, (iii) **Unstable, GM ≈ −9.7 mm**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #4 — Negative GM for Floating Bodies**: Students often conclude "the body is unstable" only after computing $I/V_{sub}$ and comparing with $BG$ but forget to subtract $BG$. A correct calculation that yields a **negative $GM$** is the desired result and earns full marks. Do not "round" the answer to zero or change the sign — show all steps clearly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision List — Memorize Before Exam Day**

### Forces on Fluids
- ☐ **Body forces** act on volume; **surface forces** act on area.
- ☐ In static fluids, **viscous shear = 0**, surface tension is **negligible** (unless at interfaces).
- ☐ The only relevant forces in hydrostatics: **gravity (body) and pressure (surface)**.

### Hydrostatic Equation
- ☐ $P_2 = P_1 + \rho g h$ — pressure depends only on **vertical depth**.
- ☐ Pressure is **isotropic** (same in all directions) at a point in a static fluid.
- ☐ **Hydrostatic paradox**: bottom pressure is independent of vessel shape.

### Pascal's Law
- ☐ Pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted **undiminished in all directions**.
- ☐ Foundation of hydraulic press, hydraulic lift, hydraulic brakes.

### Manometers
- ☐ **Piezometer**: $P_A = \rho g h$ — open vertical tube.
- ☐ **U-tube**: $P_A = \rho_m g h - \rho_f g h_1$ (with $h_1$ = working fluid above manometer).
- ☐ **Differential U-tube**: $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ (same fluid on both sides, horizontal pipeline).
- ☐ **Inverted U-tube**: $P_A - P_B = (\rho_f - \rho_m) g h$ (manometer fluid **lighter** than working fluid).
- ☐ **Inclined**: $P = \rho g L \sin\theta$ — for low pressures (gas flows).
- ☐ **Well-type**: factor $(1 + a/A) \approx 1$ for small tube/well area ratio.
- ☐ **Two-fluid micromanometer**: high sensitivity for very small $\Delta P$.

### Buoyancy & Archimedes
- ☐ $F_B = \rho_{fluid} g V_{displaced}$ — acts upward through center of buoyancy $B$.
- ☐ **Apparent weight**: $W_{app} = W - F_B = V g (\rho_{body} - \rho_{fluid})$.
- ☐ For floating body: $V_{sub}/V_{total} = \rho_{body}/\rho_{fluid}$.

### Stability
- ☐ **Metacenter M**: intersection of new buoyancy line with original vertical axis.
- ☐ $\overline{BM} = I/V_{sub}$ where $I$ = second moment of waterline area about tilt axis.
- ☐ $\overline{GM} = \overline{BM} - \overline{BG} = I/V_{sub} - BG$.
- ☐ **Stability rule of thumb**: $GM > 0$ stable, $GM = 0$ neutral, $GM < 0$ unstable.
- ☐ **Restoring couple**: $C_R = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$.
- ☐ For **submerged bodies** of uniform density, $G$ is at the geometric center; for floating bodies, $G$ may be above $B$.

### Units & Sign Conventions
- ☐ Use **SI units**: Pa, N, m, kg, m/s², kg/m³.
- ☐ $g = 9.81$ m/s² unless otherwise stated.
- ☐ Mercury density $\approx 13{,}600$ kg/m³; water $\approx 1000$ kg/m³ at 4°C.
- ☐ **Pressure head** in metres of fluid; convert to Pa using $\rho g$.
- ☐ **Absolute pressure = gauge + atmospheric** (gauge can be negative for vacuum).

### Common KTU Mistakes to Avoid
- ☐ ❌ Forgetting to include atmospheric pressure when "absolute" is asked.
- ☐ ❌ Confusing piezometer (only for liquids) with U-tube (works for gases too).
- ☐ ❌ Using total body volume instead of submerged volume in $I/V_{sub}$ for floating stability.
- ☐ ❌ Treating metacenter as a fixed point — it depends on the tilt axis and the waterline geometry.
- ☐ ❌ Mixing up the sign of $(\rho_m - \rho_f)$ vs $(\rho_f - \rho_m)$ in manometer equations.
- ☐ ❌ Computing thrust on a curved surface directly — always use the **projected area** method.
- ☐ ❌ Ignoring the **direction** of buoyant force — it is always vertically upward through $B$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Concept of Drag — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DRAG (Fluid Resistance):** Drag is the **resistive force** exerted by a fluid on a body moving relative to the fluid. It acts **parallel to the direction of fluid motion** (or opposite to the body's motion through the fluid) and always opposes the relative motion.

In vectorial form, the total drag force experienced by a body immersed in a flowing fluid is given by the universal drag equation:

$$F_D = C_D \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot V^2 \cdot A$$

Where:
- $F_D$ = Drag force **[N]**
- $C_D$ = Drag coefficient (dimensionless) **[—]**
- $\rho$ = Density of the fluid **[kg/m³]**
- $V$ = Free-stream velocity of the fluid **[m/s]**
- $A$ = Frontal (projected) area **[m²]**

The constant $\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ is the **dynamic pressure** of the free stream and is the kinetic energy per unit volume of the flowing fluid.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic directly addresses **CO2** — *"Apply the principles of fluid flow to analyze drag, boundary layers, and flow through conduits."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Cyclist in the Wind"

Imagine you are cycling on a highway. On a calm day, pedaling feels manageable. But when a truck passes you or when the wind blows against you, you feel a strong **resistive force pushing you backward**. That force is **drag**.

- If the wind is **smooth and steady**, most of the resistance comes from the **fluid "sticking"** to your body and clothes — this is **skin friction drag**.
- If you are wearing a loose raincoat that **flaps and billows**, the low pressure created behind you **sucks you backward** — this is **form (pressure) drag**.
- A **shaped helmet and tight jersey** (like pro cyclists use) dramatically reduce both — proving that drag is a function of **shape and surface roughness**.

## 1.3 Classification of Drag — The Five Major Types

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Classification Table for KTU Exams:**

| # | Type of Drag | Physical Origin | When Dominant |
|---|--------------|-----------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Skin Friction Drag** (Viscous Drag) | Tangential viscous shear stresses in the boundary layer | Slender, streamlined bodies; laminar flow |
| 2 | **Form Drag** (Pressure Drag) | Pressure difference between front and rear stagnation regions | Blunt bodies; flow separation |
| 3 | **Profile Drag** | Sum of Skin Friction + Form Drag | All bodies in viscous flow |
| 4 | **Wave Drag** | Shock waves and energy radiation | Supersonic flow (M > 1) |
| 5 | **Induced Drag** | Lift generation creates vortices at wingtips | Bodies producing lift (airfoils) |

> [!NOTE]
> For **chemical & process engineering** (which is the focus of PCBBT403), the **two most relevant drag types** are **Skin Friction Drag** and **Form Drag**, since most process equipment (pipes, tanks, cyclones, filters) operates in the **incompressible, subsonic regime**.

## 1.4 The Boundary Layer — Prandtl's Revolutionary Concept (1904)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BOUNDARY LAYER:** A thin region of fluid adjacent to a solid surface where the effects of **viscosity** are significant and the fluid velocity varies from zero (at the wall, no-slip condition) to the free-stream value $V_\infty$.

**Ludwig Prandtl (1904)** recognized that for a fluid with very low viscosity (like air and water), the viscous effects are confined to a *very thin layer* near the solid surface. Outside this layer, the fluid behaves as an **inviscid (frictionless) flow**.

### 1.4.1 Boundary Layer Formation — Step-by-Step Intuition

1. **Fluid approaches** a flat plate with uniform velocity $V_\infty$.
2. At the **leading edge** (x = 0), the fluid "touches" the plate → **no-slip condition**: velocity = 0.
3. As fluid moves along the plate, viscous diffusion transports the **retarded fluid** layer by layer into the bulk flow.
4. A **velocity profile** develops — from 0 at the wall to $V_\infty$ at the edge of the boundary layer.
5. The thickness $\delta(x)$ **grows** as we move downstream (x increases).

The conventional definition of boundary layer thickness is:

$$\delta(x) = \text{distance from the wall where } u = 0.99 \, V_\infty$$

## 1.5 Boundary Layer in Pipe Flow — Entrance Region

When fluid enters a pipe from a reservoir, it undergoes a similar process:

- **At the entrance** (x = 0): Velocity profile is nearly **uniform** (plug flow).
- As fluid proceeds: The **no-slip condition at the wall** creates a thin boundary layer on the inner pipe surface.
- These boundary layers **grow inward** from the pipe wall.
- At a certain distance $L_e$ (entrance length), the boundary layers **merge at the pipe centerline**.
- Beyond $L_e$: The flow becomes **fully developed** with a fixed parabolic (laminar) or flatter (turbulent) profile.

$$L_e = 0.06 \cdot Re \cdot D \quad \text{(laminar)}$$

$$L_e = 4.4 \cdot D \quad \text{(turbulent — independent of Re)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Insight:** In the **entrance region**, the flow is **developing** and pressure drop is **higher** than in fully developed flow. This is critical in designing heat exchangers, reactors, and short process pipes.

## 1.6 Real-World Industrial Relevance

| Engineering Application | Role of Drag / Boundary Layer |
|--------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Cyclone separators** | Form drag separates particles from gas |
| **Filtration** | Pressure drop across filter media = form + friction drag |
| **Packed bed reactors** | Drag on catalyst particles determines pressure drop (Ergun equation) |
| **Aeration tanks** | Skin friction on bubbles affects oxygen transfer |
| **Heat exchangers** | Boundary layer thickness controls thermal resistance |
| **Settling tanks** | Drag force balances gravity for terminal settling velocity |
