# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Drag Types

### 2.1.1 Skin Friction Drag (Viscous Drag)

**Origin:** Tangential shear stress $\tau_w$ acting on the wetted surface of the body due to fluid viscosity.

**Mechanism:**
- The fluid layer adjacent to the wall is stationary (no-slip).
- Adjacent fluid layers slip past each other with a velocity gradient.
- Newton's law of viscosity: $\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$.
- The wall shear stress $\tau_w$ integrated over the wetted area gives the **skin friction drag**.

$$F_{D,\text{skin}} = \int_{A_w} \tau_w \, dA$$

### 2.1.2 Form Drag (Pressure Drag)

**Origin:** Asymmetric pressure distribution created when the flow **separates** from the body.

**Mechanism — Why It Happens:**
1. Fluid approaches the body's leading edge and **stagnates** (kinetic energy → pressure energy).
2. Pressure is **highest** at the front stagnation point.
3. As fluid moves along the body, a **boundary layer** forms.
4. If the body is **blunt**, the boundary layer **separates** before reaching the rear.
5. A **low-pressure wake** forms behind the body.
6. The pressure difference between the **high-pressure front** and **low-pressure rear** → **net rearward force = form drag**.

> [!TIP]
> **Streamlining reduces form drag** by delaying flow separation. That's why modern cars, submarines, and airplane fuselages have **teardrop shapes**.

### 2.1.3 Profile Drag

For **all bodies** in a viscous fluid:

$$F_{D,\text{profile}} = F_{D,\text{skin}} + F_{D,\text{form}}$$

For a **streamlined body** (e.g., airfoil at small angle of attack), skin friction dominates (~80–90%).
For a **blunt body** (e.g., sphere, disk), form drag dominates (~90%).

## 2.2 Drag Coefficient — Detailed Analysis

The drag coefficient is **NOT a constant** — it depends on the **Reynolds number**, the body shape, and the surface roughness.

$$C_D = \frac{F_D}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 A}$$

**For a sphere** (one of the most studied cases in chemical engineering):
- $Re < 1$ (Stokes regime): $C_D = \frac{24}{Re}$ — **Stokes' Law**
- $1 < Re < 1000$ (Intermediate): $C_D \approx \frac{24}{Re}(1 + 0.15 Re^{0.687})$ — **Schiller-Naumann correlation**
- $1000 < Re < 2 \times 10^5$ (Newton's regime): $C_D \approx 0.44$ (nearly constant)
- $Re > 2 \times 10^5$ (Supercritical): $C_D$ drops sharply due to **boundary layer transition** to turbulent.

## 2.3 Boundary Layer — Quantitative Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU examiner expects you to **define** and **draw** the following four thicknesses. Memorize these — they appear every year.

### 2.3.1 Boundary Layer Thickness ($\delta$)
- **Definition:** Distance from the wall where velocity reaches **99%** of free-stream velocity.
- $u(y = \delta) = 0.99 V_\infty$

### 2.3.2 Displacement Thickness ($\delta^*$)
- **Definition:** The distance by which the **outer flow is displaced** outward due to the slow-moving boundary layer.
- Represents the **mass flow deficit** caused by the boundary layer.

$$\delta^* = \int_0^\delta \left(1 - \frac{u}{V_\infty}\right) dy$$

### 2.3.3 Momentum Thickness ($\theta$)
- **Definition:** Represents the **loss of momentum flux** due to the boundary layer.
- Critical for the **von Kármán Momentum Integral Equation**.

$$\theta = \int_0^\delta \frac{u}{V_\infty}\left(1 - \frac{u}{V_\infty}\right) dy$$

### 2.3.4 Energy Thickness ($\delta^{**}$)
- **Definition:** Represents the **kinetic energy deficit** of the boundary layer.

$$\delta^{**} = \int_0^\delta \frac{u}{V_\infty}\left(1 - \left(\frac{u}{V_\infty}\right)^2\right) dy$$

**Order of magnitude:** $\delta > \delta^* > \theta > \delta^{**}$

| 2 | $C_D = \frac{24}{Re}$ | Stokes drag (sphere, $Re<1$) | — | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

| 5 | $C_f = \frac{\tau_w}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2_\infty}$ | Skin friction coefficient | — | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters

- **Packed columns & fluidized beds:** Drag on individual particles is the basis of the **Ergun equation** and **terminal velocity** calculations.
- **Sedimentation:** Stokes' law gives the settling velocity of fine particles in a viscous fluid.
- **Pipe networks:** Entrance length $L_e$ determines whether the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** is valid.
- **Heat transfer:** Boundary layer thickness $\delta$ directly controls the **convective heat transfer coefficient** $h$.
- **Filtration & membrane separation:** Form drag on filter media dictates **pressure drop** and **energy cost**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Universal Drag Force from Dimensional Analysis

We wish to find how drag $F_D$ depends on the relevant physical parameters for a body of characteristic length $L$ moving through a fluid.

**Step 1: List all relevant variables.**
The drag force depends on:
- Fluid density: $\rho$ [kg/m³]
- Fluid viscosity: $\mu$ [Pa·s]
- Velocity: $V$ [m/s]
- Characteristic length: $L$ [m]

$$F_D = f(\rho, \mu, V, L)$$

**Step 2: Apply Buckingham Pi Theorem.**
There are 5 variables and 3 fundamental dimensions (M, L, T), so we get $5 - 3 = 2$ dimensionless groups.

**Step 3: Form the Pi groups.**

$$\Pi_1 = \frac{F_D}{\rho V^2 L^2} = C_D \quad \text{(Drag Coefficient)}$$

$$\Pi_2 = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = Re \quad \text{(Reynolds Number)}$$

**Step 4: Functional relationship.**

$$C_D = f(Re)$$

**Step 5: Reconstruct the drag force.**

$$F_D = C_D \cdot \rho V^2 L^2$$

Using a frontal area $A \propto L^2$, we obtain the standard form:

$$\boxed{F_D = C_D \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A}$$

**Why the factor $\frac{1}{2}$?**
The $\frac{1}{2}$ is conventional and arises because the **dynamic pressure** is defined as $\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ (this is the kinetic energy per unit volume of the flow). Using this convention, $C_D$ for a **flat plate perpendicular to flow** is exactly **1.18**, and for a **sphere at $Re = 10^5$** it is approximately **0.5** — values students must remember.

## 3.2 Derivation: Boundary Layer Thickness on a Flat Plate (Blasius Solution, 1908)

For a **laminar**, steady, incompressible flow over a **flat plate**, Prandtl's boundary layer equations reduce to the **Blasius equation**.

### Step 1: Governing Equations
Continuity and x-momentum in 2D steady flow:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

$$u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \nu\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}$$

### Step 2: Boundary Conditions
- No-slip at wall: $u(x, 0) = 0$
- No penetration: $v(x, 0) = 0$
- Free-stream match: $u(x, \infty) = V_\infty$

### Step 3: Similarity Transformation (Blasius)
Introduce the **similarity variable**:

$$\eta = y \sqrt{\frac{V_\infty}{\nu x}}$$

And the **stream function**:

$$\psi = \sqrt{\nu V_\infty x} \cdot f(\eta)$$

The PDEs reduce to a single **ODE (Blasius Equation)**:

$$f'''(\eta) + \frac{1}{2}f(\eta)f''(\eta) = 0$$

### Step 4: Boundary Conditions in $\eta$-Space

$$f(0) = 0, \quad f'(0) = 0, \quad f'(\infty) = 1$$

### Step 5: Numerical Solution
Blasius solved this ODE numerically. The wall shear stress is:

$$\tau_w = 0.332 \cdot \rho V_\infty^2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\nu}{V_\infty x}} = \frac{0.664 \,\rho V_\infty^2}{\sqrt{Re_x}}$$

### Step 6: Boundary Layer Thickness

$$\delta(x) \approx \frac{5x}{\sqrt{Re_x}}$$

(where $Re_x = \frac{V_\infty x}{\nu}$)

**Physical interpretation:** $\delta$ grows as $\sqrt{x}$ because viscous diffusion spreads at a rate $\sim \sqrt{\nu t}$ where $t \sim x/V_\infty$.

## 3.3 Derivation: von Kármán Momentum Integral Equation

This is the **most powerful tool** for engineering boundary layer analysis. It does not require the full velocity profile.

### Step 1: Apply Conservation of Momentum to a Control Volume
Consider a control volume of unit depth, extending from $x$ to $x + dx$ and from the wall ($y=0$) to the free stream ($y=\delta$).

**Mass flow in** at $x$: $\dot{m}_x = \rho \int_0^\delta u \, dy$
**Mass flow out** at $x+dx$: $\dot{m}_{x+dx} = \rho \int_0^\delta u \, dy + \rho \frac{d}{dx}\left(\int_0^\delta u \, dy\right) dx$

The net outflow of momentum in the x-direction is:

$$d(\dot{M}) = \rho \frac{d}{dx}\left(\int_0^\delta u^2 \, dy\right) dx$$

### Step 2: Apply Newton's Second Law
The forces on the CV are:
- Pressure force at the outer edge (often zero for flat plate): $F_p$
- Wall shear stress on the bottom: $F_\tau = -\tau_w \, dx$
- Momentum flux out minus in: $d(\dot{M})$

$$F_p - \tau_w \, dx = \rho \frac{d}{dx}\left(\int_0^\delta u^2 \, dy\right) dx$$

### Step 3: Rearrange for a Flat Plate ($F_p = 0$)
For a flat plate, the pressure is constant and uniform, so $F_p = 0$:

$$-\tau_w = \rho \frac{d}{dx}\left(\int_0^\delta u^2 \, dy\right)$$

### Step 4: Introduce Momentum Thickness $\theta$

$$\theta = \int_0^\delta \frac{u}{V_\infty}\left(1 - \frac{u}{V_\infty}\right) dy$$

Expanding and using Leibniz rule:

$$\boxed{\frac{\tau_w}{\rho V_\infty^2} = \frac{d\theta}{dx}}$$

**This is the von Kármán Momentum Integral Equation.**

### Step 5: Solve for Skin Friction Coefficient

$$C_f = \frac{\tau_w}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2} = 2\frac{d\theta}{dx}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Drag on a Flat Plate from Momentum Thickness

For a flat plate of length $L$ and width $b$:

$$F_D = \int_0^L \tau_w \cdot b \, dx = b \int_0^L \rho V_\infty^2 \frac{d\theta}{dx} dx = b \, \rho V_\infty^2 \theta(L)$$

The **average skin friction coefficient** is:

$$\bar{C}_f = \frac{F_D}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 \cdot b L} = \frac{2\theta(L)}{L}$$

For the **Blasius (exact) solution**:

$$\bar{C}_f = \frac{1.328}{\sqrt{Re_L}}$$

This is the **canonical result** KTU expects you to derive or quote.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Drag & Boundary Layer — Quick Reference

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick comparison table — perfect for last-minute revision:**

| Regime | $C_D$ for Sphere | $\delta(x)$ on Flat Plate | $C_f$ on Flat Plate | Reynolds Range |
|--------|------------------|---------------------------|---------------------|----------------|
| Stokes (creeping) | $\frac{24}{Re}$ | N/A | N/A | $Re < 1$ |
| Laminar BL | $\sim \frac{24}{Re}$ | $\frac{5x}{\sqrt{Re_x}}$ | $\frac{0.664}{\sqrt{Re_x}}$ | $Re_x < 5 \times 10^5$ |
| Turbulent BL | ~0.4–0.5 (const.) | $\frac{0.37 x}{Re_x^{1/5}}$ | $\frac{0.074}{Re_x^{1/5}}$ | $Re_x > 5 \times 10^5$ |
| Pipe Laminar | 64/Re (friction factor) | grows inward | $C_f = 16/Re$ | $Re < 2300$ |
| Pipe Turbulent | 0.316/Re^{0.25} | thin BL near wall | Blasius: $\frac{0.079}{Re^{0.25}}$ | $Re > 4000$ |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the following boundary layer parameters: (i) Boundary layer thickness $\delta$, (ii) Displacement thickness $\delta^*$, (iii) Momentum thickness $\theta$.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Boundary Layer Thickness ($\delta$):** It is defined as the distance from the solid surface (wall) measured in the y-direction at which the fluid velocity reaches **99% of the free-stream velocity** $V_\infty$. Mathematically: $u(y = \delta) = 0.99 \, V_\infty$.

**(ii) Displacement Thickness ($\delta^*$):** It represents the **distance by which the external inviscid flow is displaced outward** from the wall due to the slowing of fluid inside the boundary layer. It is the measure of the **mass flow rate deficit**:

$$\delta^* = \int_0^\delta \left(1 - \frac{u}{V_\infty}\right) dy$$

**(iii) Momentum Thickness ($\theta$):** It is a measure of the **loss of momentum flux** per unit width of the boundary layer compared to an inviscid flow:

$$\theta = \int_0^\delta \frac{u}{V_\infty}\left(1 - \frac{u}{V_\infty}\right) dy$$

**Typical values for laminar BL on flat plate:** $\delta^* \approx \delta/3$ and $\theta \approx \delta/7$.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish clearly between Skin Friction Drag and Form Drag. Which is dominant for a sphere and for a streamlined body?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Skin Friction Drag | Form Drag (Pressure Drag) |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------------|
| **Origin** | Tangential viscous shear stress $\tau_w$ | Pressure difference across the body |
| **Mechanism** | Viscosity in the boundary layer | Flow separation creating low-pressure wake |
| **Magnitude** | Proportional to wetted area | Proportional to frontal area |
| **Reynolds number effect** | $C_D \propto 1/\sqrt{Re}$ (laminar) | Weakly dependent on $Re$ |
| **Dominant in** | Streamlined bodies, low $Re$ | Blunt bodies, high $Re$ |

**For a Sphere (blunt body):** **Form Drag** is dominant (≈ 90% of total drag), because flow separates easily and creates a large low-pressure wake.

**For a Streamlined Body (e.g., airfoil):** **Skin Friction Drag** is dominant (≈ 80–90% of total drag), because the streamlined shape delays flow separation, minimizing wake size.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Rule:** Students must answer **ONE full question of 14 marks** with sub-parts. We provide **TWO alternative full questions** (Question A and Question B) following the KTU 2024 internal-choice pattern.

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the formation and growth of the boundary layer over a flat plate. Define boundary layer thickness, displacement thickness, and momentum thickness. Also state the Blasius solution for laminar boundary layer thickness.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Air at 20 °C flows over a flat plate of length 2 m at a velocity of 3 m/s. Calculate: (i) The boundary layer thickness at the trailing edge. (ii) The total drag force per unit width on one side of the plate. (iii) The average skin friction coefficient.

**Given:** $\rho_{air} = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu_{air} = 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s}$.

#### **Solution to Part (a):**

**Sketch:** (Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid Diagram and describe verbally in exam)

- Draw a flat plate horizontal with leading edge at $x = 0$.
- Show uniform velocity $V_\infty$ approaching from the left.
- At the leading edge, velocity profile is uniform.
- Downstream, draw a velocity profile starting from $u = 0$ at the wall, gradually increasing to $u = 0.99 V_\infty$ at $y = \delta(x)$.
- Show $\delta$ growing as we move downstream.
- Label: leading edge, trailing edge, free stream, boundary layer, $\delta(x)$, $\tau_w$.

**Boundary Layer Thickness ($\delta$):** The distance from the wall where $u = 0.99 V_\infty$.

**Displacement Thickness ($\delta^*$):** As given in Section 3 above — represents mass flow deficit.

**Momentum Thickness ($\theta$):** Represents loss of momentum flux.

**Blasius Solution (1908) for laminar boundary layer:**

$$\delta(x) = \frac{5.0 \, x}{\sqrt{Re_x}}$$

where $Re_x = \frac{V_\infty x}{\nu}$

**Wall shear stress:**

$$\tau_w = \frac{0.664 \, \rho V_\infty^2}{\sqrt{Re_x}}$$

[7 Marks Model Answer - includes sketch description, definitions, and formula]

#### **Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $L = 2$ m
- $V_\infty = 3$ m/s
- $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³
- $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s
- $\nu = \mu/\rho = 1.508 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s

**Step 1: Calculate Reynolds number at trailing edge.**

$$Re_L = \frac{V_\infty L}{\nu} = \frac{3 \times 2}{1.508 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.978 \times 10^5$$

**Step 2: Check regime.**
$Re_L = 3.978 \times 10^5 < 5 \times 10^5$ → **Laminar** ✓ (Blasius solution is valid)

**Step 3: Calculate boundary layer thickness at trailing edge.**

$$\delta(L) = \frac{5.0 \, L}{\sqrt{Re_L}} = \frac{5.0 \times 2}{\sqrt{3.978 \times 10^5}}$$

$$\delta(L) = \frac{10.0}{630.7} = 1.586 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} = 15.86 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Calculate average skin friction coefficient (Blasius).**

$$\bar{C}_f = \frac{1.328}{\sqrt{Re_L}} = \frac{1.328}{\sqrt{3.978 \times 10^5}} = \frac{1.328}{630.7} = 2.105 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 5: Calculate total drag force per unit width.**

$$F_D = \bar{C}_f \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^2 \cdot L \cdot (1 \text{ m width})$$

$$F_D = 2.105 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 3^2 \times 2 \times 1$$

$$F_D = 2.105 \times 10^{-3} \times 10.8 = 2.273 \times 10^{-2} \text{ N/m}$$

$$\boxed{\delta(L) = 15.86 \text{ mm}, \quad \bar{C}_f = 2.105 \times 10^{-3}, \quad F_D = 0.0227 \text{ N/m}}$$

---

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define drag coefficient. Derive the universal drag force equation $F_D = C_D \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A$ using dimensional analysis (Buckingham Pi theorem). State Stokes' law and its range of validity.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A sphere of diameter 2 mm is dropped into glycerin at 25 °C. Determine the terminal velocity of the sphere.

**Given:** Density of sphere $\rho_p = 2500$ kg/m³, Density of glycerin $\rho = 1260$ kg/m³, Viscosity of glycerin $\mu = 1.49$ kg/(m·s), $g = 9.81$ m/s².

#### **Solution to Part (a):**

**Definition:** Drag coefficient is a **dimensionless quantity** that quantifies the drag or resistance of a body in a fluid environment. It is defined as:

$$C_D = \frac{F_D}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A}$$

**Dimensional Analysis (Buckingham Pi Theorem):**

Variables: $F_D, \rho, \mu, V, L$ → 5 variables, 3 dimensions (M, L, T) → 2 Pi groups.

- $\Pi_1 = F_D / (\rho V^2 L^2)$
- $\Pi_2 = \rho V L / \mu = Re$

Therefore: $C_D = f(Re)$ and $F_D = C_D \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A$ ✓

**Stokes' Law:** For a sphere in the **creeping flow regime** ($Re < 1$):

$$F_D = 3\pi \mu V d \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad C_D = \frac{24}{Re}$$

**Range of validity:** $Re < 1$, sphere must be **rigid, smooth, and isolated** (no wall effects).

[7 Marks — full derivation with all steps]

#### **Solution to Part (b):**

**Strategy:** Assume Stokes regime, check Re, iterate if needed.

**Step 1: Force balance at terminal velocity.**
At terminal velocity $V_t$, drag force = buoyancy-corrected weight:

$$F_D = (\rho_p - \rho) \cdot g \cdot V_{sphere}$$

$$3\pi \mu V_t d = (\rho_p - \rho) \cdot g \cdot \frac{\pi d^3}{6}$$

**Step 2: Solve for $V_t$.**

$$V_t = \frac{(\rho_p - \rho) g d^2}{18 \mu}$$

**Step 3: Substitute numerical values.**

$$V_t = \frac{(2500 - 1260) \times 9.81 \times (2 \times 10^{-3})^2}{18 \times 1.49}$$

$$V_t = \frac{1240 \times 9.81 \times 4 \times 10^{-6}}{26.82}$$

$$V_t = \frac{0.04866}{26.82} = 1.815 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s} = 1.815 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Step 4: Verify Stokes regime.**

$$Re = \frac{\rho V_t d}{\mu} = \frac{1260 \times 1.815 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}{1.49}$$

$$Re = \frac{4.574 \times 10^{-3}}{1.49} = 3.07 \times 10^{-3}$$

$Re \ll 1$ ✓ **Stokes law is valid!**

$$\boxed{V_t = 1.815 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s} = 1.815 \text{ mm/s}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS — Read Carefully!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of $\frac{1}{2}$** in $F_D = \frac{1}{2} C_D \rho V^2 A$ — KTU examiner deducts **1 mark** instantly.
>
> 2. **Wrong area in drag equation:** Use **frontal area $A$** (projected), NOT wetted area — for spheres, $A = \pi d^2/4$.
>
> 3. **Forgetting buoyancy correction:** When a sphere falls in a liquid, always use $(\rho_p - \rho)$, not just $\rho_p$ — losing 2 marks here is the **#1 student error**.
>
> 4. **Applying Stokes' Law beyond its range:** Always **check $Re < 1$** at the end. If violated, you must use **Schiller-Naumann** or **Newton's regime** correlation and **iterate** until Re converges.
>
> 5. **Mixing up $\delta$, $\delta^*$, and $\theta$:** $\delta$ is the *physical* thickness, $\delta^*$ is the *displacement* (mass deficit), $\theta$ is the *momentum* loss. **Drawing them on the velocity profile** in the exam gets you **2 extra marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Read this 1 hour before the exam!**

### 🔹 Core Concepts
- **Drag** = resistive force from fluid on a body; always opposes motion.
- **Universal drag equation:** $F_D = C_D \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 A$.
- **Drag coefficient** $C_D$ depends on $Re$, shape, and roughness.

### 🔹 Five Types of Drag (Memorize the Table)
1. **Skin Friction Drag** → viscosity, shear stress → streamlined bodies
2. **Form Drag** → pressure difference, flow separation → blunt bodies
3. **Profile Drag** = Skin + Form (universal for viscous flow)
4. **Wave Drag** → supersonic flow (M > 1)
5. **Induced Drag** → bodies with lift

### 🔹 Boundary Layer — Must Know
- Prandtl (1904) — viscosity confined to thin layer near wall.
- $\delta(x) = 5x/\sqrt{Re_x}$ (Blasius, laminar)
- $\bar{C}_f = 1.328/\sqrt{Re_L}$ (laminar, flat plate)
- $L_e = 0.06 \cdot Re \cdot D$ (pipe, laminar)
- $L_e \approx 4.4 D$ (pipe, turbulent)

### 🔹 Stokes' Law (CRITICAL for PCBBT403)
- $C_D = 24/Re$ for $Re < 1$
- Terminal velocity: $V_t = \frac{(\rho_p - \rho) g d^2}{18 \mu}$
- Used for: **sedimentation, settling, particle classification**

### 🔹 Pipe Flow vs Flat Plate BL
- **Flat plate:** BL grows from the leading edge, thickness $\delta(x) \propto \sqrt{x}$.
- **Pipe:** BL grows from pipe wall inward; **merges at centerline** at $x = L_e$; **fully developed** beyond $L_e$.

### 🔹 Numerical Constants to Memorize
- Blasius $\delta$ coefficient: **5.0**
- Blasius $\bar{C}_f$ coefficient: **1.328**
- Blasius local $C_f$ coefficient: **0.664**
- Turbulent flat plate: $\delta/x = 0.37/Re_x^{1/5}$, $C_f = 0.074/Re_x^{1/5}$
- Laminar-to-turbulent transition: $Re_x \approx 5 \times 10^5$

### 🔹 Drawing Skills (Practice These for Full Marks)
- Velocity profile over flat plate — label $\delta$, $\delta^*$, $\theta$.
- Boundary layer development in pipe — show entrance region, fully developed region.
- Streamlined vs blunt body — show separation point and wake.

> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Boundary Layer Separation and Wake Formation

### Formal Definition
A **boundary layer** is the thin region of fluid adjacent to a solid surface where viscous effects are significant and the velocity varies from zero at the wall (no-slip condition) to the free-stream velocity. **Boundary layer separation** occurs when the fluid adjacent to the surface decelerates against an **adverse pressure gradient** (pressure increasing in the direction of flow) until its kinetic energy is insufficient to overcome the rising pressure, causing the flow to reverse direction and detach from the surface. The region of disturbed, recirculating fluid downstream of the separation point is called the **wake**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Hill Analogy"
Imagine a cyclist rolling up a gentle hill (adverse pressure gradient). If the cyclist pedals fast enough, momentum carries them over the crest. If the slope becomes too steep relative to their speed, they stop, roll backward, and a chaotic region of back-and-forth motion forms behind them. The "stopping point" is the **separation point**, and the chaotic backflow region is the **wake**. In fluid flow, the role of the cyclist's inertia is played by the fluid's kinetic energy, and the "hill" is the pressure rise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Separation occurs only when $\frac{dp}{dx} > 0$ (adverse gradient). Favorable pressure gradients ($\frac{dp}{dx} < 0$) accelerate fluid and prevent separation.

### Real Consequences
- **Bluff bodies** (cylinders, spheres at high Re) shed alternating vortices (von Kármán vortex street).
- **Streamlined bodies** (airfoils) delay separation, reducing form drag.
- Separation causes **pressure drag (form drag)** — dominant in turbulent flow past blunt objects.

---

## 1.2 Flow Through Pipe — Bernoulli Equation

### Formal Definition
The **Bernoulli Equation** for steady, incompressible, inviscid flow along a streamline is a statement of mechanical energy conservation:

$$
\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2
$$

For real (viscous) flow through pipes, mechanical energy is lost to friction, and the equation becomes a **modified mechanical energy balance**:

$$
\frac{p_1}{\rho} + \frac{\alpha_1 v_1^2}{2} + g z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho} + \frac{\alpha_2 v_2^2}{2} + g z_2 + \sum F
$$

where $\alpha$ is the **kinetic energy correction factor** and $\sum F$ represents frictional losses.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Waterfall Energy Budget"
Think of water flowing in a pipe from a higher tank to a lower one. The total energy at the top equals the total energy at the bottom **minus the energy lost to friction** (heat, turbulence) **plus energy added by pumps**. Bernoulli's equation is simply a ledger of this energy transfer, expressed per unit weight (or per unit mass) of fluid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three forms of Bernoulli's equation** (per unit weight, mass, volume) are used in KTU problems — know the conversion factors by heart.

---

## 1.3 Correction Factors in Bernoulli Equation

### Kinetic Energy Correction Factor ($\alpha$)
The factor $\alpha$ (also called the **Coriolis coefficient** for kinetic energy) accounts for the fact that, in real pipe flow, the velocity profile is **non-uniform** (parabolic in laminar flow, flatter in turbulent flow). The actual kinetic energy flux is greater than the $\frac{1}{2}\rho v_{avg}^2$ estimate:

$$
\alpha = \frac{1}{A} \int_A \left(\frac{v}{v_{avg}}\right)^3 \, dA
$$

- **Laminar flow**: $\alpha = 2.0$
- **Turbulent flow**: $\alpha \approx 1.05$ to $1.10$ (often taken as **1.0** for engineering work)

### Momentum Correction Factor ($\beta$)
The factor $\beta$ corrects the momentum flux for non-uniform velocity profiles:

$$
\beta = \frac{1}{A} \int_A \left(\frac{v}{v_{avg}}\right)^2 \, dA
$$

- **Laminar flow**: $\beta = 4/3$
- **Turbulent flow**: $\beta \approx 1.02$ to $1.05$ (often taken as **1.0**)

---

## 1.4 Pump Work

### Formal Definition
**Pump work** ($W_p$ or $W_s$) is the mechanical energy **added to the fluid** by a pump to overcome elevation head, friction losses, and to maintain the desired kinetic energy. It is a positive term added to the upstream energy balance:

$$
\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + H_p = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_f
$$

where $H_p$ is the **pump head** (energy added per unit weight of fluid).

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Heart of the System"
A pump is the "heart" of a piping system. Just as the heart adds energy to blood to push it through arteries (against gravity and friction), a pump adds energy to fluid to push it through the process equipment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pump Power Equation:** $P = \rho g Q H_p / \eta$, where $\eta$ is pump efficiency. KTU problems frequently ask you to calculate brake horsepower.

---

## 1.5 Pressure Losses — Straight Pipes and Fittings

### Straight Pipe Losses
For fully developed flow in a straight pipe, the **Darcy–Weisbach equation** gives the head loss:

$$
h_f = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{v^2}{2g}
$$

where $f$ is the **Darcy friction factor** (4× the Fanning friction factor), $L$ is pipe length, and $D$ is inside diameter.

### Fittings and Accessories Losses
Losses in valves, elbows, tees, etc., are computed by two equivalent methods:

1. **Equivalent Length Method:** $h_f = f \frac{L_e}{D} \frac{v^2}{2g}$ — add a fictitious length $L_e$ to the actual pipe length.
2. **Loss Coefficient (K) Method:** $h_f = K \frac{v^2}{2g}$ — every fitting has a tabulated $K$ value.

---

## 1.6 Schedule Number of Pipes

### Formal Definition
The **Schedule Number (Sch)** is a dimensionless number that indicates the **wall thickness** of a pipe for a given nominal diameter. It is defined as:

$$
\text{Schedule Number} = \frac{P \cdot S}{S_e}
$$

where $P$ is the design pressure, $S$ is the allowable stress, and $S_e$ is the stress for a thin-wall reference pipe.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Schedules:** Sch 5, 10, 20, 40 (standard), 80 (extra strong/XS), 120, 160, and Sch XXS (double extra strong). **Higher schedule = thicker wall = higher pressure rating.**

---

## 1.7 Equivalent Diameter

### Formal Definition
For **non-circular ducts** (annuli, rectangular ducts, etc.), the **equivalent (hydraulic) diameter** $D_e$ (or $D_h$) is defined as **four times the cross-sectional area divided by the wetted perimeter**:

$$
D_e = \frac{4 A_c}{P_w}
$$

This allows the use of circular-pipe correlations (Reynolds number, friction factor) for non-circular geometries.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine you have a square duct and a circular duct carrying the same mass flow rate at the same pressure drop. The equivalent diameter answers the question: "What circular pipe diameter would behave identically to this square duct?"

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Boundary Layer Separation — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Boundary Layer Formation:** As fluid approaches a solid surface, viscous shear creates a velocity gradient. The layer where $0 \le v \le 0.99 v_\infty$ is the boundary layer of thickness $\delta$.
2. **Adverse Pressure Gradient:** When the surface curves away from the flow (e.g., rear of a cylinder) or the duct diverges, static pressure **rises** in the flow direction ($\frac{dp}{dx} > 0$).
3. **Deceleration:** Within the boundary layer, fluid particles are slowed by both viscosity and the adverse gradient.
4. **Velocity Reversal:** Near the wall, fluid eventually has zero velocity, then reverses — this is the **separation point** (where $\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial y}\right)_{y=0} = 0$).
5. **Wake Formation:** Downstream of separation, the flow is chaotic, with recirculation eddies. This turbulent region is the **wake**, which exerts a lower-than-expected pressure on the body's rear face → **pressure drag**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Layer Thickness Definitions (must know for KTU):**
> - $\delta$ — total boundary layer thickness (99% of $v_\infty$)
> - $\delta^*$ — **displacement thickness** (mass flow deficit)
> - $\theta$ — **momentum thickness**
> - $\delta^{**}$ — **energy thickness**

---

## 2.2 Derivation of Bernoulli Equation (Euler's Approach)

Starting from Euler's equation of motion along a streamline for inviscid, steady, incompressible flow:

1. **Euler's equation** (inviscid, no body forces initially):

$$
\rho \frac{Dv}{Dt} = -\nabla p
$$

2. **Along a streamline**, the convective acceleration becomes $v \frac{dv}{ds}$, and $\nabla p$ projected along the streamline is $\frac{dp}{ds}$:

$$
\rho v \frac{dv}{ds} = -\frac{dp}{ds}
$$

3. **Integrate** along the streamline from point 1 to point 2:

$$
\int_1^2 \rho v \, dv = -\int_1^2 dp \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2 - \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 = -(p_2 - p_1)
$$

4. **Add gravity** (body force term $gz$):

$$
\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2
$$

---

## 2.3 Modified Bernoulli Equation (Mechanical Energy Balance)

For real flow, multiply kinetic energy by $\alpha$ and add pump work $W_p$ and friction loss $h_f$:

$$
\frac{p_1}{\rho} + \frac{\alpha_1 \bar{v}_1^2}{2} + g z_1 + W_p = \frac{p_2}{\rho} + \frac{\alpha_2 \bar{v}_2^2}{2} + g z_2 + h_f
$$

Expressed per unit **weight** of fluid (in head form):

$$
\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \alpha_1 \frac{\bar{v}_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + H_p = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \alpha_2 \frac{\bar{v}_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_f
$$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | $\delta(x) \approx \frac{5x}{\sqrt{Re_x}}$ | Boundary layer thickness (turbulent, flat plate) |
| 2 | $\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{v^2}{2g} + z = \text{const}$ | Bernoulli equation (ideal, head form) |
| 3 | $\frac{p}{\rho} + \frac{\alpha v^2}{2} + gz + W_p = \frac{p}{\rho} + \frac{\alpha v^2}{2} + gz + h_f$ | Modified Bernoulli (real flow) |
| 4 | $\alpha = \frac{1}{A}\int_A (v/\bar v)^3 \, dA$ | Kinetic energy correction factor |
| 5 | $\beta = \frac{1}{A}\int_A (v/\bar v)^2 \, dA$ | Momentum correction factor |
| 6 | $h_f = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{v^2}{2g}$ | Darcy–Weisbach equation (straight pipe) |
| 7 | $h_f = K \frac{v^2}{2g}$ | Loss coefficient (K) method for fittings |
| 8 | $h_f = f \frac{L_e}{D} \frac{v^2}{2g}$ | Equivalent length $L_e$ method |
| 9 | $P = \rho g Q H_p / \eta$ | Pump power (brake power) |
| 10 | $D_e = 4A_c / P_w$ | Equivalent (hydraulic) diameter |
| 11 | $Re = \rho v D / \mu = v D / \nu$ | Reynolds number (circular pipe) |
| 12 | $Re_e = \rho v D_e / \mu$ | Reynolds number (non-circular duct) |
| 13 | $\text{Schedule No.} = 1000 \cdot P / S$ | Schedule number definition |
| 14 | $\tau_w = \frac{f}{4} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Wall shear stress (from Darcy $f$) |
| 15 | $P_{pump} = Q \cdot \Delta p / \eta$ | Pump power (pressure form) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:**
> - **Bernoulli:** Venturi meters, pitot tubes, airplane wing lift.
> - **Equivalent Diameter:** HVAC duct design, heat exchanger optimization.
> - **Schedule Number:** Selection of process piping in oil refineries, chemical plants.
> - **Correction Factors:** Accurate energy audits in chemical process industries (CPI).

---

## 2.5 Detailed Look at Equivalent Diameter Geometries

| Geometry | Area $A_c$ | Wetted Perimeter $P_w$ | Equivalent Diameter $D_e$ |
|----------|-----------|-----------------------|---------------------------|
| Square (side $a$) | $a^2$ | $4a$ | $a$ |
| Rectangle ($a \times b$) | $ab$ | $2(a+b)$ | $\frac{2ab}{a+b}$ |
| Annulus (D, d) | $\frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$ | $\pi(D+d)$ | $D - d$ |
| Equilateral triangle (side $a$) | $\frac{\sqrt 3}{4}a^2$ | $3a$ | $\frac{a}{3\sqrt 3} = \frac{a\sqrt 3}{9} \cdot 2$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pitfall:** For a **full circular pipe**, $D_e = D$. For an **annulus**, $D_e = D - d$ (NOT the average). For a **square**, $D_e = a$, not the diagonal.

---

## 2.6 Schedule Number — Reference Table (Common Sizes)

| Nominal Size (in) | Sch 40 Wall (mm) | Sch 80 Wall (mm) | Sch 160 Wall (mm) |
|------------------|------------------|------------------|-------------------|
| ½" | 2.77 | 3.73 | 4.78 |
| 1" | 3.38 | 4.55 | 6.35 |
| 2" | 3.91 | 5.54 | 8.74 |
| 4" | 6.02 | 8.56 | 13.49 |
| 6" | 7.11 | 10.97 | 18.26 |
| 8" | 8.18 | 12.70 | 21.44 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does Schedule matter in KTU problems?** When $D$ (inside diameter) is not given directly, you must read it from schedule tables using $D_i = D_o - 2 \cdot t_{wall}$. Missing this step costs full marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Kinetic Energy Correction Factor ($\alpha$) for Laminar Pipe Flow

**Given:** Steady, laminar, fully developed flow in a circular pipe of radius $R$ has the parabolic velocity profile:

$$
v(r) = v_{max}\left(1 - \frac{r^2}{R^2}\right)
$$

**Step 1:** Compute average velocity. By definition, $\bar v = Q/A$:

$$
\bar v = \frac{1}{A}\int_A v \, dA = \frac{1}{\pi R^2}\int_0^R v_{max}\left(1 - \frac{r^2}{R^2}\right)(2\pi r)\, dr
$$

**Step 2:** Perform the integration. Let $u = 1 - r^2/R^2$, $du = -2r/R^2 \, dr$:

$$
\bar v = \frac{2 v_{max}}{R^2}\int_0^R \left(r - \frac{r^3}{R^2}\right)dr = \frac{2 v_{max}}{R^2}\left[\frac{r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4R^2}\right]_0^R
$$

$$
\bar v = \frac{2 v_{max}}{R^2}\left[\frac{R^2}{2} - \frac{R^2}{4}\right] = \frac{2 v_{max}}{R^2}\cdot\frac{R^2}{4} = \frac{v_{max}}{2}
$$

**Step 3:** Write $\alpha$ formula in terms of $r$:

$$
\alpha = \frac{1}{A}\int_A \left(\frac{v}{\bar v}\right)^3 dA = \frac{1}{\pi R^2}\int_0^R \left(\frac{2 v}{v_{max}}\right)^3 (2\pi r) \, dr
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $v(r) = v_{max}(1 - r^2/R^2)$:

$$
\alpha = \frac{16}{\pi R^2}\int_0^R \left(1 - \frac{r^2}{R^2}\right)^3 (2\pi r) \, dr = \frac{32}{R^2}\int_0^R r\left(1 - \frac{r^2}{R^2}\right)^3 dr
$$

**Step 5:** Use substitution $u = 1 - r^2/R^2$, $du = -2r/R^2 \, dr \Rightarrow r\,dr = -\frac{R^2}{2}du$:

$$
\alpha = \frac{32}{R^2}\cdot\left(-\frac{R^2}{2}\right)\int_1^0 u^3 \, du = 16\int_0^1 u^3 \, du = 16 \cdot \frac{1}{4} = 2.0
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\alpha_{\text{laminar}} = 2.0}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the value you'll use most in KTU problems whenever laminar flow is specified.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Momentum Correction Factor ($\beta$) for Laminar Flow

**Step 1:** Definition: $\beta = \frac{1}{A}\int_A (v/\bar v)^2 \, dA$

**Step 2:** Substitute parabolic profile, with $\bar v = v_{max}/2$:

$$
\beta = \frac{1}{\pi R^2}\int_0^R \left(\frac{2v}{v_{max}}\right)^2 (2\pi r) \, dr = \frac{8}{R^2}\int_0^R r\left(1 - \frac{r^2}{R^2}\right)^2 dr
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $u = 1 - r^2/R^2$:

$$
\beta = \frac{8}{R^2}\cdot\left(-\frac{R^2}{2}\right)\int_1^0 u^2\, du = 4\int_0^1 u^2\, du = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} = \frac{4}{3}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\beta_{\text{laminar}} = \frac{4}{3}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Darcy–Weisbach Equation (from Force Balance)

**Setup:** Consider a horizontal pipe of length $L$, diameter $D$, fully developed flow with pressure drop $\Delta p = p_1 - p_2$.

**Step 1: Force Balance on the fluid cylinder.** Two forces act in the flow direction:
- Pressure force on the two ends: $\Delta p \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$
- Wall shear force opposing flow: $\tau_w \cdot \pi D L$

For steady, fully developed flow (no acceleration), these balance:

$$
\Delta p \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \tau_w \cdot \pi D L
$$

**Step 2: Solve for wall shear stress:**

$$
\tau_w = \frac{\Delta p \cdot D}{4 L}
$$

**Step 3: Define Friction Factor.** The Darcy friction factor $f$ relates wall shear to dynamic pressure:

$$
\tau_w = f \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{8} \quad \text{(note: } f/4 \text{ times dynamic pressure)}
$$

**Step 4: Equate and solve for $\Delta p$:**

$$
\frac{\Delta p \cdot D}{4 L} = f \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{8}
$$

$$
\Delta p = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}
$$

**Step 5: Convert to head form** ($h_f = \Delta p / \rho g$):

$$
\boxed{h_f = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{v^2}{2g}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fanning vs. Darcy Confusion:** The Fanning friction factor $f_F = f/4$ (Darcy). Many textbooks/correlation charts use one or the other. KTU questions explicitly state which one.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Equivalent Diameter (Conceptual)

**Starting principle:** For non-circular ducts, the hydraulic diameter is defined so that the **Reynolds number** computed with $D_e$ gives the same flow regime classification as a circular pipe.

**Step 1:** For a circular pipe, the Reynolds number is $Re = \rho v D / \mu$, and the characteristic length is the **diameter** $D = 4 \cdot (\text{Area}) / (\text{Perimeter}) = 4A/P$.

**Step 2:** Generalize: for any cross-section, define:

$$
D_e = \frac{4 A_c}{P_w}
$$

where $A_c$ is the cross-sectional flow area and $P_w$ is the **wetted perimeter** (the perimeter in contact with the fluid).

**Step 3: Verification for circular pipe:** $A = \pi D^2/4$, $P = \pi D$, so $D_e = 4(\pi D^2/4)/(\pi D) = D$. ✓

**Step 4: Verification for annulus** (outer diameter $D$, inner diameter $d$):
- $A_c = \pi(D^2 - d^2)/4$
- $P_w = \pi D + \pi d = \pi(D + d)$
- $D_e = \frac{4 \cdot \pi(D^2 - d^2)/4}{\pi(D+d)} = \frac{D^2 - d^2}{D+d} = D - d$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Once $D_e$ is known, friction factor charts and pressure-drop correlations for circular pipes can be applied to rectangular ducts, annuli, etc. This is widely used in **HVAC, heat exchangers, and packed-bed reactors**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation of Schedule Number Relation

**Step 1:** The schedule number (originally ANSI B36.10) is defined as:

$$
\text{Schedule No.} = \frac{1000 \cdot P}{S}
$$

where $P$ = design internal pressure (psi or MPa), $S$ = allowable fiber stress at design temperature.

**Step 2:** Wall thickness from Schedule:

$$
t = \frac{P \cdot D_o}{2(SE + PY)} \quad \text{(Barlow's formula, modified)}
$$

**Step 3:** Solving for **inside diameter** (needed for velocity calculation):

$$
D_i = D_o - 2t
$$

**Step 4: Example.** For a 2" Sch 40 pipe, $D_o = 60.3$ mm, $t = 3.91$ mm, hence $D_i = 52.48$ mm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When a problem says "2-inch Sch 40 pipe carrying water," you must first find $D_i$ from standard tables. Skipping this gives an error of up to 15% in velocity!

---

## 3.6 Pump Power Derivation

**Step 1:** The pump delivers head $H_p$ to a mass flow rate $\dot m = \rho Q$. The useful power transferred to the fluid is:

$$
P_{\text{fluid}} = \dot m \cdot g \cdot H_p = \rho Q g H_p
$$

**Step 2:** The actual (brake) power required from the motor:

$$
\boxed{P_{\text{brake}} = \frac{\rho Q g H_p}{\eta_{\text{pump}}}
$$

**Step 3:** Equivalent **pressure form** (when $H_p$ is not given but $\Delta p$ is):

$$
H_p = \frac{\Delta p}{\rho g} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_{\text{brake}} = \frac{Q \Delta p}{\eta}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Pump efficiency $\eta$ typically ranges 60–85% for centrifugal pumps. KTU questions often give $\eta$ as a constant (e.g., 70%).

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example — Full KTU Style

**Problem:** Water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $\mu = 10^{-3}$ Pa·s) flows at $Q = 0.01$ m³/s through a 50 m long horizontal pipe of $D_i = 0.05$ m with two standard 90° elbows ($L_e/D = 30$ each) and one fully open gate valve ($L_e/D = 8$). The pipe is commercial steel ($\varepsilon = 0.046$ mm). Determine:
(a) Reynolds number and flow regime
(b) Friction factor using Colebrook equation (use Swamee–Jain explicit form)
(c) Total head loss
(d) Pump power required if overall efficiency is 70%

**Solution:**

**(a) Reynolds number:**

$$
v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.01}{\pi(0.05)^2/4} = \frac{0.01}{0.001963} = 5.09 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 5.09 \times 0.05}{10^{-3}} = 2.55 \times 10^5
$$

$$
\text{Flow regime: Turbulent} \; (\text{since } Re > 4000)
$$

**(b) Friction factor (Swamee–Jain):**

$$
\varepsilon/D = 0.046/50 = 9.2 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
f = \frac{0.25}{\left[\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}}\right)\right]^2}
$$

Plugging in:

$$
f = \frac{0.25}{\left[\log_{10}(2.486 \times 10^{-4} + 8.61 \times 10^{-5})\right]^2} = \frac{0.25}{[\log_{10}(3.347 \times 10^{-4})]^2}
$$

$$
f = \frac{0.25}{(-3.475)^2} = \frac{0.25}{12.08} = 0.0207
$$

**(c) Total head loss:**

$$
\sum L_e = 2(30 \times 0.05) + (8 \times 0.05) = 3.0 + 0.4 = 3.4 \text{ m}
$$

$$
L_{\text{total}} = 50 + 3.4 = 53.4 \text{ m}
$$

$$
h_f = f \frac{L}{D}\frac{v^2}{2g} = 0.0207 \cdot \frac{53.4}{0.05} \cdot \frac{5.09^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.0207 \times 1068 \times 1.32 = 29.2 \text{ m}
$$

**(d) Pump power:**

$$
P = \frac{\rho g Q H_p}{\eta} = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.01 \times 29.2}{0.70} = \frac{2864.5}{0.70} = 4092 \text{ W} \approx 4.09 \text{ kW}
$$
