## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Hagen-Poiseuille Velocity Profile Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Parameters: Pipe Radius R, Length L, Pressure Drop dP, Viscosity mu] --> B[Force Balance on Cylindrical Shell]
    B --> C[Shear Stress Expression: tau_r = -r/2 x dP/dz]
    C --> D[Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity: tau_r = -mu x du/dr]
    D --> E[Differential Equation: du/dr = r/2mu x dP/dz]
    E --> F[Integration with respect to r]
    F --> G[Apply Boundary Condition: at r=R, u=0]
    G --> H[Final Velocity Profile: u_r = dP/4muL x R-squared minus r-squared]
    H --> I[Parabolic Profile: u_max at r=0, u=0 at r=R]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Classification of Pumps

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Pumps] --> PD[Positive Displacement Pumps]
    P --> CD[Centrifugal Dynamic Pumps]
    PD --> P1[Reciprocating Pumps]
    PD --> P2[Rotary Pumps]
    P1 --> P1a[Piston Pump]
    P1 --> P1b[Diaphragm Pump]
    P2 --> P2a[Gear Pump]
    P2 --> P2b[Screw Pump]
    P2 --> P2c[Vane Pump]
    CD --> C1[Radial Flow Centrifugal Pump]
    CD --> C2[Axial Flow Centrifugal Pump]
    CD --> C3[Mixed Flow Centrifugal Pump]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Centrifugal Pump Characteristics and Issues

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CP[Centrifugal Pump Operation] --> PR[Priming Requirement]
    CP --> CV[Cavitation Phenomenon]
    CP --> NP[NPSH Considerations]
    CP --> WH[Water Hammer Effect]
    CP --> LH[Loss of Head and Power]
    PR --> PR1[Removal of Air from Casing]
    PR --> PR2[Required to develop Suction Lift]
    CV --> CV1[Formation of Vapor Bubbles at Low Pressure]
    CV --> CV2[Collapse at High Pressure Region]
    CV --> CV3[Causes Damage to Impeller]
    NP --> NP1[NPSH Available: Atmospheric minus Vapor Pressure minus Suction Lift]
    NP --> NP2[NPSH Required: Pump Manufacturer Spec]
    NP --> NP3[Condition: NPSH Available > NPSH Required]
    WH --> WH1[Pressure Surge due to Sudden Valve Closure]
    WH --> WH2[Can Cause Pipe Rupture]
    LH --> LH1[Manometric Efficiency]
    LH --> LH2[Mechanical Efficiency]
    LH --> LH3[Overall Efficiency]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Flow Measurement Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FM[Flow Measurement System] --> IN[Internal Flow Meters]
    FM --> OT[Other Methods: Rotameters, etc.]
    IN --> OR[Orifice Meter]
    IN --> VE[Venturimeter]
    OR --> OR1[Sharp-edged Plate with Central Hole]
    OR --> OR2[Pressure Drop: P1 minus P2]
    OR --> OR3[Coefficient of Discharge: 0.60 to 0.65]
    OR --> OR4[Q equals Cd times A2 divided by sqrt of 1 minus D2 over D1 to the 4th power times sqrt of 2g deltah]
    VE --> VE1[Converging Section to Throat]
    VE --> VE2[Diverging Section: Recovery]
    VE --> VE3[Coefficient of Discharge: 0.95 to 0.99]
    VE --> VE4[Same Equation Form as Orifice]
```

### 4.5 Schematic Representation: Moody Diagram Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RE[Reynolds Number Re on x-axis logarithmic] --> F[Friction Factor f on y-axis logarithmic]
    RE --> L[Laminar Region: Re less than 2000]
    RE --> T[Transitional Region: 2000 less than Re less than 4000]
    RE --> TU[Turbulent Region: Re greater than 4000]
    L --> LF[f equals 64 over Re]
    TU --> TR[Transition Curve Family]
    TU --> TU1[Relative Roughness epsilon over D]
    TU1 --> R1[Smooth Pipe: epsilon over D equals 0]
    TU1 --> R2[Rough Pipe: high epsilon over D]
    TU1 --> R3[Fully Rough: f independent of Re]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**State and derive the relationship between average velocity and maximum velocity for laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid through a circular pipe.**

**Model Answer:**

For fully-developed laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid in a circular pipe of radius R, the velocity distribution is parabolic:

$$u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2)$$

The **maximum velocity** occurs at the centerline (r = 0):

$$u_{max} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4\mu L}$$

The **average velocity** is obtained by integrating the local velocity over the cross-section and dividing by the area:

$$u_{avg} = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{1}{\pi R^2} \int_0^R u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{8\mu L}$$

Therefore, the relationship is:

$$\boxed{u_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} u_{max}}$$

The average velocity is exactly **half** the maximum velocity for laminar flow in a circular pipe. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the terms: (i) Fanning friction factor, (ii) Darcy friction factor. How are they related?**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Fanning Friction Factor (f_f):** It is defined as the ratio of the wall shear stress to the kinetic energy per unit volume of the flowing fluid:

$$f_f = \frac{\tau_w}{\frac{1}{2} \rho u^2}$$

**(ii) Darcy Friction Factor (f_D):** It is defined as four times the Fanning friction factor and is used in the Darcy-Weisbach equation for head loss:

$$f_D = \frac{2 \tau_w}{\frac{1}{2} \rho u^2} = 4 f_f$$

The Darcy friction factor is also given by **64/Re** for laminar flow in a circular pipe, while the Fanning value is **16/Re**.

**Relationship:**

$$\boxed{f_D = 4 \times f_f}$$ **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Starting from the basic equations, derive the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** for laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid through a horizontal circular pipe. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the **Moody diagram** and explain its significance in pipe flow analysis. Discuss the variation of friction factor with Reynolds number for (i) laminar flow, (ii) turbulent flow in smooth pipes, and (iii) fully rough turbulent flow. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Hagen-Poiseuille Derivation (7 marks)**

**Consider** steady, fully-developed, laminar, incompressible flow of a Newtonian fluid in a horizontal pipe of radius R and length L.

**Step 1: Force Balance on a Cylindrical Element of Radius r**

Forces acting in the flow (z) direction:
- Pressure force at inlet: $F_1 = P_1 \cdot \pi r^2$
- Pressure force at outlet: $F_2 = P_2 \cdot \pi r^2$
- Shear force on the curved surface: $F_3 = \tau_r \cdot 2\pi r L$ (opposing flow)

**Step 2: Apply Newton's Second Law (Steady State)**

$$P_1 \pi r^2 - P_2 \pi r^2 - \tau_r \cdot 2\pi r L = 0$$

$$\tau_r = \frac{(P_1 - P_2) r}{2L} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$

**Step 3: Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity**

For a Newtonian fluid, the shear stress is proportional to the velocity gradient:

$$\tau_r = -\mu \frac{du}{dr}$$

**Step 4: Equate the Two Expressions**

$$-\mu \frac{du}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$

$$\frac{du}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2\mu L}$$

**Step 5: Integrate**

$$u(r) = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot r^2}{4\mu L} + C_1$$

**Step 6: Apply Boundary Condition u(R) = 0 (no-slip)**

$$0 = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4\mu L} + C_1 \implies C_1 = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4\mu L}$$

**Step 7: Final Velocity Profile**

$$u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2)$$

**Step 8: Calculate Flow Rate Q**

$$Q = \int_0^R u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L}\int_0^R (R^2 r - r^3) dr = \frac{\pi \Delta P R^4}{8\mu L}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8\mu L}}$$

This is the **Hagen-Poiseuille Equation**. **(7 marks)** ✅

---

#### **Part (b) — Moody Diagram (7 marks)**

**Definition:** The Moody diagram is a log-log plot of the **Darcy friction factor (f_D)** on the y-axis versus the **Reynolds number (Re)** on the x-axis, with a family of curves parameterized by the **relative roughness (ε/D)** of the pipe.

**Significance:**
- It allows engineers to determine the friction factor for any pipe and flow condition without solving complex equations.
- It unifies the analysis of laminar, transitional, and turbulent flow.
- It accounts for surface roughness, which significantly affects turbulent flow.

**Variation of f with Re:**

**(i) Laminar Flow (Re < 2000):**
The friction factor is **independent of roughness** and depends only on Re:

$$f_D = \frac{64}{Re}$$

On the Moody diagram, this appears as a **straight line** with negative slope on the log-log plot.

**(ii) Turbulent Flow in Smooth Pipes (Re > 4000, ε/D = 0):**
The friction factor depends on Re only, given by empirical correlations such as the **Blasius equation**:

$$f_D = 0.316 \cdot Re^{-0.25} \quad \text{(for } 4000 < Re < 10^5\text{)}$$

On the Moody diagram, the curve **decreases monotonically** with Re.

**(iii) Fully Rough Turbulent Flow:**
At very high Re, the friction factor becomes **independent of Re** and depends only on relative roughness (the horizontal asymptote on the Moody diagram). This is given by:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f_D}} = -2 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7}\right)$$ **(7 marks)** ✅

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Classify **pumps** with neat sketches. Discuss the operating principle of a **centrifugal pump** in detail. Explain the concept of **priming** and why it is necessary. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **cavitation**? Explain the concept of **NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head)**. A centrifugal pump handles water at 30°C and is to deliver 0.05 m³/s at a head of 20 m. The pump has a suction lift of 3 m and suction pipe diameter of 100 mm. Calculate the NPSH available. Take atmospheric pressure = 1.013 × 10⁵ Pa, vapor pressure of water at 30°C = 4.246 kPa, friction loss in suction pipe = 0.5 m. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Classification of Pumps & Centrifugal Pump (7 marks)**

**Classification of Pumps:**

Pumps are broadly classified into two categories:

**1. Positive Displacement Pumps:** These deliver a fixed volume of fluid per cycle. They include:
- **Reciprocating Pumps:** Use a piston/plunger (e.g., piston pump, diaphragm pump)
- **Rotary Pumps:** Use rotating elements (e.g., gear pump, screw pump, vane pump)

**2. Centrifugal (Dynamic) Pumps:** These impart kinetic energy to the fluid using a rotating impeller, which is then converted to pressure energy. They include:
- **Radial Flow Pumps:** Fluid exits radially (most common)
- **Axial Flow Pumps:** Fluid exits axially (high flow, low head)
- **Mixed Flow Pumps:** Combination of radial and axial

**Operating Principle of Centrifugal Pump:**
1. Fluid enters the **eye (center)** of the rotating **impeller** through the suction pipe.
2. The impeller blades impart **kinetic energy** to the fluid, accelerating it radially outward.
3. The fluid exits the impeller tip with high velocity and enters the **volute casing**.
4. In the volute, the cross-sectional area gradually **increases**, causing the kinetic energy to convert into **pressure energy** (Bernoulli's principle).
5. The pressurized fluid is discharged through the delivery pipe.

**Priming:**
Priming is the process of **filling the pump casing and suction pipe completely with the liquid** to be pumped, before starting the pump.

**Why is it necessary?**
- Centrifugal pumps cannot pump air efficiently because air has very low density (≈ 1.2 kg/m³).
- The pressure rise developed by the impeller is proportional to fluid density: $\Delta P \propto \rho$.
- If air is present, the pressure rise is insufficient to lift water from below, and the pump will not deliver liquid.
- Priming ensures the impeller is always submerged in liquid, creating the required suction. **(7 marks)** ✅

---

#### **Part (b) — Cavitation, NPSH & Numerical (7 marks)**

**Cavitation:** Cavitation is the phenomenon of formation of **vapor bubbles** in a liquid when the local static pressure falls below the **vapor pressure** of the liquid. These bubbles collapse when they move into higher-pressure regions, causing **noise, vibration, and damage** to the impeller and casing.

**NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head):**

**NPSH Available (NPSH_A):** The total head available at the pump suction above the vapor pressure of the liquid:

$$NPSH_A = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \frac{P_{vapor}}{\rho g} - H_s - h_f$$

Where:
- $P_{atm}$ = atmospheric pressure
- $P_{vapor}$ = vapor pressure of the liquid
- $H_s$ = suction lift (positive when suction is below pump)
- $h_f$ = friction losses in suction pipe

**NPSH Required (NPSH_R):** The minimum head required by the pump to avoid cavitation, specified by the manufacturer.

**Condition for No Cavitation:** NPSH_A > NPSH_R

---

**Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Temperature: T = 30°C
- Discharge: Q = 0.05 m³/s
- Head: H = 20 m
- Suction lift: H_s = 3 m
- Suction pipe diameter: D = 100 mm = 0.1 m
- Atmospheric pressure: P_atm = 1.013 × 10⁵ Pa
- Vapor pressure: P_v = 4.246 kPa = 4246 Pa
- Friction loss: h_f = 0.5 m

**Step 1: Calculate Velocity in Suction Pipe**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$u = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.05}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.366 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Velocity Head in Suction Pipe**

$$\frac{u^2}{2g} = \frac{(6.366)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{40.53}{19.62} = 2.066 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Pressure Head (Atmospheric)**

$$\frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} = \frac{1.013 \times 10^5}{1000 \times 9.81} = 10.326 \text{ m of water}$$

**Step 4: Vapor Pressure Head**

$$\frac{P_v}{\rho g} = \frac{4246}{1000 \times 9.81} = 0.4328 \text{ m of water}$$

**Step 5: Calculate NPSH Available**

$$NPSH_A = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - \frac{P_v}{\rho g} - H_s - h_f - \frac{u^2}{2g}$$

$$NPSH_A = 10.326 - 0.4328 - 3 - 0.5 - 2.066$$

$$\boxed{NPSH_A = 4.327 \text{ m}}$$

**Conclusion:** The NPSH available is **4.327 m**. This value must be greater than the NPSH required by the pump (which is typically specified in the pump catalog) for safe operation without cavitation. **(7 marks)** ✅

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Error:** When applying Bernoulli's equation to orifice/venturi meters, students often forget the negative sign or mix up the directions of the pressure drop. Always use $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$ with D₁ > D₂.
> 2. **Diameter vs. Area Ratio:** In the derivation, the ratio is **$(D_2/D_1)^4$**, not $(D_2/D_1)^2$. Students commonly make this mistake.
> 3. **NPSH Numerical:** Do not forget the velocity head term $u^2/(2g)$ in the suction pipe. Many students miss this and lose 2 marks.
> 4. **C_d Values:** For orifice meter, C_d = 0.6 to 0.65; for venturimeter, C_d = 0.95 to 0.99. Do not interchange.
> 5. **Friction Factor Notation:** In KTU exams, clarify whether you are using f_D (Darcy) or f_f (Fanning). Mixing up these can lead to a factor of 4 error in head loss calculations.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Hagen-Poiseuille Flow** is laminar flow in a circular pipe with a **parabolic velocity profile** and **linear shear stress distribution**.
- ✅ The **Hagen-Poiseuille Equation**: $Q = \pi R^4 \Delta P / (8 \mu L)$ — pressure drop is proportional to Q and inversely proportional to R⁴.
- ✅ **Average velocity = ½ × Maximum velocity** (laminar, circular pipe only).
- ✅ **Darcy friction factor (f_D) = 64/Re** for laminar flow; **Fanning friction factor (f_f) = 16/Re**.
- ✅ **f_D = 4 f_f** (always remember this relationship).
- ✅ **Moody Diagram** is plotted on log-log axes: f_D vs. Re with relative roughness ε/D as a parameter.
- ✅ **Pumps are classified** as Positive Displacement (Reciprocating/Rotary) and Centrifugal (Radial/Axial/Mixed).
- ✅ **Priming** is essential for centrifugal pumps because they cannot displace air.
- ✅ **Cavitation** occurs when local pressure < vapor pressure; prevented by ensuring **NPSH_A > NPSH_R**.
- ✅ **Water Hammer** is a pressure surge caused by sudden valve closure in long pipelines.
- ✅ **Orifice and Venturimeter** equations have the same form, but C_d values differ (orifice ≈ 0.62, venturi ≈ 0.98).
- ✅ The **Bernoulli Equation** combined with **Continuity** gives the flow rate through restriction-type meters: $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{2g \Delta h} / \sqrt{1 - (D_2/D_1)^4}$.
- ✅ For **NPSH calculations**, remember the formula: $NPSH_A = P_{atm}/(\rho g) - P_v/(\rho g) - H_s - h_f - u^2/(2g)$.
- ✅ **Manometric head** of a pump = total head developed by the pump; **efficiencies** (manometric, mechanical, overall) are critical for power calculations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weirs: Classification and Derivation of Equations for Flow Rate in Rectangular and Triangular Notches, Concept of Area Meters: Rotameter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT403 - FLUID FLOW AND PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Weirs: Classification and Derivation of Equations for Flow Rate in Rectangular and Triangular Notches, Concept of Area Meters: Rotameter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Weirs and Notches — Formal Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A **notch** is a calibrated opening made in the side wall of a tank or reservoir, usually cut into a thin metallic plate, through which a liquid flows and whose primary function is the **measurement of the volumetric flow rate** of the liquid.
> A **weir** is essentially a larger, structural obstruction (often a dam-like barrier) placed across an open channel or stream, with a shaped crest or opening that also serves flow measurement. In many textbooks, "weir" and "notch" are used interchangeably for the same governing equations.

**Key Terminology Table:**

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Crest** | The bottom edge of the notch opening over which water spills |
| **Head (H)** | The vertical height of liquid above the crest of the notch |
| **Sill** | The base or bottom of the weir structure |
| **Apron** | The downstream floor near the weir preventing scour |
| **End Contractions** | The side contractions that occur when the notch width is less than the channel width |
| **Suppressed Weir** | A weir with side walls flush with the notch sides — **no end contractions** |
| **Discharge (Q)** | Volume rate of flow through the notch, in **m³/s** |
| **Coefficient of Discharge (Cd)** | Dimensionless factor correcting for friction, viscosity, and vena-contracta effects |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Overflow Hole in a Bathtub:**
> Imagine punching a small rectangular or V-shaped hole near the top of a bathtub. The faster the tap is open (i.e., the higher the water level above the hole), the more water gushes out per second. **A notch measures flow rate by observing *how high the water stands above a precisely shaped opening*.** The shape of the opening is the clever part:
> - A **rectangular notch** works like a wide slit — the flow rate depends on **H^(3/2)** (head to the power 1.5).
> - A **triangular (V) notch** is shaped like an inverted V — because the opening narrows toward the bottom, low flows produce a relatively high head, giving **better accuracy at small discharge rates** where the rectangular notch would be almost unreadable.

The physics rests on **Torricelli's Law**: the velocity of efflux through any small element of area equals $\sqrt{2gh}$, where *h* is the depth of that element below the free liquid surface. Integrating this velocity over the entire notch area, and multiplying by a real-world *correction factor* $C_d$, gives the discharge equation.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Weirs (KTU High-Priority)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Weirs are classified along SIX different axes in the KTU syllabus:**

**A. Based on Shape of the Notch Opening**
- **Rectangular Weir**
- **Triangular (V-notch) Weir**
- **Trapezoidal (Cipolletti) Weir**
- **Stepped Weir**

**B. Based on Shape of the Crest**
- **Sharp-Crested Weir** — thin plate edge; standard equation applies.
- **Broad-Crested Weir** — crest width large enough that flow becomes critical.
- **Narrow-Crested Weir** — intermediate width.
- **Ogee Weir** — smooth S-shaped profile (used in spillways).

**C. Based on the Number of End Contractions**
- **Weir with End Contractions** (n = 2 typically)
- **Suppressed Weir / Weir without End Contractions** (n = 0; full channel width)

**D. Based on the Nature of Discharge (Downstream Water Level)**
- **Free Weir** — downstream water level is below the crest.
- **Drowned / Submerged Weir** — downstream water level rises above the crest.

**E. Based on the Effect of Velocity of Approach**
- **Weir with Low Velocity of Approach** (negligible, $v_a \to 0$)
- **Weir with Significant Velocity of Approach** (head correction required)

**F. Based on the Liquid Measured**
- **Weir for Open Channel Flow**
- **Spillway Weir** (engineering hydraulic structures)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Torricelli's Theorem & Continuity

The entire discipline of weir flow measurement rests on two bedrock principles:

1. **Torricelli's Law (Efflux Velocity):** A small element of liquid at depth *h* below the free surface leaves the notch with theoretical velocity:
$$v_{theoretical} = \sqrt{2gh}$$

2. **Continuity of Mass:** The volumetric discharge through an elemental strip of area *dA* at depth *h* is:
$$dQ = C_d \cdot v \cdot dA = C_d \cdot \sqrt{2gh} \cdot dA$$

Integrating $dQ$ across the entire notch opening from the crest (h = 0) to the free surface (h = H) yields the **fundamental weir discharge equation** for any notch geometry.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the *only* set of formulas you must memorise for KTU ESE 2024 (Module 3).**

| # | Weir Type | Discharge Equation | Typical $C_d$ | Head Exponent |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Rectangular (Suppressed, no end contractions)** | $Q = \tfrac{2}{3} C_d \, b \, \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2}$ | **0.62 – 0.66** | 3/2 |
| 2 | **Rectangular (with end contractions)** | $Q = \tfrac{2}{3} C_d \, L_e \, \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2}$  where  $L_e = b - 0.1 n H$ | **0.62 – 0.66** | 3/2 |
| 3 | **Triangular (V-notch, semi-angle $\theta/2$)** | $Q = \tfrac{8}{15} C_d \, \tan\!\left(\tfrac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2}$ | **0.58 – 0.62** | 5/2 |
| 4 | **Right-angled Triangular (V-notch, $\theta = 90°$)** | $Q = \tfrac{8}{15} C_d \, \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2} \approx 1.417 \, H^{5/2}$ | **≈ 0.60** | 5/2 |
| 5 | **Trapezoidal (Cipolletti)** | $Q = \tfrac{2}{3} C_d \, b \, \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2}$ (with side slope 1 horizontal : 4 vertical) | **≈ 0.63** | 3/2 |
| 6 | **Velocity of Approach correction** | $H_a = H + \dfrac{v_a^2}{2g}$  (use $H_a$ in place of $H$) | — | — |

**Where:**
- $Q$ = volumetric discharge $[m^3/s]$
- $C_d$ = coefficient of discharge (dimensionless)
- $b$ = width of rectangular notch $[m]$
- $H$ = head of liquid above the crest $[m]$
- $\theta$ = total included angle of V-notch $[degrees/radians]$
- $n$ = number of end contractions (0, 1, or 2)
- $L_e$ = effective crest length $[m]$
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $\sqrt{2g} \approx 4.429$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - **Rectangular notches** are used in large dams, canals, and wastewater treatment plants where flow rates are high.
> - **V-notches** are used in **laboratories, small irrigation channels, and chemical process flows** where discharge is small but needs high precision.
> - **Cipolletti (trapezoidal)** weirs combine the simplicity of the rectangular formula with the self-compensating nature of the V-notch side slopes.

---

## 2.3 Physical Phenomena Affecting the Coefficient $C_d$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is $C_d < 1$? Three real-world effects reduce the actual discharge below the ideal theoretical value:**

1. **Vena Contracta Effect:** After passing the notch edge, the jet cross-section contracts slightly before fully forming. The actual minimum cross-section is about **62–66%** of the notch area.
2. **Frictional / Viscous Losses** at the notch edge and along the side walls.
3. **Surface Tension and Aeration** effects, especially at very low heads (< 50 mm).

For a well-designed sharp-crested rectangular weir with moderate heads (H > 50 mm), $C_d$ approaches **0.62** (Rehbock formula approximations).

---

## 2.4 Choice of Notch Geometry — When to Use What

| Criterion | Rectangular Notch | Triangular (V) Notch |
|---|---|---|
| Small flows (Q < 30 L/s) | **Inaccurate** (head too small to read) | **Excellent** |
| Large flows (Q > 300 L/s) | **Excellent** | Head becomes too large, costly |
| Accuracy | ±3% (typical) | ±1–2% (better at low flow) |
| Construction | Simple | Requires careful V-angle cutting |
| Suspended solids in water | Tolerant | Fills up, needs cleaning |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **The following derivations are exhaustive, line-by-line, and tailored to the KTU ESE 14-mark questions. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No shortcuts.**

---

## 3.1 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION — RECTANGULAR NOTCH DISCHARGE

### 3.1.1 Setup and Coordinate System

Consider a thin metallic plate with a rectangular notch of width **b** fitted to the side of a tank. Let:
- **H** = head of liquid above the crest of the notch (measured from the free liquid surface down to the notch crest).
- **b** = horizontal width of the notch $[m]$.
- A small elemental horizontal strip of thickness **dh** is considered at a depth **h** below the free liquid surface.

### 3.1.2 Elemental Strip Analysis

**Step 1 — Area of the elemental strip:**

The strip lies at depth $h$, has thickness $dh$, and stretches across the full notch width $b$:

$$dA = b \cdot dh$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical velocity of efflux at depth h (Torricelli's Law):**

The potential energy of the element at height $h$ above the notch crest converts to kinetic energy at the notch:

$$\frac{1}{2}mv^2 = mgh \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; v = \sqrt{2gh}$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical elemental discharge:**

$$dQ_{theoretical} = v \cdot dA = \sqrt{2gh} \cdot b \cdot dh = b \sqrt{2g} \cdot \sqrt{h} \, dh$$

**Step 4 — Apply the coefficient of discharge:**

The empirical coefficient $C_d$ accounts for vena contracta, friction, and turbulence:

$$dQ = C_d \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \sqrt{h} \, dh$$

### 3.1.3 Integration Across the Notch

**Step 5 — Integrate from crest (h = 0) to free surface (h = H):**

$$Q = \int_0^H C_d \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot h^{1/2} \, dh$$

$$Q = C_d \, b \, \sqrt{2g} \int_0^H h^{1/2} \, dh$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$\int_0^H h^{1/2} \, dh = \left[ \frac{h^{3/2}}{3/2} \right]_0^H = \frac{2}{3} H^{3/2}$$

**Step 7 — Final Equation for Suppressed (no end contractions) Rectangular Notch:**

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{2}{3} \, C_d \, b \, \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2}}$$

### 3.1.4 Effect of End Contractions (Francis Formula)

If the notch is narrower than the channel, the jet contracts horizontally at both ends, reducing the effective width. The effective length is:

$$L_e = b - 0.1 \, n \, H$$

where **n** is the number of end contractions (typically 2). The corrected discharge is:

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{2}{3} \, C_d \, L_e \, \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2} = \frac{2}{3} \, C_d \, (b - 0.1nH) \, \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2}}$$

### 3.1.5 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** Water flows over a rectangular notch 600 mm wide under a head of 200 mm. Take $C_d = 0.62$. Calculate the discharge. The notch is suppressed.

**Solution:**

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.62 \times 0.6 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81} \times (0.2)^{3/2}$$

Step-by-step evaluation:

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.62 \times 0.6 \times 4.429 \times 0.08944$$

$$Q = 0.4133 \times 4.429 \times 0.08944$$

$$Q = 1.831 \times 0.08944$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 0.1637 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 163.7 \text{ L/s}}$$

---

## 3.2 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION — TRIANGULAR (V-NOTCH) DISCHARGE

### 3.2.1 Setup and Geometry

Consider a V-notch of total included angle **$\theta$** cut in a thin plate. Define:
- **H** = head of liquid above the apex (lowest point) of the V.
- Let the depth below the free surface be measured as $h$ (so at the free surface, $h = 0$; at the apex, $h = H$).
- The half-angle of the V at its apex is $\theta/2$.

### 3.2.2 Width of the Elemental Strip

**Step 1 — Locate the strip:**

A horizontal elemental strip of thickness $dh$ is at depth $h$ below the free surface. The vertical distance of this strip *above the apex* is:

$$y = H - h$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the width of the strip at this location:**

The V-notch walls flare out at half-angle $\theta/2$, so the horizontal half-width at height $y$ above the apex is $y \tan(\theta/2)$. The total width of the strip is:

$$w = 2 \cdot y \cdot \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) = 2 (H - h) \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Area of the elemental strip:**

$$dA = w \cdot dh = 2 (H - h) \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) dh$$

### 3.2.3 Elemental Discharge

**Step 4 — Theoretical velocity at depth h (Torricelli):**

$$v = \sqrt{2gh}$$

**Step 5 — Elemental discharge with coefficient of discharge:**

$$dQ = C_d \cdot v \cdot dA = C_d \cdot \sqrt{2gh} \cdot 2 (H - h) \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) dh$$

**Step 6 — Factor out the constants:**

$$dQ = 2 \, C_d \, \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \cdot (H - h) \sqrt{h} \, dh$$

### 3.2.4 Total Discharge by Integration

**Step 7 — Integrate from h = 0 (free surface) to h = H (apex):**

$$Q = 2 \, C_d \, \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \int_0^H (H - h) h^{1/2} \, dh$$

**Step 8 — Expand the integrand:**

$$(H - h) h^{1/2} = H \, h^{1/2} - h^{3/2}$$

**Step 9 — Split into two integrals:**

$$Q = 2 \, C_d \, \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \left[ H \int_0^H h^{1/2} \, dh - \int_0^H h^{3/2} \, dh \right]$$

**Step 10 — Evaluate each integral:**

$$\int_0^H h^{1/2} \, dh = \frac{2}{3} H^{3/2}$$

$$\int_0^H h^{3/2} \, dh = \frac{2}{5} H^{5/2}$$

**Step 11 — Substitute back:**

$$Q = 2 \, C_d \, \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \left[ H \cdot \frac{2}{3} H^{3/2} - \frac{2}{5} H^{5/2} \right]$$

**Step 12 — Simplify the bracketed term:**

$$\frac{2}{3} H^{5/2} - \frac{2}{5} H^{5/2} = H^{5/2} \left( \frac{2}{3} - \frac{2}{5} \right) = H^{5/2} \cdot \frac{10 - 6}{15} = \frac{4}{15} H^{5/2}$$

**Step 13 — Final General Equation for V-Notch Discharge:**

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{8}{15} \, C_d \, \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2}}$$

### 3.2.5 Special Case — Right-Angled V-Notch ($\theta = 90°$)

For the very common **right-angled triangular notch**, $\theta/2 = 45°$ and $\tan(45°) = 1$:

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{8}{15} \, C_d \, \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2}}$$

Using $C_d = 0.6$ and $\sqrt{2g} = 4.429$:

$$Q = \frac{8}{15} \times 0.6 \times 4.429 \times H^{5/2} \approx 1.417 \, H^{5/2}$$

### 3.2.6 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A right-angled V-notch is used to measure water flow. The head is 250 mm. Take $C_d = 0.6$. Find the discharge.

**Solution:**

$$Q = \frac{8}{15} \times 0.6 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81} \times (0.25)^{5/2}$$

Step 1: $H^{5/2} = (0.25)^{2.5} = 0.03125$

Step 2: $\sqrt{2g} = 4.429$

Step 3: Coefficient $\frac{8}{15} \times 0.6 = 0.32$

Step 4: 

$$Q = 0.32 \times 4.429 \times 0.03125 = 0.0443 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 44.3 \text{ L/s}}$$

---

## 3.3 ROTAMETER — CONCEPT AND DISCHARGE EQUATION

### 3.3.1 Definition and Working Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **rotameter** is a **variable-area flow meter** consisting of a vertically mounted **tapered tube** (conical, wider at the top) and a free-floating **float (or "bob")** inside. As fluid flows upward, the float rises until the drag force balances its effective weight. The float position is read against a calibrated scale on the tube wall.

### 3.3.2 Force Balance on the Float

At equilibrium, the upward drag force = effective (apparent) weight of the float:

$$F_{drag} = W_{float} - F_{buoyancy}$$

$$C_d' \, A_f \, \frac{\rho v^2}{2} = V_f \, \rho_f \, g - V_f \, \rho \, g$$

$$C_d' \, A_f \, \frac{\rho v^2}{2} = V_f \, g \, (\rho_f - \rho)$$

### 3.3.3 Derivation of Float Velocity

**Step 1 — Solve for $v$:**

$$v^2 = \frac{2 \, V_f \, g \, (\rho_f - \rho)}{C_d' \, A_f \, \rho}$$

$$v = \sqrt{ \frac{2 \, V_f \, g \, (\rho_f - \rho)}{C_d' \, A_f \, \rho} }$$

**Step 2 — Actual discharge through the annular area:**

The fluid passes through the **annular area** between the float and the tube wall:

$$A = A_t - A_f$$

where $A_t$ = tube area at the float's height, $A_f$ = float cross-section.

**Step 3 — Apply the discharge coefficient:**

$$Q = C_d \, A \, v = C_d \, (A_t - A_f) \, \sqrt{ \frac{2 \, V_f \, g \, (\rho_f - \rho)}{C_d' \, A_f \, \rho} }$$

$$\boxed{Q = C_d \, A \, \sqrt{ \frac{2 \, g \, V_f \, (\rho_f - \rho)}{A_f \, \rho} }}$$

### 3.3.4 Variables and Their Meanings

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $Q$ | Volumetric discharge | $m^3/s$ |
| $C_d$ | Discharge coefficient of the rotameter | dimensionless |
| $A$ | Annular area $(A_t - A_f)$ | $m^2$ |
| $V_f$ | Volume of the float | $m^3$ |
| $A_f$ | Maximum cross-sectional area of the float | $m^2$ |
| $\rho_f$ | Density of the float material | $kg/m^3$ |
| $\rho$ | Density of the flowing fluid | $kg/m^3$ |
| $g$ | Acceleration due to gravity | $9.81 \, m/s^2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a taper?** Because the tube widens upward, as flow increases, the float rises into a wider section, **increasing the annular area $A$**. This self-regulating geometry gives a **linear-ish scale** (especially for liquids) and a **wide turndown ratio** (often 10:1).

### 3.3.5 Advantages, Disadvantages, and Applications

| Aspect | Rotameter Characteristic |
|---|---|
| **Type** | Variable area meter |
| **Installation** | Vertical, fluid flowing upward |
| **Pressure drop** | Constant and equal to the float's effective weight ÷ float area |
| **Scale** | Linear (for liquids), nearly linear (for gases) |
| **Accuracy** | ±2% of full scale (typical), up to ±1% (precision types) |
| **Advantage** | Direct visual reading, no external power, simple |
| **Disadvantage** | Must be installed vertically; float position can be affected by viscosity/density changes if not compensated |
| **Applications** | Laboratory flow measurement, chemical dosing, medical oxygen flow, purge meters |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Rectangular Notch — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Tank with Liquid] --> B[Liquid Free Surface at height H above crest]
    B --> C[Rectangular Notch of width b in thin plate]
    C --> D[Elemental Strip: width b, thickness dh, depth h below surface]
    D --> E[Apply Torricelli: v = sqrt 2gh]
    E --> F[dQ theoretical = b x sqrt 2gh x dh]
    F --> G[Apply discharge coefficient Cd]
    G --> H[dQ actual = Cd x b x sqrt 2gh x dh]
    H --> I[Integrate from h=0 to h=H]
    I --> J[Final: Q = 2 by 3 x Cd x b x sqrt 2g x H to the 3 by 2]
    J --> K{End Contractions present?}
    K -->|Yes n=2| L[Use Le = b minus 0.1 n H]
    K -->|No Suppressed| M[Use full b]
    L --> N[Discharge Q output in m3 per second]
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 Triangular (V-Notch) — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Tank with Liquid] --> B[Free Surface H above apex of V]
    B --> C[V notch of included angle theta]
    C --> D[Strip at depth h, width w = 2 times H minus h times tan theta by 2]
    D --> E[Strip area dA = 2 times H minus h times tan theta by 2 times dh]
    E --> F[Apply Torricelli v = sqrt 2gh]
    F --> G[dQ = Cd times 2 times tan theta by 2 times sqrt 2g times H minus h times sqrt h times dh]
    G --> H[Integrate from h=0 to h=H]
    H --> I[Expand integrand: H h^1 by 2 minus h^3 by 2]
    I --> J[Integrals: 2 by 3 H^5 by 2 minus 2 by 5 H^5 by 2]
    J --> K[Simplify: 4 by 15 H^5 by 2]
    K --> L[Final: Q = 8 by 15 times Cd times tan theta by 2 times sqrt 2g times H^5 by 2]
```

## 4.3 Rotameter — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fluid enters at bottom] --> B[Flows upward through tapered tube]
    B --> C[Pushes the float upward by drag force]
    C --> D{Forces balanced at equilibrium?}
    D -->|Upward drag exceeds weight| E[Float rises to wider section]
    D -->|Downward effective weight exceeds drag| F[Float falls to narrower section]
    D -->|Balanced| G[Float settles at position reading]
    E --> H[Annular area increases, drag decreases, equilibrium restored]
    F --> I[Annular area decreases, drag increases, equilibrium restored]
    G --> J[Read flow rate directly from scale]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Output: Q = Cd x A x sqrt 2 g Vf rho_f minus rho by Af rho]
```

## 4.4 Classification of Weirs — Hierarchical Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Weirs and Notches] --> B[By Shape]
    A --> C[By Crest Type]
    A --> D[By End Contractions]
    A --> E[By Discharge Type]
    B --> B1[Rectangular]
    B --> B2[Triangular V notch]
    B --> B3[Trapezoidal Cipolletti]
    B --> B4[Stepped]
    C --> C1[Sharp Crested]
    C --> C2[Broad Crested]
    C --> C3[Narrow Crested]
    C --> C4[Ogee]
    D --> D1[With End Contractions n=2]
    D --> D2[Suppressed n=0]
    E --> E1[Free Weir]
    E --> E2[Drowned Submerged Weir]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Head must be in METRES** in all KTU numerical problems — converting cm or mm to m is the most common place students lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Do not forget the coefficient of discharge $C_d$** in the final numerical answer. Examiners often deduct 2 marks if the student writes the theoretical equation without the empirical $C_d$.
> 3. In the V-notch derivation, the **width of the strip** must be written as $w = 2(H-h)\tan(\theta/2)$ — examiners specifically test whether students used the *total* width (with the factor of 2) or only the half-width.
> 4. For the **rotameter**, examiners often give a value with the float *in equilibrium* and ask for the discharge — students forget to use the **annular area** $(A_t - A_f)$ instead of just the float area. Lose 2 marks.
> 5. Show the **integration step clearly**. A 14-mark question demands a full derivation — skipping from $dQ$ directly to $Q$ loses 4–5 marks.
> 6. Use $g = 9.81$ m/s² (NOT 10 m/s²) in KTU papers unless specifically instructed otherwise.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define a weir. Distinguish between a notch and a weir.**

**Model Answer:**

A **weir** is a hydraulic structure (dam or barrier) built across an open channel to measure the flow of liquid, where the liquid flows over a crest or through a notch/opening in the structure.

| Notch | Weir |
|---|---|
| Small calibrated opening in a thin plate | Larger structural obstruction across the channel |
| Used for **small discharges** in laboratories/industry | Used for **large flows** in rivers, canals, dams |
| Plate thickness is small | Structural thickness may be substantial |

The governing equations for discharge are the same in both cases.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper]**
**State the empirical equation for the discharge over a rectangular notch. What is the meaning of the coefficient of discharge?**

**Model Answer:**

The discharge over a **rectangular notch (suppressed)** is given by:

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} \, C_d \, b \, \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2}$$

where:
- $Q$ = discharge $[m^3/s]$, $b$ = notch width $[m]$, $H$ = head above crest $[m]$, $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$.

**Coefficient of discharge ($C_d$):** It is a dimensionless empirical factor (typically **0.62 to 0.66** for sharp-crested rectangular weirs) that accounts for the **vena contracta effect**, frictional losses, and turbulence in the flow. It represents the ratio of *actual discharge* to *theoretical discharge*:

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question contains two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Internal choice is provided between two full questions (Question A or Question B). You attempt **ONE full 14-mark question.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive an expression for the discharge through a **rectangular notch** with end contractions, and explain the significance of the coefficient of discharge. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rectangular notch 0.5 m wide is used to measure the discharge of water in a channel. The head of water over the notch is 150 mm. If the coefficient of discharge is 0.62, calculate the discharge. The notch is **suppressed** (no end contractions). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Step 1 — Consider a rectangular notch** of width $b$ in a thin vertical plate, with head $H$ of liquid above the crest.

**Step 2 — Elemental strip** of thickness $dh$ at depth $h$ below the free surface:

$$dA = b \cdot dh$$

**Step 3 — Velocity by Torricelli's theorem:**

$$v = \sqrt{2gh}$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical elemental discharge:**

$$dQ = v \cdot dA = b \sqrt{2g} \cdot \sqrt{h} \, dh$$

**Step 5 — Apply $C_d$:**

$$dQ = C_d \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot h^{1/2} \, dh$$

**Step 6 — Integrate from $h = 0$ to $h = H$:**

$$Q = C_d \, b \, \sqrt{2g} \int_0^H h^{1/2} \, dh = C_d \, b \, \sqrt{2g} \cdot \frac{2}{3} H^{3/2}$$

**Step 7 — With end contractions (Francis formula),** effective length:

$$L_e = b - 0.1 n H$$

where $n$ = number of end contractions. Hence:

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d (b - 0.1 n H) \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2}}$$

**Significance of $C_d$:** The theoretical derivation assumes ideal, frictionless flow, but in practice the jet contracts at the notch edge (**vena contracta**), there are frictional losses, and turbulence. $C_d$ (typically **0.62–0.66**) is a correction factor representing the ratio of actual to theoretical discharge.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Given:** $b = 0.5$ m, $H = 150$ mm $= 0.15$ m, $C_d = 0.62$, suppressed notch (n = 0).

**Step 1 — Apply the rectangular notch equation:**

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} \times C_d \times b \times \sqrt{2g} \times H^{3/2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.62 \times 0.5 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81} \times (0.15)^{3/2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\sqrt{2g}$:**

$$\sqrt{2g} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.429$$

**Step 4 — Compute $H^{3/2}$:**

$$(0.15)^{3/2} = 0.15 \times \sqrt{0.15} = 0.15 \times 0.3873 = 0.05809$$

**Step 5 — Multiply all terms:**

$$Q = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.62 \times 0.5 \times 4.429 \times 0.05809$$

$$Q = 0.4133 \times 4.429 \times 0.05809$$

$$Q = 1.831 \times 0.05809 = 0.1064 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 0.1064 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 106.4 \text{ L/s}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]

**(a)** Derive an expression for the discharge through a **triangular (V-notch) weir** having a total included angle $\theta$. Show that for a right-angled V-notch, the discharge is proportional to $H^{5/2}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A right-angled V-notch is used to measure a flow of water. The head over the notch is 200 mm. If $C_d = 0.6$, calculate the discharge in litres per second. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Step 1 — Geometry of the V-notch** of total angle $\theta$ (half-angle $\theta/2$):

A strip at depth $h$ below the free surface has its centre a vertical distance $(H - h)$ above the apex.

**Step 2 — Width of the strip:**

The walls of the V are at half-angle $\theta/2$ from the vertical. The half-width at height $(H-h)$ above the apex is $(H-h)\tan(\theta/2)$. Total width:

$$w = 2 (H - h) \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Strip area:**

$$dA = 2 (H - h) \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) dh$$

**Step 4 — Apply Torricelli and $C_d$:**

$$dQ = C_d \cdot \sqrt{2gh} \cdot 2 (H - h) \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) dh$$

**Step 5 — Integrate:**

$$Q = 2 C_d \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \int_0^H (H - h) h^{1/2} \, dh$$

**Step 6 — Expand and integrate:**

$$Q = 2 C_d \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \left[ H \cdot \frac{2}{3} H^{3/2} - \frac{2}{5} H^{5/2} \right]$$

**Step 7 — Simplify:**

$$Q = 2 C_d \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \cdot \frac{4}{15} H^{5/2}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{8}{15} C_d \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2}}$$

**Step 8 — For a right-angled V-notch** ($\theta = 90°$): $\tan(45°) = 1$:

$$Q = \frac{8}{15} C_d \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2}$$

Since $\frac{8}{15} C_d \sqrt{2g}$ is a constant, $Q \propto H^{5/2}$. **Q.E.D.**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Given:** Right-angled V-notch, $H = 200$ mm $= 0.2$ m, $C_d = 0.6$.

**Step 1 — Apply the right-angle V-notch formula:**

$$Q = \frac{8}{15} \times C_d \times \sqrt{2g} \times H^{5/2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\sqrt{2g}$:**

$$\sqrt{2g} = 4.429$$

**Step 3 — Compute $H^{5/2}$:**

$$H^{5/2} = (0.2)^{5/2} = (0.2)^2 \times \sqrt{0.2} = 0.04 \times 0.4472 = 0.01789$$

**Step 4 — Compute the constant factor:**

$$\frac{8}{15} \times 0.6 \times 4.429 = 0.5333 \times 0.6 \times 4.429 = 1.4174$$

**Step 5 — Multiply:**

$$Q = 1.4174 \times 0.01789 = 0.02536 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 25.36 \text{ L/s}}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Weirs and Area Meters**

- **Weir vs. Notch:** Often used interchangeably; notch is the small calibrated opening, weir is the larger structure.
- **Classification axes:** Shape (Rect/Tri/Trap), Crest (Sharp/Broad/Narrow/Ogee), End contractions (with/without — Francis formula), Discharge type (Free/Drowned).
- **Torricelli's Law** is the foundation: $v = \sqrt{2gh}$ where $h$ is depth below free surface.
- **Rectangular Notch (Suppressed):** $Q = \tfrac{2}{3} C_d \, b \sqrt{2g} \, H^{3/2}$.
- **Rectangular Notch (With End Contractions):** Replace $b$ with $L_e = b - 0.1nH$, where $n$ = number of end contractions.
- **V-Notch (General):** $Q = \tfrac{8}{15} C_d \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2}$.
- **V-Notch (Right-Angled):** $Q = 1.417 \, H^{5/2}$ (with $C_d = 0.6$ and $g = 9.81$).
- **Head Exponent:** Rectangular → **1.5**, Triangular → **2.5** — this is why V-notches are superior for *small* flows.
- **Typical $C_d$ values:** Rectangular ≈ **0.62**, V-notch ≈ **0.60**, Cipolletti ≈ **0.63**.
- **Francis formula constants:** $L_e = b - 0.1nH$ where each end contraction reduces effective width by $0.1H$.
- **Rotameter** = variable-area flow meter with vertical tapered tube + free float.
- **Rotameter force balance:** Drag force = effective weight of float → yields the discharge equation.
- **Rotameter formula:** $Q = C_d A \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 g V_f (\rho_f - \rho)}{A_f \rho} }$ where $A$ is the **annular** area $(A_t - A_f)$.
- **Rotameter reading** is taken at the **largest cross-section of the float** (the widest "drum" of the bob).
- **Always use $g = 9.81$ m/s²** in KTU numericals (not 10) unless specified.
- **Always convert head to metres** before substituting into the formula.
- **V-notch is preferred for low flows**; rectangular for high flows.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Local Velocity Measurement: Pitot Tube: Principle and Equations, Resistance of Immersed Bodies-Concept of Drag and Drag Coefficient
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT403 - FLUID FLOW AND PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Local Velocity Measurement: Pitot Tube: Principle and Equations, Resistance of Immersed Bodies-Concept of Drag and Drag Coefficient # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Local Velocity Measurement – Pitot Tube ### Formal Definition A **Pitot Tube** is a **local velocity measurement device** used in fluid mechanics to determine the **point (local) velocity of a flowing fluid** at a specific location within a streamline. It operates on the principle of converting the **kinetic energy (dynamic pressure)** of the fluid into **pressure energy (static pressure)** by bringing the fluid to a **stagnation point**. The instrument consists of two concentric tubes: - An **inner tube (impact/pressure tube)** with an opening facing directly into the flow, which measures the **stagnation (total) pressure**. - An **outer tube (static tube)** with side openings, which measures the **static pressure** of the fluid. - A combined version called the **Pitot-Static Tube** measures the difference between the two. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 3):** The Pitot Tube is strictly a *local* velocity measurement device — it does NOT measure average velocity across a pipe cross-section. This is a frequent exam distinction. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine sticking your hand out of a moving car's window. When you **face your palm directly into the wind**, you feel a strong push — that push is the *stagnation pressure* (the fluid has been brought to rest against your palm). When you **turn your palm parallel to the wind**, you barely feel a push — that's the *static pressure*. A Pitot tube essentially does this: it *catches* the moving fluid at one opening and *ignores* the flow at side openings. The **difference** between these two pressures tells us how fast the fluid was moving. --- ## 1.2 Resistance of Immersed Bodies – Drag and Drag Coefficient ### Formal Definition **Drag** is the **net hydrodynamic force exerted by a flowing fluid on a solid body immersed (placed) in the flow path**, acting in the direction of the fluid motion (parallel to the flow). It is the resistance force that the fluid offers to the relative motion of the body. The **Drag Coefficient (C_D)** is a **dimensionless quantity** that quantifies the drag or resistance of a body in a fluid environment. It depends on: - The shape of the body - The Reynolds Number of the flow - The surface roughness - The degree of turbulence in the free stream $$C_D = \frac{F_D}{\frac{1}{2} \rho v^2 A}$$ where: - $F_D$ = Drag Force (N) - $\rho$ = Fluid density (kg/m³) - $v$ = Velocity of the body relative to fluid (m/s) - $A$ = Projected area (m²) ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of trying to **run through a swimming pool versus running through open air**. The water resists your movement much more — that resistance is "drag." Now imagine two runners: one holding a **streamlined kayak paddle** flat against their chest, and the other holding a **large beach umbrella** open. The umbrella runner will experience *much more drag* because of their shape, even at the same speed. The **drag coefficient** is a number that tells engineers "how much drag a given shape produces." A perfect streamlined teardrop has $C_D \approx 0.04$; a flat plate facing the wind has $C_D \approx 1.28$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Distinction (KTU-Favorite Question):** > - **Pitot Tube** → measures *flow* velocity (stationary body, moving fluid). > - **Drag Coefficient** → measures *body's resistance* to a moving fluid (moving body, or equivalently fluid flowing past a stationary body). > Both use the same physical principle (Bernoulli's equation / momentum transfer) but in inverted applications. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Pitot Tube – Operational Principle ### The Physics of Measurement A Pitot tube relies on **two simultaneous phenomena** occurring at a stagnation point: 1. **Conversion of Kinetic Energy to Pressure Energy:** When a fluid stream is brought to rest at the tube's opening, its velocity reduces to zero. By the principle of **conservation of energy (Bernoulli's equation)**, the kinetic energy lost must appear as an increase in pressure (stagnation pressure). 2. **Conservation of Mass and Energy:** Since the fluid enters the tube smoothly and decelerates isothermally (assuming incompressible flow), the **total mechanical energy is conserved** along the streamline from the free stream to the stagnation point. ### Step-by-Step Working Logic 1. Fluid flows with free-stream velocity $v$ at point (1) in the main flow. 2. The Pitot tube is inserted such that its bent tip points **directly into the oncoming flow**. 3. At the **mouth of the Pitot tube (stagnation point)**, the fluid decelerates from $v$ to $0$. 4. The kinetic energy $\frac{1}{2}mv^2$ per unit volume converts to a pressure rise $P_2 - P_1$. 5. A manometer or pressure transducer connected to the tube measures this pressure difference. 6. Using Bernoulli's equation, the unknown $v$ is calculated from the measured pressure. ### Assumptions in the Derivation - Fluid is **incompressible** (low Mach number, $M < 0.3$). - Flow is **steady, laminar/irrotational** along the streamline to the stagnation point. - **No friction losses** between point (1) and stagnation point (2). - The **stagnation point** is on the central streamline of the tube. ### Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aircraft Pitot Tubes (Air Data Computers):** Measure airspeed via the stagnation pressure on the aircraft nose. - **Wind Tunnels:** Measure local air velocity in aerodynamic testing. - **Hydraulic Engineering:** Measure flow velocity in rivers, open channels, and large pipes. - **HVAC Systems:** Verify airflow in ventilation ducts. --- ## 2.2 Drag Force – Components and Physics ### Two Major Components of Drag 1. **Form Drag (Pressure Drag):** Caused by the **pressure difference** between the front (upstream) and rear (downstream) faces of the body. It dominates for **bluff bodies** (cylinders, flat plates, spheres at high Re). 2. **Skin Friction Drag (Viscous Drag):** Caused by the **tangential shear stress** on the surface of the body due to fluid viscosity. It dominates for **streamlined bodies** (aerofoils, fish shapes). $$F_D = F_{D,\text{form}} + F_{D,\text{skin friction}}$$ ### Factors Affecting Drag Coefficient - **Reynolds Number ($Re$):** $Re = \frac{\rho v d}{\mu}$ — Strongly affects $C_D$. - **Body Geometry:** Spheres, cylinders, discs, streamlined bodies all have distinct $C_D$ vs $Re$ curves. - **Surface Roughness:** Affects boundary layer transition from laminar to turbulent. - **Compressibility Effects:** Important at high Mach numbers. - **Turbulence Intensity of Free Stream:** Higher freestream turbulence reduces $C_D$ for bluff bodies. ### Real-World Utility - **Vehicle Design:** Minimizing $C_D$ for fuel efficiency (Tesla $C_D \approx 0.24$, trucks $C_D \approx 0.6$). - **Bridge/Pylon Design:** Wind loading on cylindrical structures. - **Sports Engineering:** Swimsuit and bicycle design (the infamous 2008 Speedo LZR Racer banned from Olympics). - **Particle Settling (KTU – Module 3 Link!):** Drag coefficient determines terminal settling velocity in fluidization and sedimentation. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2$ | Bernoulli's equation (incompressible, no losses) | Pa | | 2 | $v = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_2 - P_1)}{\rho}}$ | **Pitot tube velocity equation** (ideal) | m/s | | 3 | $v = C \sqrt{\frac{2 g h (R - 1)}{1}} = C \sqrt{2 g R h}$ | Pitot tube with **mercury manometer** | m/s | | 4 | $v = C \sqrt{\frac{2 g h (\rho_m/\rho_f - 1)}{1}}$ | Pitot tube with **differential manometer** (general) | m/s | | 5 | $v = C \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta h}{\rho_f (1 - A_2^2/A_1^2)}}$ | Pitot tube with **piezometer** (static + stagnation) | m/s | | 6 | $F_D = C_D \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho v^2 A$ | **Drag force definition** | N | | 7 | $C_D = \frac{F_D}{\frac{1}{2} \rho v^2 A}$ | **Drag coefficient definition** | dimensionless | | 8 | $Re = \frac{\rho v d}{\mu} = \frac{v d}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number | dimensionless | | 9 | $C_D = \frac{24}{Re}$ | Stokes' regime ($Re < 0.1$, sphere) | dimensionless | | 10 | $C_D = \frac{18.5}{Re^{0.6}}$ | Intermediate regime ($0.1 < Re < 1000$, sphere) | dimensionless | | 11 | $C_D \approx 0.44$ | Newton's regime ($10^3 < Re < 2 \times 10^5$, sphere) | dimensionless | | 12 | $C_D \approx 1.28$ | Flat plate (broadside), all $Re$ | dimensionless | | 13 | $C_D \approx 0.3 - 0.4$ | Streamlined body | dimensionless | | 14 | $v_t = \sqrt{\frac{2 m g}{C_D \rho A}}$ | Terminal velocity of falling particle | m/s | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitot Tube Equation Variants:** Students often confuse the manometer form. Memorize both the **piezometer** and **differential manometer** versions — both are favorite exam questions. > [!IMPORTANT] > The factor $A_2^2/A_1^2$ in equation (5) is a **velocity of approach correction**, often neglected but **valid KTU exam material** for full marks. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Pitot Tube Working Equation (Ideal Case) ### Setup Consider a fluid flowing with **free-stream velocity $v_1$** at point (1) in the main flow. The Pitot tube is inserted such that its tip (point 2) lies on the **same streamline** as point (1) and is brought to **stagnation**, meaning $v_2 = 0$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's equation** between point (1) (free stream) and point (2) (stagnation point inside tube), assuming both points lie on the **same horizontal plane** ($z_1 = z_2$): $$\begin{aligned} P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 + \rho g z_1 &= P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2 + \rho g z_2 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute $z_1 = z_2$ and $v_2 = 0$** (the fluid is brought to rest at the stagnation point): $$\begin{aligned} P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 + \rho g z_1 &= P_2 + 0 + \rho g z_1 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — The potential energy terms cancel** from both sides: $$\begin{aligned} P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 &= P_2 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Define stagnation pressure** $P_0 = P_2$ and rearrange for velocity $v_1$: $$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 &= P_2 - P_1 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Solve for $v_1$:** $$\begin{aligned} v_1^2 &= \frac{2(P_2 - P_1)}{\rho} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6 — Take the square root for the final ideal Pitot tube equation:** $$\begin{aligned} \boxed{v_1 = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_2 - P_1)}{\rho}}} \end{aligned}$$ where: - $v_1$ = free-stream velocity (m/s) — *what we want* - $P_2$ = stagnation pressure at tube mouth (Pa) - $P_1$ = static pressure in the free stream (Pa) - $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³) > **Logic Note:** This derivation assumes an **ideal** Pitot tube (frictionless, inviscid flow). For real fluids, a **discharge coefficient $C$** is introduced: > $$\begin{aligned} v_1 = C \sqrt{\frac{2(P_2 - P_1)}{\rho}} \end{aligned}$$ > where $C \approx 0.98$ to $1.00$ for well-designed Pitot tubes. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Pitot Tube with Differential Mercury Manometer ### Setup A **U-tube differential manometer** containing mercury (density $\rho_m$) is connected between the **stagnation pressure tapping** (point 2) and the **static pressure tapping** (point 1) of a Pitot-static tube. The manometer shows a **deflection $h$** of mercury. The flowing fluid has density $\rho_f$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Apply manometer equation.** Starting at point (1) in the fluid, traversing down to the mercury interface, across the mercury, and up to point (2): $$\begin{aligned} P_1 + \rho_f g (h + x) - \rho_m g h - \rho_f g x = P_2 \end{aligned}$$ where $x$ is the height of fluid above the lower mercury level (this cancels in next step). **Step 2 — Simplify by canceling $\rho_f g x$:** $$\begin{aligned} P_1 + \rho_f g h - \rho_m g h &= P_2 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange to obtain the pressure difference:** $$\begin{aligned} P_2 - P_1 &= (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute into the ideal Pitot tube equation:** $$\begin{aligned} v_1 &= \sqrt{\frac{2(P_2 - P_1)}{\rho_f}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute the manometer expression:** $$\begin{aligned} v_1 &= \sqrt{\frac{2 (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h}{\rho_f}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6 — Factor out $\rho_f$ and define specific gravity $S = \rho_m/\rho_f$:** $$\begin{aligned} v_1 &= \sqrt{\frac{2 g h (\rho_m - \rho_f)}{\rho_f}} = \sqrt{2 g h \left(\frac{\rho_m}{\rho_f} - 1\right)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 7 — Final compact form with manometer fluid specific gravity $S_m$:** $$\begin{aligned} \boxed{v_1 = \sqrt{2 g h (S_m - 1)}} \end{aligned}$$ For a mercury manometer with $S_m = 13.6$ and water as the flowing fluid, this simplifies to $v_1 = \sqrt{2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot h \cdot 12.6} = \sqrt{247.3 \, h}$. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Drag Force from Buckingham Pi Theorem ### Why the Drag Coefficient Exists **Step 1 — List the variables that affect drag force on a body:** - $F_D$ = drag force (M L T⁻²) - $v$ = velocity (L T⁻¹) - $L$ = characteristic length (L) - $\rho$ = fluid density (M L⁻³) - $\mu$ = fluid viscosity (M L⁻¹ T⁻¹) **Step 2 — Number of variables: $n = 5$. Number of fundamental dimensions: $m = 3$ (M, L, T).** **Step 3 — Number of dimensionless groups:** $n - m = 5 - 3 = 2$ groups. **Step 4 — Choose repeating variables:** $\rho$, $v$, $L$. **Step 5 — Form Π₁** using $F_D$: $$\begin{aligned} \Pi_1 &= F_D \cdot \rho^a v^b L^c \end{aligned}$$ Equating dimensions: $M^0 L^0 T^0 = (MLT^{-2})(ML^{-3})^a (LT^{-1})^b (L)^c$ - M: $1 + a = 0 \Rightarrow a = -1$ - T: $-2 - b = 0 \Rightarrow b = -2$ - L: $1 - 3a + b + c = 0 \Rightarrow 1 + 3 - 2 + c = 0 \Rightarrow c = -2$ Therefore: $\Pi_1 = \frac{F_D}{\rho v^2 L^2}$ **Step 6 — Form Π₂** using $\mu$: $$\begin{aligned} \Pi_2 &= \mu \cdot \rho^a v^b L^c \end{aligned}$$ Equating dimensions: $M^0 L^0 T^0 = (ML^{-1}T^{-1})(ML^{-3})^a (LT^{-1})^b (L)^c$ - M: $1 + a = 0 \Rightarrow a = -1$ - T: $-1 - b = 0 \Rightarrow b = -1$ - L: $-1 - 3a + b + c = 0 \Rightarrow -1 + 3 - 1 + c = 0 \Rightarrow c = -1$ Therefore: $\Pi_2 = \frac{\mu}{\rho v L} = \frac{1}{Re}$ **Step 7 — Final functional relationship:** $$\begin{aligned} \Pi_1 &= \phi(\Pi_2) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{F_D}{\rho v^2 L^2} = \phi\left(\frac{1}{Re}\right) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 8 — Define drag coefficient** $C_D = \phi(1/Re)$ and projected area $A \propto L^2$: $$\begin{aligned} \boxed{F_D = C_D \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho v^2 A} \end{aligned}$$ > **Logic Note:** The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ in $C_D$ is a historical convention (originated in aerodynamics). The dimensionless form is **why $C_D$ values are reported independent of fluid, velocity, or size** — a $C_D$ of 0.44 for a sphere is valid in air, water, or oil if $Re$ matches. --- ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Standard Style) ### Problem A Pitot-static tube is used to measure the velocity of water flowing in a pipe. A differential mercury manometer connected to the Pitot-static tube shows a deflection of $h = 10$ cm. Calculate the velocity of water at the point where the Pitot tube is placed. **Given:** - $h = 10$ cm $= 0.1$ m - $\rho_{Hg} = 13600$ kg/m³ - $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³ - $g = 9.81$ m/s² - $C = 1$ (assumed ideal) ### Solution **Step 1 — Use the Pitot tube equation with manometer correction:** $$\begin{aligned} v &= C \sqrt{\frac{2 g h (\rho_{Hg}/\rho_{water} - 1)}{1}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the specific gravity of mercury with respect to water:** $$\begin{aligned} S &= \frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_{water}} = \frac{13600}{1000} = 13.6 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute all numerical values:** $$\begin{aligned} v &= 1 \cdot \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.1 \times (13.6 - 1)} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} v &= \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.1 \times 12.6} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} v &= \sqrt{24.72} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \boxed{v &= 4.97 \text{ m/s}} \end{aligned}$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Pitot Tube Functional Architecture and Energy Conversion ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Free Stream Fluid
Velocity v1, Pressure P1] --> B[Approaches Pitot Tube Mouth] B --> C[Impact Tube Inlet
Faces Oncoming Flow] C --> D[Stagnation Point
Velocity becomes ZERO] D --> E[Fluid Decelerates
Kinetic Energy to Pressure Energy] E --> F[Total Stagnation Pressure P2
measured at impact tube] A --> G[Static Pressure Tapping
on side of outer tube] G --> H[Static Pressure P1
measured at static tube] F --> I[Differential Manometer
or Pressure Transducer] H --> I I --> J[Apply Bernoulli Equation
P1 + half rho v1 sq = P2] J --> K[Calculate v1 = sqrt of 2 P2 minus P1 over rho] K --> L[Apply Discharge Coefficient C
Real fluid correction] L --> M[Final Local Velocity v1] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Drag Force Components Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Immersed Body in Flow] --> B[Two Drag Mechanisms] B --> C[Form Drag or Pressure Drag] B --> D[Skin Friction Drag or Viscous Drag] C --> E[Pressure Difference
Front face to Rear face] C --> F[Flow Separation
Wake Formation] C --> G[Bluff Bodies
Cylinders, Flat Plates, Discs] D --> H[Shear Stress on Surface] D --> I[Boundary Layer Effects] D --> J[Streamlined Bodies
Aerofoils, Fish Shapes] E --> K[Total Drag Force FD] F --> K H --> K I --> K G --> L[Reynolds Number Regimes] J --> L L --> M[Stokes Regimen Re under 0.1] L --> N[Intermediate Re 0.1 to 1000] L --> O[Newton Regimen Re 1000 to 200000] L --> P[Post Critical Re above 200000] M --> Q[Drag Coefficient CD] N --> Q O --> Q P --> Q K --> R[FD = CD times half rho v sq A] Q --> R ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Pitot-Static Tube Construction ```mermaid flowchart LR A[L shaped Inner Tube
Impact or Stagnation Tube] --> B[Open Tip Faces Flow
Measures Stagnation Pressure] C[Outer Coaxial Tube
Static Tube] --> D[Side Openings Perpendicular to Flow
Measures Static Pressure] B --> E[Stem connects to Manometer Limb 1] D --> F[Annular space connects to Manometer Limb 2] E --> G[Differential U Tube Manometer
with Mercury or other heavy fluid] F --> G G --> H[Deflection h indicates Dynamic Pressure
h into fluid property gives local velocity] ``` > **KTU Note:** The above are **block-level functional architecture diagrams** representing the flow of information (not physical shapes). For KTU board exam diagrams, students are expected to draw: > 1. A labeled cross-section showing the **L-shaped inner tube** and **straight outer tube** with arrows. > 2. The **U-tube manometer** connected to the two pressure tappings. > 3. Streamlines bending into the stagnation point. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Pitot Tube and State its Working Equation `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** A Pitot tube is a device used for the **local measurement of fluid velocity** at a point in a stream. It is based on the principle that the **kinetic energy of the fluid is converted into pressure energy** at a **stagnation point**, where the fluid is brought to rest. **Working Equation:** $$\begin{aligned} v &= C \sqrt{\frac{2(P_2 - P_1)}{\rho}} \end{aligned}$$ where $P_2$ is the stagnation (total) pressure, $P_1$ is the static pressure, $\rho$ is the fluid density, and $C$ is the discharge coefficient (close to 1.0 for well-designed tubes). --- ### Question 2: Define Drag Coefficient. What are its SI Units? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The **drag coefficient $C_D$** is a dimensionless number that quantifies the resistance of a body to motion through a fluid. It is defined as the ratio of the drag force to the dynamic pressure times a characteristic area: $$\begin{aligned} C_D = \frac{F_D}{\frac{1}{2} \rho v^2 A} \end{aligned}$$ **SI Units:** $C_D$ is **dimensionless** (no units). The drag coefficient depends on the **Reynolds number**, **body geometry**, **surface roughness**, and **Mach number**. For a smooth sphere, $C_D$ varies from very high values at low $Re$ ($\sim 24/Re$ for Stokes flow) to about 0.44 in Newton's regime. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern) ### Question A — Full 14-Mark Question `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 3]` **(a)** Derive the working equation of a Pitot tube for measuring local fluid velocity. State clearly the assumptions involved. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Pitot-static tube is placed at the center of a 30 cm diameter pipe carrying water. A differential mercury manometer connected to the tube shows a deflection of 8 cm. Calculate: - (i) The local velocity at the center. - (ii) The volumetric flow rate, assuming the velocity profile follows the relation $v = v_{max} \left(1 - \frac{r}{R}\right)^{1/7}$. - (Take $C = 1$, $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³, $\rho_{Hg} = 13600$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².) **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** **Assumptions:** 1. The fluid is **incompressible** and flows **steadily**. 2. The flow is **inviscid** (no friction) between the free stream and stagnation point. 3. The Pitot tube is aligned with the flow and the stagnation point is on the **central streamline**. 4. Points (1) and (2) lie on the **same horizontal plane** ($z_1 = z_2$). **Derivation:** Applying **Bernoulli's equation** between the free-stream point (1) and the stagnation point (2): $$\begin{aligned} P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 + \rho g z_1 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2 + \rho g z_2 \end{aligned}$$ Substituting $z_1 = z_2$ and $v_2 = 0$: $$\begin{aligned} P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 = P_2 \end{aligned}$$ Rearranging: $$\begin{aligned} v_1 = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_2 - P_1)}{\rho}} \end{aligned}$$ With the discharge coefficient $C$: $$\begin{aligned} \boxed{v_1 = C \sqrt{\frac{2(P_2 - P_1)}{\rho}}} \end{aligned}$$ **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **(i) Local velocity at center:** The manometer equation gives: $$\begin{aligned} P_2 - P_1 &= (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_{water}) g h \\ &= (13600 - 1000) \times 9.81 \times 0.08 \\ &= 12600 \times 9.81 \times 0.08 \\ &= 9888.48 \text{ Pa} \end{aligned}$$ Local velocity: $$\begin{aligned} v_{max} &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 9888.48}{1000}} = \sqrt{19.777} = 4.447 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) Volumetric flow rate using 1/7th power law:** For a pipe with the 1/7th power velocity profile, the **ratio of average to maximum velocity** is: $$\begin{aligned} \frac{v_{avg}}{v_{max}} &= \frac{49}{60} = 0.8167 \end{aligned}$$ Therefore: $$\begin{aligned} v_{avg} &= 0.8167 \times 4.447 = 3.632 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned}$$ Cross-sectional area of the pipe: $$\begin{aligned} A &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.3)^2 = 0.0707 \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned}$$ Volumetric flow rate: $$\begin{aligned} Q &= A \times v_{avg} = 0.0707 \times 3.632 = 0.2568 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 256.8 \text{ L/s} \end{aligned}$$ --- ### Question B — Alternative 14-Mark Question `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** Explain the concept of **drag force** and **drag coefficient** for an immersed body. Discuss how the drag coefficient varies with Reynolds number for a **smooth sphere**, with the help of a labeled sketch. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A spherical particle of diameter 2 mm and density 2500 kg/m³ is settling under gravity in air at 30°C ($\rho_{air} = 1.164$ kg/m³, $\mu_{air} = 1.85 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s). Calculate the **terminal settling velocity** of the particle. Use the appropriate drag coefficient correlation for the relevant Reynolds number regime. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B **Part (a) — Concept Explanation (7 marks):** **Drag Force:** When a body is immersed in a moving fluid, the fluid exerts a **resistive force** on the body in the direction of fluid flow. This force is called the **drag force** $F_D$. Drag has two components: - **Form (pressure) drag** — due to pressure differences and wake formation. - **Skin friction (viscous) drag** — due to shear stresses on the surface. **Drag Coefficient:** $$\begin{aligned} C_D = \frac{F_D}{\frac{1}{2} \rho v^2 A} \end{aligned}$$ **$C_D$ vs $Re$ for a Smooth Sphere** (3 distinct regimes): | Regime | Reynolds Number Range | Drag Coefficient | |--------|----------------------|------------------| | **Stokes' (creeping) flow** | $Re < 0.1$ | $C_D = 24/Re$ | | **Intermediate (Allen) flow** | $0.1 < Re < 1000$ | $C_D = 18.5/Re^{0.6}$ | | **Newton's regime** | $1000 < Re < 2 \times 10^5$ | $C_D \approx 0.44$ (constant) | | **Post-critical regime** | $Re > 2 \times 10^5$ | $C_D$ drops sharply to $\approx 0.1$ | The drop at the **critical Reynolds number** is due to **boundary layer transition** from laminar to turbulent — a turbulent boundary layer resists flow separation better, reducing the wake and the pressure drag dramatically. **Part (b) — Terminal Velocity (7 marks):** **Given:** $d_p = 2$ mm $= 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\rho_p = 2500$ kg/m³, $\rho_f = 1.164$ kg/m³, $\mu_f = 1.85 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s. **Step 1 — Assume Stokes' regime initially** and check $Re$ at the end. For Stokes' flow: $$\begin{aligned} v_t = \frac{(\rho_p - \rho_f) g d_p^2}{18 \mu} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} v_t = \frac{(2500 - 1.164) \times 9.81 \times (2 \times 10^{-3})^2}{18 \times 1.85 \times 10^{-5}} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} v_t = \frac{2498.84 \times 9.81 \times 4 \times 10^{-6}}{3.33 \times 10^{-4}} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} v_t = \frac{0.09804}{3.33 \times 10^{-4}} = 294.4 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate $Re$ to verify the regime:** $$\begin{aligned} Re &= \frac{\rho_f v_t d_p}{\mu_f} = \frac{1.164 \times 294.4 \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}{1.85 \times 10^{-5}} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} Re &= \frac{0.6854}{1.85 \times 10^{-5}} = 37,050 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — $Re \approx 3.7 \times 10^4$ falls in Newton's regime** ($10^3 < Re < 2 \times 10^5$), so $C_D = 0.44$. **Step 4 — Re-calculate using Newton's regime formula:** $$\begin{aligned} v_t = \sqrt{\frac{2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g d_p}{C_D \rho_f}} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} v_t = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 2498.84 \times 9.81 \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}{0.44 \times 1.164}} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} v_t = \sqrt{\frac{98.04}{0.5122}} = \sqrt{191.4} = 13.84 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Verify $Re$ again:** $$\begin{aligned} Re = \frac{1.164 \times 13.84 \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}{1.85 \times 10^{-5}} = 1742 \end{aligned}$$ This is borderline — still in Newton's regime. So $v_t \approx 13.84$ m/s. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting the discharge coefficient $C$** in the Pitot tube equation — even though KTU often takes $C=1$, you should **state it explicitly** to score full marks. > 2. **Using the wrong manometer equation** — for a U-tube mercury manometer, the formula is $v = \sqrt{2gh(S_m - 1)}$, NOT $v = \sqrt{2gh \cdot S_m}$. The "$-1$" term is **frequently forgotten**, costing 2-3 marks. > 3. **Using average velocity profile ratio of 0.5** for a turbulent profile in 1/7th power law pipe flow. The correct ratio is **49/60 = 0.8167**, not 0.5 (which is for laminar parabolic flow). KTU examiners will deduct 1-2 marks for this. > 4. **For unit consistency** in numerical problems — always convert cm to m and mm to m BEFORE substituting into the equation. Marks lost in dimensional inconsistency are unforgiving. > 5. **Drag coefficient definition confusion** — $C_D$ is defined with $\frac{1}{2}\rho v^2 A$, NOT $\rho v^2 A$. The $\frac{1}{2}$ is **historically conventioned** and is non-negotiable. > 6. **Not verifying the assumed regime** when calculating terminal velocity — always compute $Re$ at the end to confirm the regime used. This shows engineering rigor and earns full method marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### Pitot Tube – Quick Recall - A Pitot tube measures **local point velocity**, not average flow rate. - **Principle:** Conversion of kinetic energy to pressure energy at a stagnation point. - **Ideal Equation:** $v = \sqrt{2(P_2 - P_1)/\rho}$ - **With manometer (mercury):** $v = \sqrt{2gh(S_m - 1)}$ - **Discharge coefficient $C \approx 0.98$ to $1.00$** for well-designed tubes. - **Assumptions:** Steady, incompressible, inviscid, same horizontal level. ### Drag and Drag Coefficient – Quick Recall - **Drag $F_D$** = resistive force on a body immersed in a fluid flow. - **Two components:** Form (pressure) drag + Skin friction (viscous) drag. - **Drag Coefficient $C_D$:** Dimensionless number relating drag to dynamic pressure. - **Formula:** $F_D = C_D \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2 A$ - **$A$** = projected (frontal) area perpendicular to flow. - **Stokes' regime ($Re < 0.1$):** $C_D = 24/Re$ — dominant in fine particle settling. - **Intermediate regime:** $C_D = 18.5/Re^{0.6}$ - **Newton's regime ($10^3 < Re < 2 \times 10^5$):** $C_D \approx 0.44$ for sphere. - **Critical drop at $Re \approx 2 \times 10^5$** due to laminar-to-turbulent boundary layer transition. - **Bluff bodies** (flat plate) have high $C_D \approx 1.28$; **streamlined bodies** have low $C_D \approx 0.04$. ### Cross-Links to Other KTU Modules - **Module 2 (Bernoulli's Equation):** Direct foundation for Pitot tube derivation. - **Module 3 (Reynolds Number, Boundary Layer):** Determines $C_D$ regime. - **Module 4 (Sedimentation, Fluidization):** Terminal velocity uses $C_D$ for particle drag. ### KTU 2024 High-Yield Tips - Memorize **both forms** of the Pitot tube equation (pressure form + manometer form). - Practice **at least 2 numerical problems** with mercury and water as the fluid pair. - Always **re-verify** the Reynolds number regime when solving terminal velocity problems. - Know the **drag coefficient curve for a sphere** — it is a guaranteed 7-mark question in some form.

topics.title: Variation of Drag Coefficient with Particle Reynolds Number, Free Settling-Motion of Particle Under Gravitational and Centrifugal Fields, Terminal Settling Velocity and Stokes' Law, Hindered Settling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT403 - FLUID FLOW AND PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Variation of Drag Coefficient with Particle Reynolds Number, Free Settling-Motion of Particle Under Gravitational and Centrifugal Fields, Terminal Settling Velocity and Stokes' Law, Hindered Settling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Sedimentation & Particle Dynamics

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Drag Coefficient ($C_D$) — Formal Definition

The **Drag Coefficient ($C_D$)** is a dimensionless quantity that quantifies the resistance experienced by a particle moving through a fluid medium. It is formally defined as the ratio of the drag force ($F_D$) to the product of the fluid's kinetic energy per unit volume and the projected area of the particle.

$$C_D = \frac{F_D}{\frac{1}{2} \rho u^2 A_p} = \frac{2 F_D}{\rho u^2 A_p}$$

Where:
- $F_D$ = Drag force (N)
- $\rho$ = Density of the fluid (kg/m³)
- $u$ = Relative velocity between particle and fluid (m/s)
- $A_p$ = Projected area of the particle (m²)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The drag coefficient is **NOT a constant** — it is a strong function of the **Particle Reynolds Number ($Re_p$)**, defined as $Re_p = \dfrac{D_p \, u \, \rho}{\mu}$, where $D_p$ is the particle diameter.

### 1.2 Particle Reynolds Number ($Re_p$) — Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Real-World):** Imagine a small soap bubble rising through water. If the water is viscous (like honey), the bubble rises *very slowly* — viscous forces dominate. If the water is thin and fast-moving, the bubble rises *quickly* — inertial forces dominate. The **Particle Reynolds Number** is simply the ratio of these inertial to viscous forces acting on a particle.

$$Re_p = \frac{D_p u \rho}{\mu} = \frac{\rho u D_p}{\mu} = \frac{\text{Inertial Forces}}{\text{Viscous Forces}}$$

- **Low $Re_p$ (< 0.1):** Viscous (friction) forces dominate → Flow streams smoothly around the particle (Laminar flow).
