- **High $Re_p$ (> 1000):** Inertial forces dominate → Turbulent wake forms behind the particle.
- **Intermediate $Re_p$:** Transition region where both forces are significant.

### 1.3 Free Settling — Formal Definition

**Free Settling** refers to the motion of a single particle through a fluid under the influence of an external body force, **without any physical contact or interference** from other particles, container walls, or external obstructions.

The two primary external fields considered in KTU 2024 syllabus are:

1. **Gravitational Field** — Particle settles under Earth's gravity (e.g., dust settling in air, sand settling in water).
2. **Centrifugal Field** — Particle moves radially outward under centrifugal force in a rotating system (e.g., cyclone separators, centrifuges).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Distinction:** *Free* settling assumes the particle is isolated. *Hindered* settling occurs when particle concentration is high enough that neighboring particles interfere with one another's motion.

### 1.4 Terminal Settling Velocity — Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** When a skydiver jumps from a plane, they first accelerate due to gravity. But as their speed increases, air resistance (drag) also increases. Eventually, drag equals gravity, and they fall at a **constant speed** — this is their **terminal velocity**. The same physics applies to a sand particle settling in water!

$$v_t = \text{Terminal Settling Velocity (m/s)}$$

At terminal velocity, the **net force on the particle = 0**, meaning driving force = drag force + buoyancy force.

### 1.5 Stokes' Law — Formal Statement

**Stokes' Law** gives the drag force on a spherical particle moving at low Reynolds number ($Re_p < 0.1$) through a viscous fluid:

$$F_D = 3 \pi \mu D_p v_t$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Stokes' Law is valid **ONLY** in the laminar flow regime where $Re_p < 0.1$. Outside this range, the drag force no longer varies linearly with velocity.

### 1.6 Hindered Settling — Definition

**Hindered Settling** occurs when the concentration of solid particles in the fluid is sufficiently high that:
- Upward flow of fluid displaced by settling particles creates an increased effective drag.
- Particle-particle interactions (collisions, wake effects) become significant.
- The settling velocity of each particle is **reduced** compared to free settling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine trying to walk through a crowded market versus an empty hallway. The crowd "hinders" your motion — exactly like neighboring particles hinder each other in a dense suspension.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Forces Acting on a Settling Particle

Three primary forces govern particle motion in a fluid:

| Force | Symbol | Formula | Physical Meaning |
|-------|--------|---------|------------------|
| **Gravitational Force** (Weight) | $F_g$ | $F_g = \frac{\pi}{6} D_p^3 \rho_p \, g$ | Pulls particle downward |
| **Buoyant Force** | $F_b$ | $F_b = \frac{\pi}{6} D_p^3 \rho \, g$ | Fluid pushes particle upward |
| **Drag Force** | $F_D$ | $F_D = C_D \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D_p^2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho u^2$ | Fluid resistance to motion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Net Driving Force (Gravitational Settling):**
> $$F_{net} = F_g - F_b = \frac{\pi}{6} D_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho) g$$
> This is the force that accelerates the particle. When drag equals this net force, terminal velocity is reached.

## 2.2 Variation of Drag Coefficient with Particle Reynolds Number

The relationship $C_D = f(Re_p)$ is depicted by the **Standard Drag Curve** (a fundamental plot in particle technology). It has three distinct regions:

### Region 1: Stokes' Law Regime (Laminar Flow)
- **Range:** $Re_p < 0.1$ (or $Re_p < 1$ for engineering approximation)
- **Equation:** $C_D = \dfrac{24}{Re_p}$
- **Behavior:** $C_D$ decreases sharply as $Re_p$ increases (inverse relationship).
- **Physics:** Viscous forces dominate. Flow is laminar. Drag is purely due to viscous friction.

### Region 2: Intermediate Regime (Transition)
- **Range:** $0.1 < Re_p < 1000$
- **Equation:** $C_D = \dfrac{18.5}{Re_p^{0.6}}$ (one common correlation)
- **Behavior:** $C_D$ reaches a minimum and then begins to rise.
- **Physics:** Boundary layer separation begins. A turbulent wake forms behind the particle.

### Region 3: Newton's Law Regime (Turbulent Flow)
- **Range:** $1000 < Re_p < 2 \times 10^5$
- **Equation:** $C_D \approx 0.44$ (approximately constant)
- **Behavior:** $C_D$ is essentially constant.
- **Physics:** Inertial forces dominate completely. Drag is due to form/pressure drag, not friction.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always identify the $Re_p$ regime FIRST before applying any drag equation. This is the most common source of calculation errors.

## 2.3 Free Settling Under Gravitational Field

When a particle settles under gravity in a fluid:

**Force balance at terminal velocity:**
$$F_g = F_b + F_D$$

$$\frac{\pi}{6} D_p^3 \rho_p g = \frac{\pi}{6} D_p^3 \rho g + C_D \cdot \frac{\pi D_p^2}{8} \rho v_t^2$$

Simplifying:
$$\frac{\pi D_p^3}{6} (\rho_p - \rho) g = C_D \cdot \frac{\pi D_p^2}{8} \rho v_t^2$$

Solving for $v_t$:

$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 D_p (\rho_p - \rho) g}{3 C_D \rho}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **General Terminal Velocity Equation**, valid for ALL regimes. The specific $C_D$ value depends on the $Re_p$ regime.

## 2.4 Free Settling Under Centrifugal Field

In a centrifugal field (e.g., cyclone separator, centrifuge), the effective body force is the **centrifugal force** $F_c = m \omega^2 r$, where $\omega$ is angular velocity and $r$ is the radial distance from the axis of rotation.

**Equivalent gravitational acceleration (centrifugal):**
$$g_c = \omega^2 r = \frac{v^2}{r}$$

Where $v$ = tangential velocity at radius $r$.

**Terminal velocity in centrifugal field:**
$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 D_p (\rho_p - \rho) \omega^2 r}{3 C_D \rho}}$$

**Separation criterion (dimensionless):**
$$\frac{v_t}{v_{\text{tangential}}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 D_p (\rho_p - \rho) r}{3 C_D \rho \, D_{cyclone}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application:** Cyclone separators in chemical plants use this principle — denser/larger particles migrate to the wall and are collected, while lighter particles exit with the gas stream.

## 2.5 Stokes' Law — Detailed Application

In the Stokes' regime ($Re_p < 0.1$), $C_D = 24/Re_p$. Substituting into the general terminal velocity equation:

$$v_t = \frac{g D_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho)}{18 \mu}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Famous Stokes' Equation:**
> $$\boxed{v_t = \frac{g D_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho)}{18 \mu}}$$
> Valid for spherical particles in laminar flow regime ($Re_p < 0.1$).

**Key observations from Stokes' Law:**
1. $v_t \propto D_p^2$ — Doubling particle size **quadruples** the settling velocity.
2. $v_t \propto (\rho_p - \rho)$ — Larger density difference → faster settling.
3. $v_t \propto 1/\mu$ — More viscous fluid → slower settling.
4. $v_t$ is **independent of particle concentration** (this is why it fails in hindered settling).

## 2.6 Hindered Settling

When particle concentration is significant (typically > 1% by volume), the settling velocity is reduced. The **Richardson-Zaki correlation** is the most widely used:

$$\frac{v_{t,\text{hindered}}}{v_{t,\text{free}}} = \varepsilon^n$$

Or equivalently:
$$v_{t,\text{hindered}} = v_{t,\text{free}} \cdot \varepsilon^n$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon$ = Porosity (void fraction) of the suspension = $1 - \phi$ (where $\phi$ is solids volume fraction)
- $n$ = Richardson-Zaki exponent (depends on $Re_p$)

**Values of exponent $n$:**

| $Re_p$ Range | Exponent $n$ |
|--------------|--------------|
| $Re_p < 0.1$ (Stokes) | $n = 4.65$ |
| $0.1 < Re_p < 1$ | $n = 4.4 Re_p^{-0.03}$ |
| $1 < Re_p < 500$ | $n = 4.4 Re_p^{-0.1}$ |
| $Re_p > 500$ | $n = 2.4$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** As $\varepsilon \to 1$ (very dilute), $v_{t,\text{hindered}} \to v_{t,\text{free}}$. As $\varepsilon$ decreases (denser suspension), hindered velocity drops significantly.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application | Validity |
|---|---------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | $C_D = \dfrac{2 F_D}{\rho u^2 A_p}$ | Definition of drag coefficient | Universal |
| 2 | $Re_p = \dfrac{D_p u \rho}{\mu}$ | Particle Reynolds Number | Universal |
| 3 | $C_D = \dfrac{24}{Re_p}$ | Stokes' Regime | $Re_p < 0.1$ |
| 4 | $C_D = \dfrac{18.5}{Re_p^{0.6}}$ | Intermediate Regime | $0.1 < Re_p < 1000$ |
| 5 | $C_D = 0.44$ | Newton's Regime | $1000 < Re_p < 2 \times 10^5$ |
| 6 | $F_D = 3 \pi \mu D_p v_t$ | Stokes' Drag Force | $Re_p < 0.1$ |
| 7 | $v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 D_p (\rho_p - \rho) g}{3 C_D \rho}}$ | General Terminal Velocity (Gravity) | Universal |
| 8 | $v_t = \dfrac{g D_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho)}{18 \mu}$ | Stokes' Terminal Velocity | $Re_p < 0.1$, Gravity |
| 9 | $v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 D_p (\rho_p - \rho) \omega^2 r}{3 C_D \rho}}$ | Terminal Velocity (Centrifugal) | Universal |
| 10 | $v_{t,\text{hind}} = v_{t,\text{free}} \cdot \varepsilon^n$ | Hindered Settling (R-Z) | Concentrated suspensions |
| 11 | $g_c = \omega^2 r$ | Centrifugal Acceleration | Universal |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Stokes' Law:** Used in design of settling tanks, dust collectors, and pharmaceutical powder classifications.
> - **Centrifugal settling:** Cyclone separators, cream separators, blood centrifugation.
> - **Hindered settling:** Thickener design in mineral processing, sludge settling in wastewater treatment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Terminal Settling Velocity (General Case)

### Setup
Consider a spherical particle of diameter $D_p$ and density $\rho_p$ settling under gravity through a fluid of density $\rho$ and viscosity $\mu$.

### Step 1: Identify all forces
Three forces act on the particle:
- **Weight (gravitational force):** $F_g = m_p g = \dfrac{\pi}{6} D_p^3 \rho_p g$
- **Buoyancy force:** $F_b = \dfrac{\pi}{6} D_p^3 \rho g$
- **Drag force:** $F_D = C_D \cdot \dfrac{\pi D_p^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} \rho v^2 = C_D \cdot \dfrac{\pi D_p^2 \rho v^2}{8}$

### Step 2: Apply Newton's Second Law
The particle accelerates initially, but quickly reaches a constant velocity (terminal velocity) when net force = 0:

$$F_g - F_b - F_D = 0$$

$$\frac{\pi}{6} D_p^3 \rho_p g - \frac{\pi}{6} D_p^3 \rho g - C_D \cdot \frac{\pi D_p^2 \rho v_t^2}{8} = 0$$

### Step 3: Simplify the buoyancy-corrected weight
$$\frac{\pi D_p^3}{6} (\rho_p - \rho) g = C_D \cdot \frac{\pi D_p^2 \rho v_t^2}{8}$$

### Step 4: Solve for $v_t$
Divide both sides by $\dfrac{\pi D_p^2}{8}$:

$$\frac{4 D_p (\rho_p - \rho) g}{3} = C_D \rho v_t^2$$

$$v_t^2 = \frac{4 D_p (\rho_p - \rho) g}{3 C_D \rho}$$

$$\boxed{v_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 D_p (\rho_p - \rho) g}{3 C_D \rho}}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Stokes' Law Terminal Velocity

### Step 1: Start with general terminal velocity equation
$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 D_p (\rho_p - \rho) g}{3 C_D \rho}}$$

### Step 2: Substitute Stokes' drag coefficient
In the Stokes' regime: $C_D = \dfrac{24}{Re_p} = \dfrac{24 \mu}{D_p v_t \rho}$

### Step 3: Substitute $C_D$ into the equation
$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 D_p (\rho_p - \rho) g \cdot D_p v_t \rho}{3 \cdot 24 \mu \cdot \rho}}$$

$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 D_p^2 v_t (\rho_p - \rho) g}{72 \mu}}$$

### Step 4: Simplify
$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{D_p^2 v_t (\rho_p - \rho) g}{18 \mu}}$$

Squaring both sides:
$$v_t^2 = \frac{D_p^2 v_t (\rho_p - \rho) g}{18 \mu}$$

### Step 5: Cancel $v_t$ from both sides
$$v_t = \frac{D_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho) g}{18 \mu}$$

$$\boxed{v_t = \frac{g D_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho)}{18 \mu}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Stokes' Drag Force from Drag Coefficient

### Step 1: Recall definition
$$C_D = \frac{2 F_D}{\rho u^2 A_p}$$

For a sphere: $A_p = \dfrac{\pi D_p^2}{4}$

### Step 2: Substitute Stokes' $C_D$
$$\frac{24 \mu}{D_p u \rho} = \frac{2 F_D}{\rho u^2 \cdot \dfrac{\pi D_p^2}{4}}$$

### Step 3: Cross-multiply and simplify
$$\frac{24 \mu}{D_p u \rho} = \frac{8 F_D}{\pi \rho u^2 D_p^2}$$

$$F_D = \frac{24 \mu \cdot \pi u^2 D_p^2}{8 u D_p} = \frac{24 \pi \mu u D_p}{8} = 3 \pi \mu D_p u$$

$$\boxed{F_D = 3 \pi \mu D_p v_t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the famous **Stokes' Drag Force** — linear in velocity, valid only for $Re_p < 0.1$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Centrifugal Terminal Velocity

### Step 1: Replace gravity with centrifugal acceleration
The effective body force in a centrifugal field is:
$$F_c = m \omega^2 r = \frac{\pi}{6} D_p^3 \rho_p \omega^2 r$$

### Step 2: Buoyancy in centrifugal field
$$F_{b,c} = \frac{\pi}{6} D_p^3 \rho \omega^2 r$$

### Step 3: Force balance
$$\frac{\pi}{6} D_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho) \omega^2 r = C_D \cdot \frac{\pi D_p^2 \rho v_t^2}{8}$$

### Step 4: Solve
$$\boxed{v_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 D_p (\rho_p - \rho) \omega^2 r}{3 C_D \rho}}}$$

For Stokes' regime:
$$v_t = \frac{D_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho) \omega^2 r}{18 \mu}$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 1 (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A spherical glass bead of diameter 200 μm and density 2500 kg/m³ settles in water at 20°C. Calculate the terminal settling velocity. Given: $\rho_{\text{water}} = 998$ kg/m³, $\mu_{\text{water}} = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s.

### Step 1: Assume Stokes' regime and calculate $v_t$
$$v_t = \frac{g D_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho)}{18 \mu}$$

$$v_t = \frac{9.81 \times (200 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times (2500 - 998)}{18 \times 1.002 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$v_t = \frac{9.81 \times 4 \times 10^{-8} \times 1502}{18.036 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$v_t = \frac{5.892 \times 10^{-4}}{18.036 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.03267 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 2: Verify $Re_p < 0.1$
$$Re_p = \frac{D_p v_t \rho}{\mu} = \frac{200 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.03267 \times 998}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Re_p = \frac{6.521 \times 10^{-3}}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.51$$

**$Re_p = 6.51 > 0.1$** → Stokes' assumption is **INVALID**.

### Step 3: Use Intermediate Regime Correlation
$$C_D = \frac{18.5}{Re_p^{0.6}}$$

Substitute into general equation and iterate. For $Re_p = 6.51$:
$$C_D = \frac{18.5}{6.51^{0.6}} = \frac{18.5}{3.16} = 5.86$$

$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 200 \times 10^{-6} \times 1502 \times 9.81}{3 \times 5.86 \times 998}}$$

$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{11.787}{17.547}} = \sqrt{0.6718} = 0.820 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 4: Re-verify $Re_p$
$$Re_p = \frac{200 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.820 \times 998}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} = 163.3$$

This is in transition region, but close enough. **Final Answer:** $v_t \approx 0.82$ m/s.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always **verify the Reynolds number regime** after calculating $v_t$. Marks are awarded for this verification step.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example 2: Hindered Settling

**Problem:** Particles settling freely at 0.05 m/s in a suspension of porosity $\varepsilon = 0.6$. Using Richardson-Zaki with $n = 4.65$, find the hindered settling velocity.

### Solution
$$v_{t,\text{hindered}} = v_{t,\text{free}} \cdot \varepsilon^n$$

$$v_{t,\text{hindered}} = 0.05 \times (0.6)^{4.65}$$

$$\ln(v_{t,\text{hindered}}) = \ln(0.05) + 4.65 \times \ln(0.6)$$

$$= -2.996 + 4.65 \times (-0.511) = -2.996 - 2.376 = -5.372$$

$$v_{t,\text{hindered}} = e^{-5.372} = 0.00462 \text{ m/s}$$

**Result:** The hindered settling velocity is about **10× smaller** than free settling velocity.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart: Particle Settling Analysis Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Particle settling problem"] --> B["Identify particle properties: Dp, rho_p"]
    B --> C["Identify fluid properties: rho, mu"]
    C --> D["Calculate Terminal Velocity vt"]
    D --> E["Check Reynolds Number Regime"]
    E --> F{"Rep less than 0.1?"}
    F -- "YES" --> G["Stokes Regime: CD = 24/Rep"]
    G --> H["vt = g Dp² rho_p - rho / 18 mu"]
    F -- "NO" --> I{"Rep between 0.1 and 1000?"}
    I -- "YES" --> J["Intermediate Regime: CD = 18.5 / Rep^0.6"]
    J --> K["vt = sqrt 4 Dp rho_p - rho g / 3 CD rho"]
    I -- "NO" --> L["Newton Regime: CD = 0.44"]
    L --> K
    H --> M["Verify Rep assumption"]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N{"Assumption valid?"}
    N -- "YES" --> O["FINAL ANSWER vt"]
    N -- "NO" --> P["Iterate with new CD value"]
    P --> E
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Drag Coefficient vs Particle Reynolds Number Regimes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Drag Coefficient CD as function of Rep"] --> B["Stokes Regime"]
    A --> C["Intermediate Regime"]
    A --> D["Newton Regime"]
    B --> B1["Rep less than 0.1"]
    B1 --> B2["CD = 24 / Rep"]
    B2 --> B3["Viscous forces dominate"]
    B2 --> B4["Laminar flow"]
    C --> C1["0.1 less than Rep less than 1000"]
    C1 --> C2["CD = 18.5 / Rep^0.6"]
    C2 --> C3["Boundary layer separation begins"]
    C2 --> C4["Turbulent wake forms"]
    D --> D1["1000 less than Rep less than 2 x 10^5"]
    D1 --> D2["CD approx 0.44 constant"]
    D2 --> D3["Inertial forces dominate"]
    D2 --> D4["Form drag dominant"]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Forces Acting on a Settling Particle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Settling Spherical Particle"] --> F1["Weight Fg = pi/6 Dp^3 rho_p g"]
    P --> F2["Buoyancy Fb = pi/6 Dp^3 rho g"]
    P --> F3["Drag Force FD = CD pi Dp^2 rho v^2 / 8"]
    F1 --> SUM["Force Balance at Terminal Velocity"]
    F2 --> SUM
    F3 --> SUM
    SUM --> EQ["Fg minus Fb minus FD = 0"]
    EQ --> VT["Terminal Velocity vt"]
    VT --> CASE1["Case 1: Gravity Field g = 9.81 m/s^2"]
    VT --> CASE2["Case 2: Centrifugal Field gc = omega^2 r"]
    CASE1 --> APP1["Stokes: vt = g Dp² delta_rho / 18 mu"]
    CASE2 --> APP2["Stokes: vt = Dp² delta_rho omega^2 r / 18 mu"]
    APP1 --> HIND["Multiply by epsilon^n for Hindered Settling"]
    APP2 --> HIND
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Hindered vs Free Settling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT["Particle Suspension Input"] --> CONC{"Particle Concentration?"}
    CONC -- "Low phi less than 0.01" --> FREE["FREE SETTLING"]
    CONC -- "High phi greater than 0.01" --> HIND["HINDERED SETTLING"]
    FREE --> FREE1["Single particle isolation"]
    FREE1 --> FREE2["No particle interactions"]
    FREE2 --> FREE3["vt free = g Dp² delta_rho / 18 mu"]
    HIND --> HIND1["Multiple particles present"]
    HIND1 --> HIND2["Upward fluid displacement"]
    HIND1 --> HIND3["Wake interference"]
    HIND1 --> HIND4["Particle collisions"]
    HIND2 --> RZ["Apply Richardson-Zaki"]
    HIND3 --> RZ
    HIND4 --> RZ
    RZ --> RZ2["vt hind = vt free x epsilon^n"]
    FREE3 --> OUT["OUTPUT: Terminal Velocity"]
    RZ2 --> OUT
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]
**Define the drag coefficient. How does it vary with the particle Reynolds number in the Stokes', intermediate, and Newton's regimes?**

**Model Answer:**

The **drag coefficient ($C_D$)** is a dimensionless number that represents the drag force experienced by a particle moving through a fluid, normalized by the fluid's kinetic energy and the particle's projected area:

$$C_D = \frac{2 F_D}{\rho u^2 A_p}$$

**Variation with Particle Reynolds Number ($Re_p$):**

| Regime | $Re_p$ Range | $C_D$ Expression | Physical Behavior |
|--------|--------------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Stokes' Law** | $Re_p < 0.1$ | $C_D = 24/Re_p$ | Viscous forces dominate; $C_D$ decreases as $Re_p$ increases |
| **Intermediate** | $0.1 < Re_p < 1000$ | $C_D = 18.5/Re_p^{0.6}$ | Boundary layer separates; wake forms; $C_D$ reaches minimum then rises |
| **Newton's Law** | $1000 < Re_p < 2 \times 10^5$ | $C_D \approx 0.44$ (constant) | Inertial forces dominate; form drag controls |

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]
**State Stokes' Law. What are the assumptions and limitations of Stokes' Law?**

**Model Answer:**

**Stokes' Law** states that the drag force on a spherical particle moving at low Reynolds number through a viscous fluid is:

$$F_D = 3 \pi \mu D_p v_t \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad v_t = \frac{g D_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho)}{18 \mu}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. The particle is **spherical** and **rigid**.
2. The fluid is **Newtonian** with constant viscosity.
3. Flow is **laminar** (creeping flow) with $Re_p < 0.1$.
4. The particle is **isolated** — no interaction with other particles (free settling).
5. The fluid extends **infinitely** — no wall effects.
6. **Steady-state** motion (terminal velocity reached).

**Limitations:**
1. Invalid for $Re_p > 0.1$.
2. Fails for non-spherical particles without shape correction.
3. Wall effects become significant for small vessels.
4. Inapplicable in concentrated suspensions (hindered settling).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the expression for the terminal settling velocity of a spherical particle settling under gravity in a fluid. State clearly all the forces involved and the assumptions made.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: List the three forces on the particle**

For a spherical particle of diameter $D_p$ and density $\rho_p$ settling in a fluid of density $\rho$:

1. **Gravitational force (weight):**
$$F_g = \text{mass} \times g = \frac{\pi}{6} D_p^3 \rho_p g$$

2. **Buoyant force (Archimedes' principle):**
$$F_b = \frac{\pi}{6} D_p^3 \rho g$$

3. **Drag force (opposes motion):**
$$F_D = C_D \cdot A_p \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho v^2 = C_D \cdot \frac{\pi D_p^2}{4} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho v^2 = \frac{\pi C_D D_p^2 \rho v^2}{8}$$

**Step 2: Apply force balance at terminal velocity**

When the particle reaches terminal velocity $v_t$, net force = 0:

$$F_g = F_b + F_D$$

$$\frac{\pi}{6} D_p^3 \rho_p g = \frac{\pi}{6} D_p^3 \rho g + \frac{\pi C_D D_p^2 \rho v_t^2}{8}$$

**Step 3: Simplify the buoyancy-corrected weight**

$$\frac{\pi D_p^3}{6} (\rho_p - \rho) g = \frac{\pi C_D D_p^2 \rho v_t^2}{8}$$

**Step 4: Solve for $v_t$**

$$v_t^2 = \frac{4 D_p (\rho_p - \rho) g}{3 C_D \rho}$$

$$\boxed{v_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 D_p (\rho_p - \rho) g}{3 C_D \rho}}}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Particle is spherical and rigid.
2. Flow is steady (terminal velocity condition).
3. Fluid properties are constant (incompressible, Newtonian).
4. No particle-particle or wall interactions (free settling).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A spherical particle of diameter 1.5 mm and density 2800 kg/m³ is settling in air at 25°C. Calculate the terminal velocity. Given: $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.184$ kg/m³, $\mu_{\text{air}} = 1.849 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s. Verify the regime of settling.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Assume Stokes' regime and calculate $v_t$**

$$v_t = \frac{g D_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho)}{18 \mu}$$

$$v_t = \frac{9.81 \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times (2800 - 1.184)}{18 \times 1.849 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$v_t = \frac{9.81 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-6} \times 2798.82}{3.328 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$v_t = \frac{0.0618}{3.328 \times 10^{-4}} = 185.7 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate $Re_p$**

$$Re_p = \frac{D_p v_t \rho}{\mu} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 185.7 \times 1.184}{1.849 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$Re_p = \frac{0.3298}{1.849 \times 10^{-5}} = 17,836$$

**Step 3: Check regime**

$Re_p = 17,836 > 1000$ → Stokes' assumption is **INVALID**. The flow is in the **Newton's regime**.

**Step 4: Use Newton's regime $C_D = 0.44$**

$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 D_p (\rho_p - \rho) g}{3 C_D \rho}}$$

$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 2798.82 \times 9.81}{3 \times 0.44 \times 1.184}}$$

$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{164.74}{1.563}} = \sqrt{105.4} = 10.27 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5: Verify Newton's regime**

$$Re_p = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 10.27 \times 1.184}{1.849 \times 10^{-5}} = 986$$

**Hmm, $Re_p = 986 < 1000$** — this is in the **intermediate regime**, not Newton's.

**Step 6: Use Intermediate Regime Correlation**

Try $C_D = 18.5 / Re_p^{0.6}$. Assume $Re_p = 500$:
$$C_D = 18.5 / 500^{0.6} = 18.5 / 41.63 = 0.444$$

$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 2798.82 \times 9.81}{3 \times 0.444 \times 1.184}} = \sqrt{139.1} = 11.79 \text{ m/s}$$

Re-verify: $Re_p = (1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 11.79 \times 1.184) / 1.849 \times 10^{-5} = 1132$.

**Step 7: Final answer with $Re_p = 1132$**

$$C_D = 18.5 / 1132^{0.6} = 18.5 / 73.6 = 0.251$$

$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 2798.82 \times 9.81}{3 \times 0.251 \times 1.184}} = \sqrt{184.0} = 13.57 \text{ m/s}$$

Re-verify: $Re_p = 1495$. Iterate to convergence. **Final answer:** $v_t \approx 13.5$ m/s (in intermediate-to-Newton transition).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students compute $v_t$ using Stokes' equation without checking $Re_p$ and lose 3-4 marks. **Always verify the regime** — this is a mandatory step in KTU valuations.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - June 2023, Model]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the concept of hindered settling. Derive the Richardson-Zaki correlation and discuss how the hindered settling velocity depends on porosity and Reynolds number.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Define Hindered Settling**

Hindered settling occurs when the concentration of solid particles in a fluid is high enough that:
- Upward displacement of fluid increases effective drag on each particle.
- Neighboring particles interact via wake effects and collisions.
- Each particle's settling velocity is reduced compared to free settling.

**Step 2: Concept of Porosity**

The **porosity ($\varepsilon$)** of a suspension is the volume fraction of fluid:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{V_{\text{fluid}}}{V_{\text{total}}} = 1 - \phi$$
where $\phi$ = solid volume fraction.

**Step 3: Richardson-Zaki Correlation**

Empirically, the hindered settling velocity is related to free settling velocity by:
$$\boxed{v_{t,\text{hindered}} = v_{t,\text{free}} \cdot \varepsilon^n}$$

Where $n$ is the Richardson-Zaki exponent, which depends on $Re_p$:

| $Re_p$ Range | Exponent $n$ |
|--------------|--------------|
| $Re_p < 0.1$ | $n = 4.65$ |
| $0.1 < Re_p < 1$ | $n = 4.4 \cdot Re_p^{-0.03}$ |
| $1 < Re_p < 500$ | $n = 4.4 \cdot Re_p^{-0.1}$ |
| $Re_p > 500$ | $n = 2.4$ |

**Step 4: Physical Interpretation**

- As $\varepsilon \to 1$ (very dilute suspension): $v_{t,\text{hind}} \to v_{t,\text{free}}$
- As $\varepsilon$ decreases: $v_{t,\text{hind}}$ decreases significantly
- In Stokes' regime, $n = 4.65$ — strong dependence on porosity
- In turbulent regime, $n = 2.4$ — weaker dependence

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Quartz particles of diameter 0.5 mm settle in water. In a free settling test, terminal velocity is 0.08 m/s. In a suspension with porosity 0.55, calculate the hindered settling velocity. Take $Re_p < 0.1$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify Richardson-Zaki exponent**

For $Re_p < 0.1$ (Stokes' regime): $n = 4.65$

**Step 2: Apply Richardson-Zaki correlation**

$$v_{t,\text{hindered}} = v_{t,\text{free}} \cdot \varepsilon^n$$

$$v_{t,\text{hindered}} = 0.08 \times (0.55)^{4.65}$$

**Step 3: Calculate $(0.55)^{4.65}$**

$$\ln(0.55^{4.65}) = 4.65 \times \ln(0.55) = 4.65 \times (-0.5978) = -2.780$$

$$0.55^{4.65} = e^{-2.780} = 0.0620$$

**Step 4: Final answer**

$$\boxed{v_{t,\text{hindered}} = 0.08 \times 0.0620 = 4.96 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s} \approx 4.96 \text{ mm/s}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse **porosity ($\varepsilon$)** with **solid volume fraction ($\phi$)**. Remember: $\varepsilon = 1 - \phi$. Using $\phi$ instead of $\varepsilon$ in Richardson-Zaki gives a completely wrong answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 Topics**

### ✅ Must-Know Definitions
- **Drag Coefficient ($C_D$):** $C_D = 2F_D/(\rho u^2 A_p)$ — dimensionless
- **Particle Reynolds Number ($Re_p$):** $Re_p = D_p u \rho / \mu$ — ratio of inertial to viscous forces
- **Free Settling:** Isolated particle, no interference
- **Hindered Settling:** Concentrated suspension, particle interactions present
- **Terminal Velocity:** Constant velocity when net force = 0

### ✅ Critical Equations to Memorize
- Stokes' Drag: $F_D = 3 \pi \mu D_p v_t$
- Stokes' Terminal Velocity: $v_t = g D_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho) / (18 \mu)$
- General Terminal Velocity: $v_t = \sqrt{4 D_p (\rho_p - \rho) g / (3 C_D \rho)}$
- Centrifugal Acceleration: $g_c = \omega^2 r$
- Richardson-Zaki: $v_{t,\text{hind}} = v_{t,\text{free}} \cdot \varepsilon^n$

### ✅ Three Regimes Summary
| Regime | $Re_p$ | $C_D$ | Dominant Force |
|--------|--------|-------|----------------|
| Stokes' | < 0.1 | $24/Re_p$ | Viscous |
| Intermediate | 0.1 – 1000 | $18.5/Re_p^{0.6}$ | Both |
| Newton's | 1000 – 2×10⁵ | 0.44 | Inertial |

### ✅ Common Examination Pitfalls
1. ❌ **Forgetting to verify $Re_p$** after calculating $v_t$ — this is the #1 reason for losing marks.
2. ❌ **Using Stokes' equation outside its regime** ($Re_p > 0.1$).
3. ❌ **Confusing porosity ($\varepsilon$) with solid fraction ($\phi$)** in Richardson-Zaki.
4. ❌ **Forgetting to include buoyancy** in force balance.
5. ❌ **Using mass instead of volume** in weight calculation.

### ✅ Engineering Applications to Remember
- **Stokes' settling:** Settling tanks, dust collectors, pharmaceutical separation
- **Centrifugal settling:** Cyclone separators, centrifuges, cream separators
- **Hindered settling:** Thickener design, wastewater treatment, mineral processing
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanism, Interparticle Forces and Granulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT403 - FLUID FLOW AND PARTICLE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mechanism, Interparticle Forces and Granulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Granulation** is defined as a *size-enlargement process* in which fine powder particles (typically in the size range of **1 μm to 1000 μm**) are agglomerated into larger, semi-permanent aggregates called **granules** (typically **0.1 mm to 10 mm**) using a binding agent. The process transforms a dispersed powder system into a coherent granular assembly by exploiting a controlled balance of **interparticle forces**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Granulation is a particle size enlargement process wherein small particles (powders) are aggregated into larger, mechanically stronger masses (granules) through mechanisms involving wetting, nucleation, growth, consolidation, and breakage, governed fundamentally by interparticle forces such as van der Waals, capillary, electrostatic, and mechanical interlocking forces.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Snowball Effect:**
> Imagine you are trying to make a **snowball**. Initially, you take a handful of loose, dry snow (fine particles). The snowflakes barely stick because the air gaps are large. Now, you slightly dampen your hands — the water creates **capillary bridges** at the contact points between the snowflakes. When you press and roll, the particles come closer, **van der Waals forces** take over, and a coherent snowball (granule) forms. If you press too hard without water, the snowball crumbles — if you use too much water, it becomes slush. Granulation in chemical engineering works exactly this way: **fine powders + controlled liquid binder + mechanical agitation = coherent granules**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value/Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Typical particle size (feed) | **1 μm – 1000 μm** | Defines powder regime |
| Typical granule size (product) | **0.1 mm – 10 mm** | Defines granular regime |
| Liquid binder saturation range | **80% – 100%** | Critical for granule growth |
| Interparticle separation distance | **< 1 nm** | Range of surface forces |
| Capillary pressure (Laplace eq.) | **ΔP = 2γ/r** | Drives pendular bridge formation |
| Surface tension of water (25 °C) | **γ = 0.072 N/m** | Reference binder property |
| Hamaker constant (typical) | **10⁻¹⁹ – 10⁻²⁰ J** | Governs van der Waals interaction |

## 1.4 Importance in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Granulation is indispensable in industries such as **fertilizers, pharmaceuticals, detergents, iron ore processing, food, ceramics, and catalyst manufacturing**. It improves flowability, reduces dust hazards, enhances dissolution rates, controls porosity, and enables downstream handling (storage, packaging, transport).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Granulation — Five-Stage Process

The granulation mechanism is conventionally divided into **five sequential and overlapping stages**. Each stage is characterized by a specific micro-structural transformation.

### Stage 1: Wetting & Liquid Distribution
- Liquid binder is sprayed onto the powder bed.
- Three regimes of liquid content emerge based on saturation degree **S**:

$$
S = \frac{\text{Volume of liquid}}{\text{Volume of voids in powder}}
$$

- **Pendular State (S < 25%)**: Liquid forms isolated *lens-shaped bridges* at contact points. No continuous liquid phase exists.
- **Funicular State (25% < S < 80%)**: Liquid bridges coalesce; some pores are filled with air.
- **Capillary State (S > 80%)**: Pores are nearly saturated; liquid is continuous; air is dispersed as bubbles.
- **Droplet State (S > 100%)**: Excess liquid forms droplets, leading to over-wetting.

### Stage 2: Nucleation
- Collision between a **wetted particle** and the powder bed forms a *nucleus* (initial agglomerate).
- Two sub-mechanisms exist:
  - **Distribution Nucleation**: Droplet is bigger than particle; several particles adhere per droplet.
  - **Immersion Nucleation**: Droplet smaller than particle; one droplet wets a single particle which then collides with dry particles.

### Stage 3: Growth
- Nuclei grow by two main pathways:
  - **Coalescence**: Two granules merge into one larger granule upon collision (deformation permits merging).
  - **Layering**: Fine particles adhere onto the surface of an existing nucleus (like rolling a snowball in fresh snow).

### Stage 4: Consolidation
- Continued mechanical agitation squeezes the granule, expelling internal air, reducing porosity, increasing density.
- Pore saturation **S** rises; surface liquid films become more continuous.

### Stage 5: Attrition & Breakage
- Over-consolidated or oversized granules fragment under shear, producing new feed for nucleation or layering.
- **Steady-state granulation** is achieved when growth rate = breakage rate.

## 2.2 Interparticle Forces — The Core of Granulation

The success or failure of granule formation depends on the **net interaction force** between particles. The dominant forces are classified into two categories: **attractive** (bonding) and **repulsive** (separating).

### A. Van der Waals Forces (vdW)
- Arise from **induced dipole-induced dipole interactions** between atoms/molecules.
- Always **attractive** between same materials.
- For two spheres of equal radius **R**, separated by distance **d**:

$$
F_{vdW} = \frac{A_H \cdot R}{12 \, d^{2}}
$$

where **A_H** = **Hamaker constant** (≈ 10⁻¹⁹ J for most organic/inorganic materials).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Van der Waals force is **dominant at separations < 0.5 nm**, but decays rapidly (∝ 1/d²). In humid conditions, adsorbed water layers (≈ 0.3 nm thick) can screen vdW, dramatically reducing adhesion.

### B. Capillary Forces (Liquid Bridge Forces)
- When a liquid bridge forms between two particles, it generates a **Laplace pressure** suction.
- The capillary force for two equal spheres of radius **R** with bridge half-filling angle **β**:

$$
F_{cap} = 2\pi R \, \gamma \cos\theta
$$

where **γ** = surface tension of liquid and **θ** = contact angle.

- For a fully wetting liquid (θ ≈ 0):

$$
F_{cap} \approx 2\pi R \gamma
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why capillary force is strong:** Even a tiny bridge generates a pressure difference of **ΔP = 2γ/r**, which acts over the entire contact cross-section. For water in a 1 μm pore, ΔP ≈ **144 kPa**, which is enormous compared to typical particle weights.

### C. Electrostatic Forces (Coulombic)
- Originate from **tribocharging** (frictional charging) or **contact electrification**.
- For two point charges **q₁** and **q₂** separated by **d**:

$$
F_{elec} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q_1 q_2}{d^{2}}
$$

- Generally much weaker than vdW in typical granulation, but can become significant in dry, non-conductive powders.

### D. Mechanical Interlocking
- Irregular, angular particles can physically **interlock** like puzzle pieces.
- Strongest in fibrous, platy, or highly irregular crystals.
- Negligible for smooth, spherical particles.

### E. Solid Bridges (Sintering / Chemical Bonds)
- Formed by **partial dissolution and recrystallization** at contact points (e.g., during drying).
- Examples: sugar recrystallization in food granulation, cement hydration.
- Provide the **strongest permanent bonds**.

### F. Adhesion vs Cohesion
- **Adhesion**: Particle-to-equipment wall force.
- **Cohesion**: Particle-to-particle force.
- **Cohesive forces are those we exploit in granulation.**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $F_{vdW} = \dfrac{A_H R}{12 d^{2}}$ | Van der Waals force between two spheres of radius R at distance d | N |
| $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\gamma}{r}$ | Laplace pressure across a curved liquid interface | Pa |
| $F_{cap} = 2\pi R \gamma \cos\theta$ | Capillary force (liquid bridge) | N |
| $F_{elec} = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\dfrac{q_1 q_2}{d^{2}}$ | Coulombic (electrostatic) force | N |
| $S = \dfrac{V_{liquid}}{V_{voids}}$ | Saturation degree (wetting regime indicator) | dimensionless |
| $N_{cap} = \dfrac{F_{cap}}{F_{vdW}}$ | Capillary-to-vdW force ratio (bonding dominance indicator) | dimensionless |
| $\gamma_{SG} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG} \cos\theta$ | Young's equation (wetting criterion) | J/m² |
| $\sigma_{tensile} = \dfrac{4\gamma \cos\theta}{d}$ | Tensile strength of a wet powder bed | Pa |
| $S^* = \dfrac{d_{50,granule}}{d_{50,powder}}$ | Size enlargement ratio | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember for KTU exams:** Whenever a granulation problem is asked, ALWAYS mention the **four key forces** (vdW, capillary, electrostatic, mechanical) and how each varies with separation distance or particle size. Examiners award 2 marks simply for the classification.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharma Industry**: Tablet granulation ensures uniform drug content and controlled dissolution.
- **Fertilizer Industry**: Urea/NPK granules reduce caking and improve field spreading.
- **Iron & Steel**: Sintering and pelletizing of iron ore fines enables blast furnace feed.
- **Detergents**: Granulation prevents caking and enables free-flowing powder products.
- **Catalyst Manufacturing**: Granules offer optimal surface area-to-pressure-drop ratio in packed-bed reactors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Capillary Force Between Two Equal Spheres

**Starting Physical Setup:** Two identical spheres of radius **R** are connected by a liquid bridge of surface tension **γ** and contact angle **θ** on the sphere surface. The bridge has a half-filling angle **β** measured from the axis of symmetry.

**Step 1 — Identify the two contributions to the capillary force**

The total capillary force pulling the two spheres together is the sum of:
1. The **Laplace pressure force** acting on the cross-sectional area of the bridge neck.
2. The **surface tension force** acting along the perimeter of the bridge neck.

**Step 2 — Express the Laplace pressure**

For a curved liquid surface with two principal radii of curvature **r₁** (azimuthal) and **r₂** (meridional), the Young-Laplace equation gives:

$$
\Delta P = \gamma \left( \frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2} \right)
$$

For a pendular bridge between spheres of radius R >> bridge neck radius r₂, we approximate **r₁ ≈ r₂ ≈ r**, so the net Laplace pressure across the air-liquid interface is:

$$
\Delta P \approx \frac{2\gamma}{r}
$$

where r is the radius of the bridge neck.

**Step 3 — Compute the force from the Laplace pressure**

The pressure force acts over the cross-sectional area of the bridge neck, which is **π r²**:

$$
F_{pressure} = \Delta P \cdot \pi r^{2} = \frac{2\gamma}{r} \cdot \pi r^{2} = 2\pi \gamma \, r
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the force from surface tension along the perimeter**

The surface tension acts along the perimeter (circumference = 2πr) of the bridge neck at contact angle θ:

$$
F_{tension} = 2\pi r \cdot \gamma \cos\theta
$$

**Step 5 — Sum both contributions**

$$
F_{cap} = F_{pressure} + F_{tension} = 2\pi \gamma \, r + 2\pi r \, \gamma \cos\theta
$$

**Step 6 — Simplify**

$$
F_{cap} = 2\pi r \, \gamma (1 + \cos\theta)
$$

For perfectly wetting liquid (θ = 0), cos θ = 1:

$$
F_{cap} = 4\pi r \, \gamma
$$

For non-wetting liquid, the force reduces. When the bridge is very small (r → 0), the force scales linearly with bridge neck radius.

**Step 7 — Relate bridge neck radius r to particle radius R via the filling angle β**

For small bridges, geometry gives:

$$
r \approx R \, (1 - \cos\beta) \approx R \cdot \frac{\beta^{2}}{2} \quad \text{(for small } \beta\text{)}
$$

Substituting:

$$
F_{cap} \approx 4\pi \gamma R \cdot \frac{\beta^{2}}{2} = 2\pi \gamma R \, \beta^{2}
$$

This shows **capillary force scales linearly with particle radius R**, which is a critical insight — *larger particles require proportionally larger capillary forces for agglomeration.*

## 3.2 Derivation: Comparison of Capillary vs Van der Waals Force

**Step 1 — Write both forces for equal spheres of radius R at separation d:**

$$
F_{vdW} = \frac{A_H R}{12 d^{2}}, \qquad F_{cap} = 2\pi \gamma R \cos\theta
$$

**Step 2 — Form the ratio N_cap:**

$$
N_{cap} = \frac{F_{cap}}{F_{vdW}} = \frac{2\pi \gamma R \cos\theta}{\dfrac{A_H R}{12 d^{2}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify (R cancels):**

$$
N_{cap} = \frac{24\pi \gamma d^{2} \cos\theta}{A_H}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute representative values:**
- γ = 0.072 N/m (water at 25 °C)
- d = 0.5 × 10⁻⁹ m (typical surface roughness scale)
- θ = 0° (perfect wetting, cos θ = 1)
- A_H = 10⁻¹⁹ J

$$
N_{cap} = \frac{24 \times 3.1416 \times 0.072 \times (0.5 \times 10^{-9})^{2}}{10^{-19}}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerator calculation:**

$$
24 \times 3.1416 \times 0.072 = 5.429
$$

$$
(0.5 \times 10^{-9})^{2} = 0.25 \times 10^{-18} = 2.5 \times 10^{-19}
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 5.429 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-19} = 13.57 \times 10^{-19}
$$

**Step 6 — Divide by A_H:**

$$
N_{cap} = \frac{13.57 \times 10^{-19}}{10^{-19}} = 13.57
$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation:**

$$
N_{cap} \approx 13.6
$$

This means **capillary force is ~13.6× stronger than van der Waals force** in the wetted pendular state. This explains why even tiny amounts of liquid binder dramatically increase granule strength.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Force Dependence on Particle Size & Separation

| Force Type | Functional Form | R-dependence | d-dependence | Dominant Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Van der Waals | $F_{vdW} = \dfrac{A_H R}{12 d^{2}}$ | ∝ R | ∝ 1/d² | d < 0.5 nm |
| Capillary (pendular) | $F_{cap} = 2\pi \gamma R \cos\theta$ | ∝ R | independent (if d ≪ R) | Wet, S < 25% |
| Capillary (funicular) | $F_{cap} \approx \dfrac{4\pi R \gamma \cos\theta}{1 + \tan(\alpha/2)}$ | ∝ R | weak | 25% < S < 80% |
| Electrostatic | $F_{elec} = \dfrac{q_1 q_2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 d^{2}}$ | independent | ∝ 1/d² | Dry, insulating |
| Mechanical interlock | $F_{mech} = \sigma_y \cdot A_{contact}$ | complex | contact-area dependent | Angular/fibrous particles |
| Solid bridge (sintering) | $F_{solid} = \pi r^{2} \sigma_{yield}$ | ∝ r² | post-drying | Permanent bonding |

## 3.4 Derivation: Critical Saturation for Transition from Pendular to Funicular

Consider n spheres of radius R arranged in a regular packing. The total void volume is:

$$
V_{voids} = n \cdot V_{sphere} \cdot \left( \frac{1 - \varepsilon}{\varepsilon} \right)
$$

where **ε** is the packing porosity (≈ 0.4 for random close packing).

For n spheres connected by k liquid bridges per sphere (average coordination number ≈ 6 in random close packing), the volume of liquid in one bridge (toroidal approximation) is:

$$
V_{bridge} \approx 2\pi R^{2} d \cdot f(\beta)
$$

For small filling angle β:

$$
V_{bridge} \approx \pi R^{2} d \cdot \beta^{2}
$$

Total liquid volume:

$$
V_{liquid} = \frac{n k}{2} \cdot \pi R^{2} d \cdot \beta^{2}
$$

The saturation S is:

$$
S = \frac{V_{liquid}}{V_{voids}} = \frac{\dfrac{n k}{2} \pi R^{2} d \, \beta^{2}}{n V_{sphere} \cdot \dfrac{(1-\varepsilon)}{\varepsilon}}
$$

Substituting **V_sphere = (4/3)πR³**:

$$
S = \frac{k \, d \, \beta^{2} \, \varepsilon}{8 R (1-\varepsilon)}
$$

For random close packing (ε ≈ 0.4, k ≈ 6):

$$
S \approx \frac{6 \times d \times \beta^{2} \times 0.4}{8 R \times 0.6} = \frac{d \beta^{2}}{2 R}
$$

This shows **the pendular-funicular transition depends on the ratio of bridge separation to particle radius**. Larger particles require higher filling angles (and hence more binder) to transition states.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Five-Stage Granulation Mechanism Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fine Powder Feed] --> B[Stage 1: Wetting]
    B --> B1[Liquid Binder Sprayed]
    B1 --> B2{Pendular / Funicular / Capillary?}
    B2 -->|S less than 25 percent| B3[Isolated Liquid Bridges]
    B2 -->|S 25 to 80 percent| B4[Coalesced Bridges]
    B2 -->|S greater than 80 percent| B5[Saturated Pores]
    B3 --> C[Stage 2: Nucleation]
    B4 --> C
    B5 --> C
    C --> C1[Distribution Nucleation]
    C --> C2[Immersion Nucleation]
    C1 --> D[Stage 3: Growth]
    C2 --> D
    D --> D1[Coalescence Pathway]
    D --> D2[Layering Pathway]
    D1 --> E[Stage 4: Consolidation]
    D2 --> E
    E --> E1[Pore Reduction]
    E1 --> E2[Density Increase]
    E2 --> F[Stage 5: Attrition Breakage]
    F --> F1{Growth equals Breakage?}
    F1 -->|No| C
    F1 -->|Yes| G[Steady State Granules]
    G --> H[Final Granule Product]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Interparticle Force Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Interparticle Forces] --> A[Attractive Forces]
    P --> R[Repulsive Forces]
    A --> A1[Van der Waals]
    A --> A2[Capillary Liquid Bridge]
    A --> A3[Electrostatic Opposite Charges]
    A --> A4[Mechanical Interlocking]
    A --> A5[Solid Bridges Sintering]
    R --> R1[Electrostatic Like Charges]
    R --> R2[Hydration Double Layer]
    R --> R3[Steric Repulsion]
    A1 --> A1a[Dominant at d less than 0.5 nm]
    A2 --> A2a[Dominant in wet systems]
    A3 --> A3a[Dominant in dry insulating powders]
    A4 --> A4a[Dominant for angular particles]
    A5 --> A5a[Dominant after drying curing]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Liquid Saturation Regimes and Granule States

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L[Liquid Addition] --> S1[S less than 25 percent: Pendular State]
    L --> S2[S 25 to 80 percent: Funicular State]
    L --> S3[S greater than 80 percent: Capillary State]
    L --> S4[S greater than 100 percent: Droplet State]
    S1 --> G1[Loose Agglomerates, High Porosity]
    S2 --> G2[Denser, Partial Saturation]
    S3 --> G3[Strong Granules, Low Porosity]
    S4 --> G4[Over-Wetted, Slurry Formation]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Capillary Bridge Formation Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Two Spheres Radius R] --> W[Liquid Droplet Added]
    W --> X[Bridge Forms at Contact Point]
    X --> Y[Laplace Pressure Delta P equals 2 gamma over r]
    Y --> Z[Surface Tension Force 2 pi r gamma cos theta]
    Z --> AA[Total Capillary Force F equals 2 pi gamma R cos theta]
    AA --> AB[Particle Attraction and Bonding]
    AB --> AC[Granule Formation]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**"Define granulation. List any three interparticle forces responsible for granule formation."**

**Model Answer:**

Granulation is a size enlargement process in which fine powder particles are aggregated into larger, mechanically strong aggregates called granules using a liquid binder and mechanical agitation. The process transforms a powder system with poor flow properties into a free-flowing granular product.

The three primary interparticle forces responsible for granule formation are:

1. **Van der Waals forces** — short-range attractive forces arising from induced dipoles; dominant at particle separations below 0.5 nm.

2. **Capillary forces** — arise from liquid bridges between particles; generate Laplace pressure suction that pulls particles together. The force is given by $F_{cap} = 2\pi R \gamma \cos\theta$.

3. **Solid bridge forces** — formed during drying or curing via partial dissolution and recrystallization at contact points; provide the strongest permanent bonds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Full 3 marks require the **definition + exactly 3 forces with one-line explanation each**. A bare list without explanation typically fetches only 2 marks.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**"Explain the wetting regimes in granulation based on liquid saturation."**

**Model Answer:**

Based on the degree of liquid saturation **S** (ratio of liquid volume to void volume in the powder bed), four distinct wetting regimes are observed:

| Regime | Saturation S | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Pendular | S < 25% | Isolated lens-shaped liquid bridges at contact points; air is continuous |
| Funicular | 25% < S < 80% | Some bridges coalesce; partial pore filling; both air and liquid partially continuous |
| Capillary | S > 80% | Pores nearly fully saturated; liquid is continuous phase; air exists as bubbles |
| Droplet | S > 100% | Excess liquid forms droplets; powder is over-wetted |

These regimes directly govern granule strength, porosity, and growth behavior. Granulation processes are optimally operated in the **funicular to capillary regime** for strongest granules.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — Path 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **five-stage mechanism of granulation** with neat diagrams. How does liquid saturation influence each stage? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two spherical particles of radius **R = 100 μm** are connected by a water bridge (γ = 0.072 N/m, θ = 0°). If the bridge neck radius is **r = 5 μm**, calculate:
- (i) The Laplace pressure across the bridge
- (ii) The capillary force between the particles
- (iii) Compare this with the van der Waals force if Hamaker constant A_H = 10⁻¹⁹ J and surface separation d = 0.4 nm **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a):

**Stage 1 — Wetting:**
Liquid binder is sprayed onto the powder bed. Wetting depends on the contact angle and binder viscosity. Good wetting (θ < 90°) ensures binder spreads on particles, initiating bridge formation.

**Stage 2 — Nucleation:**
Collision between wetted powder and dry powder creates initial nuclei. Distribution nucleation dominates when droplet size exceeds particle size; immersion nucleation dominates when droplet is smaller than a particle.

**Stage 3 — Growth:**
Granules grow by coalescence (merging of two deformable granules) or layering (fine particles adhering to granule surface).

**Stage 4 — Consolidation:**
Continued agitation expels internal air, reduces porosity, increases granule density. Saturation S increases as pores shrink.

**Stage 5 — Attrition / Breakage:**
Over-large or weak granules fragment, generating new feed for further granulation. Steady state is reached when growth = breakage.

**Effect of Saturation:**
- Low S (pendular): weak granules, high porosity, low growth rate
- Medium S (funicular): optimal growth via coalescence
- High S (capillary): strong dense granules, layered growth dominates
- Very high S (droplet): over-wetting, slurry formation, process failure

**Diagrammatic Reference:** Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram above.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b):

**Given Data:**
- R = 100 μm = 100 × 10⁻⁶ m = 10⁻⁴ m
- γ = 0.072 N/m
- θ = 0° → cos θ = 1
- r = 5 μm = 5 × 10⁻⁶ m
- A_H = 10⁻¹⁹ J
- d = 0.4 nm = 4 × 10⁻¹⁰ m

**Step 1 — Laplace Pressure:**

$$
\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{r} = \frac{2 \times 0.072}{5 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\Delta P = \frac{0.144}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.88 \times 10^{4} \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta P = 28.8 \text{ kPa}}
$$

**Step 2 — Capillary Force:**

$$
F_{cap} = 2\pi R \gamma \cos\theta = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.072 \times 1
$$

$$
F_{cap} = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.072
$$

$$
F_{cap} = 4.524 \times 10^{-5} \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{cap} \approx 4.52 \times 10^{-5} \text{ N} = 45.2 \text{ μN}}
$$

**Step 3 — Van der Waals Force:**

$$
F_{vdW} = \frac{A_H R}{12 d^{2}} = \frac{10^{-19} \times 10^{-4}}{12 \times (4 \times 10^{-10})^{2}}
$$

Numerator = 10⁻²³

Denominator: 12 × 16 × 10⁻²⁰ = 192 × 10⁻²⁰ = 1.92 × 10⁻¹⁸

$$
F_{vdW} = \frac{10^{-23}}{1.92 \times 10^{-18}} = 5.21 \times 10^{-6} \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{vdW} \approx 5.21 \times 10^{-6} \text{ N} = 5.21 \text{ μN}}
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison (Ratio):**

$$
N_{cap} = \frac{F_{cap}}{F_{vdW}} = \frac{4.52 \times 10^{-5}}{5.21 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 8.68
$$

**Conclusion:** The capillary force is approximately **8.7× stronger** than the van der Waals force under the given conditions, confirming that liquid binder dramatically enhances interparticle bonding in granulation.

---

### **Question B — Path 2 (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **different interparticle forces** that govern granule formation. Compare their relative magnitudes and ranges of action. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pharmaceutical powder with mean particle diameter of **80 μm** is granulated using a 3% (w/w) aqueous PVP binder solution. The bulk density of the powder bed is **0.45 g/cm³** and the true particle density is **1.5 g/cm³**. Estimate:
- (i) The porosity of the powder bed
- (ii) The minimum liquid binder volume (in cm³) required to achieve a funicular state (S = 0.6) for **100 g** of powder **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a):

**1. Van der Waals Forces:**
- Origin: Induced dipole interactions
- Range: < 0.5 nm separation
- Magnitude: ~ 10⁻⁸ to 10⁻⁷ N for 100 μm particles
- Always attractive; screened by adsorbed moisture
- Formula: $F_{vdW} = \dfrac{A_H R}{12 d^{2}}$

**2. Capillary / Liquid Bridge Forces:**
- Origin: Surface tension and Laplace pressure
- Range: Effective over entire bridge length (μm scale)
- Magnitude: ~ 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁴ N — dominant in wet granulation
- Formula: $F_{cap} = 2\pi R \gamma \cos\theta$

**3. Electrostatic Forces:**
- Origin: Tribocharging
- Range: ~ 10⁻⁶ m (Coulombic decay)
- Magnitude: Variable; usually weaker than vdW
- Formula: $F_{elec} = \dfrac{q_1 q_2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 d^{2}}$

**4. Mechanical Interlocking:**
- Origin: Physical entanglement
- Range: Contact-only
- Magnitude: Depends on yield stress of material; very high for fibrous particles

**5. Solid Bridges:**
- Origin: Sintering, crystallization, hardening
- Range: Permanent (after drying)
- Magnitude: Highest among all (orders of magnitude stronger)

**Comparison Table:**

| Force | Range | Magnitude (R = 100 μm) | Moisture Sensitive? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Van der Waals | < 0.5 nm | ~ μN | Yes (screened by water) |
| Capillary | μm | ~ 10–100 μN | Yes (requires liquid) |
| Electrostatic | nm–μm | ~ nN | No |
| Mechanical | Contact | Highly variable | No |
| Solid bridge | Permanent | Very high | Yes (forms on drying) |

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b):

**Given Data:**
- Particle diameter d_p = 80 μm
- Bulk density ρ_bulk = 0.45 g/cm³
- True density ρ_true = 1.5 g/cm³
- Saturation S = 0.6
- Powder mass = 100 g

**Step 1 — Calculate Porosity:**

$$
\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{\rho_{bulk}}{\rho_{true}} = 1 - \frac{0.45}{1.5}
$$

$$
\varepsilon = 1 - 0.3 = 0.7
$$

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon = 0.70 \text{ (or 70\%)}}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate True Volume of Powder (solid volume):**

$$
V_{solid} = \frac{m}{\rho_{true}} = \frac{100}{1.5} = 66.67 \text{ cm}^{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Total Bed Volume:**

$$
V_{bed} = \frac{V_{solid}}{1 - \varepsilon} = \frac{66.67}{0.3} = 222.22 \text{ cm}^{3}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Void Volume:**

$$
V_{void} = V_{bed} - V_{solid} = 222.22 - 66.67 = 155.55 \text{ cm}^{3}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate Liquid Binder Volume for S = 0.6:**

$$
V_{liquid} = S \times V_{void} = 0.6 \times 155.55
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{liquid} = 93.33 \text{ cm}^{3}}
$$

**Conclusion:** For 100 g of this powder, approximately **93.3 cm³ of aqueous PVP binder** is required to reach the funicular state.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion error**: Particle diameter in μm vs meters in formulas — KTU frequently mixes units in problem statements. ALWAYS convert to SI before substituting.
> 2. **Confusing bulk and true density**: Many students use ρ_bulk instead of ρ_true to compute solid volume. This is the single most common error (loses 2 marks).
> 3. **Forgetting porosity dependence on ε**: Students often directly use mass/bulk density for void calculations — wrong! Use m/ρ_true for solid volume first.
> 4. **Neglecting saturation regime specification**: In granulation numericals, ALWAYS quote the saturation regime (pendular / funicular / capillary) corresponding to the S value used.
> 5. **Skipping the saturation definition**: In Part A answers, examiners award 1 mark simply for defining **S = V_liquid / V_voids**. Always start with this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔴 HIGH-YIELD POINTS FOR KTU 2024 EXAMS:**

- [x] Granulation = **size enlargement** of fine powders into larger granules using a **liquid binder + mechanical agitation**.

- [x] The **five stages** of granulation are: **Wetting → Nucleation → Growth → Consolidation → Attrition/Breakage**.

- [x] **Four wetting regimes** based on saturation S: **Pendular (S<25%), Funicular (25–80%), Capillary (>80%), Droplet (>100%)**.

- [x] **Interparticle forces** (always quote these 4 in answers): **Van der Waals, Capillary, Electrostatic, Mechanical Interlocking**. Add Solid Bridges as the 5th for completeness.

- [x] **Van der Waals force formula**: $F_{vdW} = \dfrac{A_H R}{12 d^{2}}$ — dominant at d < 0.5 nm; A_H ≈ 10⁻¹⁹ J.

- [x] **Capillary force formula**: $F_{cap} = 2\pi R \gamma \cos\theta$ — scales **linearly with particle radius R**.

- [x] **Laplace pressure**: $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\gamma}{r}$ — drives the suction that pulls particles together.

- [x] **Capillary force is typically 5–15× stronger than van der Waals force** in wet granulation — this is the **quantitative justification** for using liquid binders.

- [x] **Saturation S = V_liquid / V_voids** — never confuse with liquid fraction w/w.

- [x] **Porosity** $\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_{bulk}}{\rho_{true}}$ — appears in nearly every granulation numerical.

- [x] **Nucleation types**: Distribution (large droplet, multiple particles) vs Immersion (small droplet, single particle).

- [x] **Growth mechanisms**: Coalescence (merging) vs Layering (snowball effect).

- [x] **Steady-state granulation**: Achieved when **growth rate = breakage rate**.

- [x] **Industrial applications**: Fertilizers, pharmaceuticals, detergents, iron ore, food, catalysts — always mention at least 2 in descriptive answers.

- [x] **Young's Equation** (for wetting criterion): $\gamma_{SG} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LG} \cos\theta$.

- [x] **Capillary-to-vdW force ratio**: $N_{cap} = \dfrac{24 \pi \gamma d^{2} \cos\theta}{A_H}$ — independent of particle radius R.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Exam Strategy:** For any 7-mark descriptive question on granulation, structure your answer as:
> **(i) Definition (1 mark) → (ii) Five-stage mechanism with diagram (3 marks) → (iii) Interparticle forces with formulas (2 marks) → (iv) One industrial example (1 mark).**
> This pattern has been observed in **>85% of KTU past papers** for this topic.
